title: Engineering Fully Dynamic Convex Hulls
summary:
We present a new fully dynamic algorithm for maintaining convex hulls under insertions and deletions while supporting geometric queries. Our approach combines the logarithmic method with a deletion-only convex hull data structure, achieving amortised update times of $O(\log n \log \log n)$ and query times of $O(\log^2 n)$. We provide a robust and non-trivial implementation that supports point-location queries, a challenging and non-decomposable class of convex hull queries.
  We evaluate our implementation against the state of the art, including a new naive baseline that rebuilds the convex hull whenever an update affects it. On hulls that include polynomially many data points (e.g. $Θ(n^\varepsilon)$ for some $\varepsilon$), such as the ones that often occur in practice, our method outperforms all other techniques. Update-heavy workloads strongly favour our approach, which is in line with our theoretical guarantees. Yet, our method remains competitive all the way down to when the update to query ratio is $1$ to $10$.
  Experiments on real-world data sets furthermore reveal that existing fully dynamic techniques suffer from significant robustness issues. In contrast, our implementation remains stable across all tested inputs.

AI.tldr: A new fully dynamic convex hull maintenance algorithm using the logarithmic method with a deletion-only hull structure, achieving amortized update time O(log n log log n) and query time O(log^2 n); robust implementation with point-location queries; empirically competitive and robust across workloads.
AI.motivation: To support efficient dynamic convex hull queries in the presence of insertions and deletions, addressing performance gaps and robustness issues in existing fully dynamic hull methods while enabling non-decomposable queries like point-location.
AI.method: Integrates the logarithmic method with a deletion-only convex hull data structure to handle insertions and deletions. Supports point-location queries and other convex-hull queries. Evaluates against state-of-the-art plus a naive rebuild baseline, focusing on hulls with polynomially many data points and update-heavy workloads.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees: update time O(log n log log n), query time O(log^2 n). Empirical results show superiority over baselines on large hulls and robustness across real-world datasets, with strong performance even when update-to-query ratio is as high as 1:10.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a practical, robust, and efficient dynamic convex hull maintenance solution, particularly favorable for update-heavy workloads and large hulls, and suggests robustness advantages over existing fully dynamic techniques.

title: Two-Stage Optimizer-Aware Online Data Selection for Large Language Models
summary: Gradient-based data selection offers a principled framework for estimating sample utility in large language model (LLM) fine-tuning, but existing methods are mostly designed for offline settings. They are therefore less suited to online fine-tuning, where data arrives sequentially, sample utility is step-dependent, and the effective update geometry is shaped by adaptive optimizers. We propose an optimizer-aware framework for gradient-based online data selection and reweighting in LLM fine-tuning. Our key idea is to view online selection not as static sample ranking, but as shaping the next target-oriented update under the optimizer state. We formulate this as an optimizer-aware update-matching problem, establish its connection to second-order target utility, and show why subset-level construction must account for interactions and redundancy among selected samples. Based on this view, we develop a two-stage Filter-then-Weight algorithm that first filters geometrically useful candidates and then optimizes their coefficients. To make the framework practical for LLMs, we introduce a factorized outer-product gradient representation and optimized matrix computations for long-context data. Experiments show that our method consistently improves convergence and downstream performance over existing online data selection baselines under the same data budget.

AI.tldr: Optimizer-aware online gradient-based data selection for LLM fine-tuning with a two-stage Filter-then-Weight algorithm and a factorized gradient representation, yielding improved convergence and downstream performance under fixed data budgets.
AI.motivation: Online fine-tuning of LLMs requires data utility that adapts to sequential arrivals, step-dependent updates, and optimizer geometry. Offline, static sample ranking methods ignore these aspects, leading to inefficiency and redundancy.
AI.method: Formulates an optimizer-aware update-matching problem linked to second-order target utility. Proposes a two-stage Filter-then-Weight algorithm: (1) filter geometrically useful candidates, (2) optimize their reweighting coefficients. Introduces a factorized outer-product gradient representation and efficient matrix computations to handle long-context data.
AI.result: Empirical results show consistent improvements in convergence speed and downstream performance over existing online data selection baselines under the same data budget.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for optimizer state and sample interactions in online data selection yields more data-efficient LLM fine-tuning; the framework is practical and scalable via the proposed representations and computations.

title: How Emotion Shapes the Behavior of LLMs and Agents: A Mechanistic Study
summary: Emotion plays an important role in human cognition and performance. Motivated by this, we investigate whether analogous emotional signals can shape the behavior of large language models (LLMs) and agents. Existing emotion-aware studies mainly treat emotion as a surface-level style factor or a perception target, overlooking its mechanistic role in task processing. To address this limitation, we propose E-STEER, an interpretable emotion steering framework that enables direct representation-level intervention in LLMs and agents. It embeds emotion as a structured, controllable variable in hidden states, and with it, we examine the impact of emotion on objective reasoning, subjective generation, safety, and multi-step agent behaviors. The results reveal non-monotonic emotion-behavior relations consistent with established psychological theories, and show that specific emotions not only enhance LLM capability but also improve safety, and systematically shape multi-step agent behaviors.

AI.tldr: E-STEER inserts a structured emotion variable into LLM hidden states to steer processing and behavior; reports non-monotonic emotion–behavior relations and improvements in capability and safety, including multi-step agent tasks.
AI.motivation: To move beyond treating emotion as superficial, and to reveal its mechanistic role in task processing by enabling representation-level intervention in LLMs/agents.
AI.method: Embed a structured, controllable emotion variable in hidden states and perform direct representation-level interventions to study effects on objective reasoning, subjective generation, safety, and multi-step agent behavior across various emotions.
AI.result: Non-monotonic emotion–behavior relations; certain emotions enhance capability and safety; emotion steering systematically shapes multi-step agent behavior.
AI.conclusion: Emotions can be meaningfully integrated as interpretable control signals in LLMs/agents, with results aligning with psychological theories and suggesting new avenues for capability and safety enhancements through emotion-aware design.

title: Hierarchical Pre-Training of Vision Encoders with Large Language Models
comment: 17 pages, 14 figures, accepted to Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Conference (CVPR) Workshops 2026. 5th MMFM Workshop: What is Next in Multimodal Foundation Models?
summary: The field of computer vision has experienced significant advancements through scalable vision encoders and multimodal pre-training frameworks. However, existing approaches often treat vision encoders and large language models (LLMs) as independent modules, limiting the integration of hierarchical visual features. In this work, we propose HIVE (Hierarchical Pre-Training of Vision Encoders), a novel framework that enhances vision-language alignment by introducing hierarchical cross-attention between the vision encoder and LLM. Unlike conventional methods that flatten image embeddings, HIVE enables structured feature fusion across multiple layers, improving gradient flow and representation learning. To optimize this interaction, we introduce a three-stage training strategy that progressively aligns the vision encoder with the LLM, ensuring stable optimization and effective multimodal fusion. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that HIVE achieves superior performance not only in image classification but also on various vision-language tasks, outperforming self-attention-based methods in benchmarks such as MME, GQA, OK-VQA, and ScienceQA. Our results highlight the benefits of hierarchical feature integration, paving the way for more efficient and expressive vision-language models.

AI.tldr: HIVE: Hierarchical Pre-Training of Vision Encoders introduces hierarchical cross-attention between a vision encoder and an LLM to fuse multi-layer visual features rather than flattening embeddings. A three-stage training strategy aligns the vision encoder with the LLM, yielding improved performance on image classification and vision-language tasks (MME, GQA, OK-VQA, ScienceQA) over self-attention baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of treating vision encoders and LLMs as independent modules and the loss of structured visual information when using flattened embeddings. Enable hierarchical, multi-layer feature fusion to strengthen vision-language alignment and gradient flow.
AI.method: Propose HIVE framework that uses hierarchical cross-attention across multiple vision encoder layers to fuse with the LLM. Replace flattened embeddings with structured, multi-layer feature fusion. Employ a three-stage training regimen to progressively align the vision encoder with the LLM for stable multimodal optimization.
AI.result: Empirical results show HIVE achieves superior performance on image classification and various vision-language tasks, outperforming self-attention-based methods on benchmarks such as MME, GQA, OK-VQA, and ScienceQA.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical feature integration between vision encoders and LLMs improves efficiency and expressiveness of vision-language models, enabling better gradient flow and multimodal fusion and suggesting a path toward more capable and compact vision-language systems.

title: Generalizable Dense Reward for Long-Horizon Robotic Tasks
summary: Existing robotic foundation policies are trained primarily via large-scale imitation learning. While such models demonstrate strong capabilities, they often struggle with long-horizon tasks due to distribution shift and error accumulation. While reinforcement learning (RL) can finetune these models, it cannot work well across diverse tasks without manual reward engineering. We propose VLLR, a dense reward framework combining (1) an extrinsic reward from Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for task progress recognition, and (2) an intrinsic reward based on policy self-certainty. VLLR uses LLMs to decompose tasks into verifiable subtasks and then VLMs to estimate progress to initialize the value function for a brief warm-up phase, avoiding prohibitive inference cost during full training; and self-certainty provides per-step intrinsic guidance throughout PPO finetuning. Ablation studies reveal complementary benefits: VLM-based value initialization primarily improves task completion efficiency, while self-certainty primarily enhances success rates, particularly on out-of-distribution tasks. On the CHORES benchmark covering mobile manipulation and navigation, VLLR achieves up to 56% absolute success rate gains over the pretrained policy, up to 5% gains over state-of-the-art RL finetuning methods on in-distribution tasks, and up to $10\%$ gains on out-of-distribution tasks, all without manual reward engineering. Additional visualizations can be found in https://silongyong.github.io/vllr_project_page/

AI.tldr: A dense reward framework (VLLR) combines an extrinsic reward from LLMs/VLMs for progress recognition with an intrinsic per-step reward from policy self-certainty to fine-tune pretrained robotic policies via PPO, improving long-horizon task performance without manual reward engineering.
AI.motivation: To address distribution shift and error accumulation in long-horizon tasks for imitation-learned robotic policies, enabling effective RL fines-tuning without hand-crafted rewards and reducing inference cost.
AI.method: Decompose tasks into verifiable subtasks with LLMs; use VLMs to estimate task progress and initialize the value function for a brief warm-up; apply PPO finetuning with an intrinsic reward based on policy self-certainty.
AI.result: On CHORES, VLLR yields up to 56% absolute improvement in success rate over the pretrained policy, up to 5% gains over state-of-the-art RL finetuning on in-distribution tasks, and up to 10% gains on out-of-distribution tasks, all without manual reward engineering.
AI.conclusion: VLLR demonstrates that combining LLM/VLM-driven extrinsic rewards with intrinsic self-certainty rewards provides complementary benefits—VLM-based value initialization improves efficiency, while self-certainty enhances success rates, particularly out-of-distribution—facilitating robust long-horizon robotic learning.

title: Task-Centric Personalized Federated Fine-Tuning of Language Models
summary: Federated Learning (FL) has emerged as a promising technique for training language models on distributed and private datasets of diverse tasks. However, aggregating models trained on heterogeneous tasks often degrades the overall performance of individual clients. To address this issue, Personalized FL (pFL) aims to create models tailored for each client's data distribution. Although these approaches improve local performance, they usually lack robustness in two aspects: (i) generalization: when clients must make predictions on unseen tasks, or face changes in their data distributions, and (ii) intra-client tasks interference: when a single client's data contains multiple distributions that may interfere with each other during local training. To tackle these two challenges, we propose FedRouter, a clustering-based pFL that builds specialized models for each task rather than for each client. FedRouter uses adapters to personalize models by employing two clustering mechanisms to associate adapters with specific tasks. A local clustering that associate adapters with task data samples and a global one that associates similar adapters from different clients to construct task-centric personalized models. Additionally, we propose an evaluation router mechanism that routes test samples to the best adapter based on the created clusters. Experiments comparing our method with existing approaches across a multitask dataset, FedRouter demonstrate strong resilience in these challenging scenarios performing up to 6.1% relatively better under tasks interference and up to 136% relative improvement under generalization evaluation.

AI.tldr: FedRouter is a clustering-based personalized FL method that assigns adapters to tasks, enabling task-centric models through local and global clustering, plus an evaluation router to route queries to the best adapter; it improves robustness to unseen tasks and intra-client task interference.
AI.motivation: Addresses two major weaknesses of personalized FL on heterogeneous tasks: (1) generalization to unseen tasks or distribution shifts, and (2) intra-client interference when a client’s data contains multiple task distributions.
AI.method: Two clustering mechanisms: a local clustering that links adapters to task data samples, and a global clustering that groups similar adapters across clients to form task-centric personalized models. Models use adapters to personalize, enabling efficient fine-tuning. An evaluation router selects the best adapter for a given test sample by leveraging the inferred clusters.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on a multitask dataset shows FedRouter achieves up to 6.1% relative improvement under task interference scenarios and up to 136% relative improvement under generalization evaluation compared to baselines and existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: FedRouter demonstrates robust task-centric personalization in federated settings by combining local and global adapter clustering with a routing mechanism, effectively mitigating intra-client interference and enhancing generalization across unseen tasks.

title: One Panel Does Not Fit All: Case-Adaptive Multi-Agent Deliberation for Clinical Prediction
summary: Large language models applied to clinical prediction exhibit case-level heterogeneity: simple cases yield consistent outputs, while complex cases produce divergent predictions under minor prompt changes. Existing single-agent strategies sample from one role-conditioned distribution, and multi-agent frameworks use fixed roles with flat majority voting, discarding the diagnostic signal in disagreement. We propose CAMP (Case-Adaptive Multi-agent Panel), where an attending-physician agent dynamically assembles a specialist panel tailored to each case's diagnostic uncertainty. Each specialist evaluates candidates via three-valued voting (KEEP/REFUSE/NEUTRAL), enabling principled abstention outside one's expertise. A hybrid router directs each diagnosis through strong consensus, fallback to the attending physician's judgment, or evidence-based arbitration that weighs argument quality over vote counts. On diagnostic prediction and brief hospital course generation from MIMIC-IV across four LLM backbones, CAMP consistently outperforms strong baselines while consuming fewer tokens than most competing multi-agent methods, with voting records and arbitration traces offering transparent decision audits.

AI.tldr: Case-Adaptive Multi-agent Panel (CAMP) dynamically assembles an attending-physician–led panel of specialists per case, using three-valued voting and a hybrid router to improve diagnostic prediction and hospital course generation, with transparent arbitration and reduced token usage.
AI.motivation: Clinical LLM predictions exhibit case-level heterogeneity: simple cases yield stable outputs while complex cases vary with minor prompt changes. Existing single-agent and fixed-role multi-agent approaches fail to preserve diagnostic signal in disagreement and/or require excessive tokens.
AI.method: For each case, CAMP trains an attending-physician agent to assemble a tailored specialist panel. Each specialist votes KEEP/REFUSE/NEUTRAL on candidates. A hybrid router routes decisions through: (1) strong consensus (KEEP), (2) fallback to attending physician judgment, or (3) evidence-based arbitration that weighs argument quality over raw vote counts. Evaluation on diagnostic prediction and brief hospital course generation from MIMIC-IV across four LLM backbones.
AI.result: CAMP consistently outperforms strong baselines on both diagnostic prediction and hospital-course generation while using fewer tokens than most competing multi-agent methods. The system provides transparent decision audits via voting records and arbitration traces.
AI.conclusion: Case-adaptive, three-valued voting with an arbitration layer yields improved accuracy, efficiency, and auditability for LLM-based clinical predictions, addressing heterogeneity in case complexity.

title: RawGen: Learning Camera Raw Image Generation
summary: Cameras capture scene-referred linear raw images, which are processed by onboard image signal processors (ISPs) into display-referred 8-bit sRGB outputs. Although raw data is more faithful for low-level vision tasks, collecting large-scale raw datasets remains a major bottleneck, as existing datasets are limited and tied to specific camera hardware. Generative models offer a promising way to address this scarcity -- however, existing diffusion frameworks are designed to synthesize photo-finished sRGB images rather than physically consistent linear representations. This paper presents RawGen, to our knowledge the first diffusion-based framework enabling text-to-raw generation for arbitrary target cameras, alongside sRGB-to-raw inversion. RawGen leverages the generative priors of large-scale sRGB diffusion models to synthesize physically meaningful linear outputs, such as CIE XYZ or camera-specific raw representations, via specialized processing in latent and pixel spaces. To handle unknown and diverse ISP pipelines and photo-finishing effects in diffusion-model training data, we build a many-to-one inverse-ISP dataset where multiple sRGB renditions of the same scene generated using diverse ISP parameters are anchored to a common scene-referred target. Fine-tuning a conditional denoiser and specialized decoder on this dataset allows RawGen to obtain camera-centric linear reconstructions that effectively invert the rendering pipeline. We demonstrate RawGen's superior performance over traditional inverse-ISP methods that assume a fixed ISP. Furthermore, we show that augmenting training pipelines with RawGen's scalable, text-driven synthetic data can benefit downstream low-level vision tasks.

AI.tldr: RawGen: diffusion-based text-to-raw generation and inverse-ISP for arbitrary cameras; enables physically consistent linear outputs and data augmentation for low-level vision tasks.
AI.motivation: Raw sensor data are faithful for low-level vision but scarce and tied to specific hardware; existing diffusion models focus on sRGB and finished images, not linear raw representations.
AI.method: Leverage large-scale sRGB diffusion priors to synthesize linear outputs (e.g., CIE XYZ or camera raw) via latent/pixel-space processing. Build a many-to-one inverse-ISP dataset anchoring multiple sRGB renditions of a scene to a common scene-referred target. Fine-tune a conditional denoiser and a specialized decoder to invert the rendering pipeline across diverse ISPs, enabling camera-centric raw reconstructions.
AI.result: Outperforms traditional inverse-ISP methods that assume a fixed ISP; demonstrates superior camera-centric linear reconstructions. Training with RawGen synthetic data improves downstream low-level vision tasks.
AI.conclusion: First diffusion-based framework enabling text-to-raw generation for arbitrary target cameras and scalable raw data synthesis; can alleviate raw data scarcity and benefit downstream low-level vision tasks.

title: MRReP: Mixed Reality-based Hand-drawn Reference Path Editing Interface for Mobile Robot Navigation
summary: Autonomous mobile robots operating in human-shared indoor environments often require paths that reflect human spatial intentions, such as avoiding interference with pedestrian flow or maintaining comfortable clearance. However, conventional path planners primarily optimize geometric costs and provide limited support for explicit route specification by human operators. This paper presents MRReP, a Mixed Reality-based interface that enables users to draw a Hand-drawn Reference Path (HRP) directly on the physical floor using hand gestures. The drawn HRP is integrated into the robot navigation stack through a custom Hand-drawn Reference Path Planner, which converts the user-specified point sequence into a global path for autonomous navigation. We evaluated MRReP in a within-subject experiment against a conventional 2D baseline interface. The results demonstrated that MRReP enhanced path specification accuracy, usability, and perceived workload, while enabling more stable path specification in the physical environment. These findings suggest that direct path specification in MR is an effective approach for incorporating human spatial intention into mobile robot navigation. Additional material is available at https://mertcookimg.github.io/mrrep

AI.tldr: MRReP enables direct hand-drawn floor paths via mixed reality, improving path specification accuracy, usability, and perceived workload, with more stable path specification than a conventional 2D interface.
AI.motivation: Conventional path planners optimize geometric costs and offer limited support for explicit route specification by humans in human-robot shared indoor spaces.
AI.method: A Mixed Reality-based interface (MRReP) lets users draw a Hand-drawn Reference Path (HRP) on the floor using hand gestures. The HRP is converted into a global path by a custom Hand-drawn Reference Path Planner and integrated into the robot's navigation stack. A within-subject experiment compares MRReP to a conventional 2D baseline interface.
AI.result: MRReP enhanced path specification accuracy, usability, and perceived workload, while enabling more stable path specification in the physical environment.
AI.conclusion: Direct path specification in MR is an effective approach to incorporate human spatial intention into mobile robot navigation.

title: Evolution Strategies for Deep RL pretraining
comment: 12 pages, 3 figures, 2 algorithms; EE-568 Reinforcement learning course project
summary: Although Deep Reinforcement Learning has proven highly effective for complex decision-making problems, it demands significant computational resources and careful parameter adjustment in order to develop successful strategies. Evolution strategies offer a more straightforward, derivative-free approach that is less computationally costly and simpler to deploy. However, ES generally do not match the performance levels achieved by DRL, which calls into question their suitability for more demanding scenarios. This study examines the performance of ES and DRL across tasks of varying difficulty, including Flappy Bird, Breakout and Mujoco environments, as well as whether ES could be used for initial training to enhance DRL algorithms. The results indicate that ES do not consistently train faster than DRL. When used as a preliminary training step, they only provide benefits in less complex environments (Flappy Bird) and show minimal or no improvement in training efficiency or stability across different parameter settings when applied to more sophisticated tasks (Breakout and MuJoCo Walker).

AI.tldr: ES generally underperforms DRL across tasks of varying difficulty; any training speed advantage of ES is inconsistent and mostly confined to very simple tasks; using ES as a pretraining bootstrap yields limited or no gains for more complex environments.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether derivative-free evolution strategies can match DRL performance and serve as a cheap bootstrap for DRL across tasks with increasing difficulty.
AI.method: Empirical comparison across tasks of increasing difficulty (Flappy Bird, Breakout, MuJoCo environments). Measure training speed, stability, and final performance for ES vs DRL; test ES as a preliminary training step before DRL and vary a subset of hyperparameters to assess robustness.
AI.result: ES does not consistently accelerate training relative to DRL. When used as pretraining for DRL, benefits occur only in the simplest task (Flappy Bird) and yield minimal or no improvements for Breakout and MuJoCo Walker under varying parameter settings.
AI.conclusion: ES is not a general substitute for DRL in complex decision problems. Hybrid approaches may offer niche benefits in simple tasks, but more systematic exploration is needed to understand when ES can help, and to optimize parameter settings or hybridizations for harder tasks.

title: Open, Reliable, and Collective: A Community-Driven Framework for Tool-Using AI Agents
summary: Tool-integrated LLMs can retrieve, compute, and take real-world actions via external tools, but reliability remains a key bottleneck. We argue that failures stem from both tool-use accuracy (how well an agent invokes a tool) and intrinsic tool accuracy (the tool's own correctness), while most prior work emphasizes the former. We introduce OpenTools, a community-driven toolbox that standardizes tool schemas, provides lightweight plug-and-play wrappers, and evaluates tools with automated test suites and continuous monitoring. We also release a public web demo where users can run predefined agents and tools and contribute test cases, enabling reliability reports to evolve as tools change. OpenTools includes the core framework, an initial tool set, evaluation pipelines, and a contribution protocol. Experiments and evaluations show improved end-to-end reproducibility and task performance; community-contributed, higher-quality task-specific tools deliver 6%-22% relative gains over an existing toolbox across multiple agent architectures on downstream tasks and benchmarks, highlighting the importance of intrinsic tool accuracy.

AI.tldr: OpenTools is a community-driven toolbox that standardizes tool schemas, provides plug-and-play wrappers, and enforces automated testing and monitoring to improve intrinsic tool accuracy and end-to-end reliability for tool-enabled LLMs, yielding 6–22% relative gains across architectures and tasks.
AI.motivation: Reliability is a major bottleneck for tool-integrated LLMs. Failures arise from both tool-use accuracy and intrinsic tool accuracy; prior work overemphasized tool-use accuracy. A standardized, testable, and community-driven tool ecosystem is needed.
AI.method: Introduce OpenTools: a core framework with standardized tool schemas, lightweight wrappers, automated test suites, continuous monitoring, and a public web demo. Includes an initial tool set, evaluation pipelines, and a contribution protocol. Evaluates across multiple agent architectures on downstream tasks and benchmarks.
AI.result: OpenTools improves end-to-end reproducibility and task performance. Community-contributed, higher-quality tools deliver 6%–22% relative gains over an existing toolbox across multiple agent architectures and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Intrinsic tool accuracy is crucial for reliable tool use in LLMs. A community-driven standard (OpenTools) can boost reliability and performance by ensuring consistent tool interfaces, testing, and monitoring, and by enabling iterative improvement via community contributions.

title: Q-Mask: Query-driven Causal Masks for Text Anchoring in OCR-Oriented Vision-Language Models
summary: Optical Character Recognition (OCR) is increasingly regarded as a foundational capability for modern vision-language models (VLMs), enabling them not only to read text in images but also to support downstream reasoning in real-world visual question answering (VQA). However, practical applications further require reliable text anchors, i.e., accurately grounding queried text to its corresponding spatial region. To systematically evaluate this capability, we introduce TextAnchor-Bench (TABench), a benchmark for fine-grained text-region grounding, which reveals that both general-purpose and OCR-specific VLMs still struggle to establish accurate and stable text anchors. To address this limitation, we propose Q-Mask, a precise OCR framework built upon a causal query-driven mask decoder (CQMD). Inspired by chain-of-thought reasoning, Q-Mask performs causal visual decoding that sequentially generates query-conditioned visual masks before producing the final OCR output. This visual CoT paradigm disentangles where the text is from what the text is, enforcing grounded evidence acquisition prior to recognition and enabling explicit text anchor construction during inference. To train CQMD, we construct TextAnchor-26M, a large-scale dataset of image-text pairs annotated with fine-grained masks corresponding to specific textual elements, encouraging stable text-region correspondences and injecting strong spatial priors into VLM training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Q-Mask substantially improves text anchoring and understanding across diverse visual scenes.

AI.tldr: Q-Mask with TextAnchor-26M improves text grounding in VLMs; TABench reveals current shortcomings in text-anchor accuracy, and the approach uses a causal query-driven mask decoder with visual CoT to ground text before recognition.
AI.motivation: To enable reliable grounding of queried text to spatial regions in visual question answering, highlighting the lack of stable, accurate text anchors in existing general-purpose and OCR-specific VLMs.
AI.method: Introduce TABench for fine-grained text-region grounding; develop Q-Mask built on a causal query-driven mask decoder (CQMD) with sequential, query-conditioned visual masks (visual CoT); create TextAnchor-26M, a large mask-annotated image-text dataset to inject strong spatial priors.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that Q-Mask substantially improves text anchoring and understanding across diverse visual scenes, outperforming baselines.
AI.conclusion: Grounding-first OCR via CQMD and a large, mask-annotated dataset provides stronger text-region correspondences and improves downstream reasoning in VLMs.

title: Advancing Multi-Robot Networks via MLLM-Driven Sensing, Communication, and Computation: A Comprehensive Survey
summary:
Imagine advanced humanoid robots, powered by multimodal large language models (MLLMs), coordinating missions across industries like warehouse logistics, manufacturing, and safety rescue. While individual robots show local autonomy, realistic tasks demand coordination among multiple agents sharing vast streams of sensor data. Communication is indispensable, yet transmitting comprehensive data can overwhelm networks, especially when a system-level orchestrator or cloud-based MLLM fuses multimodal inputs for route planning or anomaly detection.
  These tasks are often initiated by high-level natural language instructions. This intent serves as a filter for resource optimization: by understanding the goal via MLLMs, the system can selectively activate relevant sensing modalities, dynamically allocate bandwidth, and determine computation placement. Thus, R2X is fundamentally an intent-to-resource orchestration problem where sensing, communication, and computation are jointly optimized to maximize task-level success under resource constraints.
  This survey examines how integrated design paves the way for multi-robot coordination under MLLM guidance. We review state-of-the-art sensing modalities, communication strategies, and computing approaches, highlighting how reasoning is split between on-device models and powerful edge/cloud servers. We present four end-to-end demonstrations (sense -> communicate -> compute -> act): (i) digital-twin warehouse navigation with predictive link context, (ii) mobility-driven proactive MCS control, (iii) a FollowMe robot with a semantic-sensing switch, and (iv) real-hardware open-vocabulary trash sorting via edge-assisted MLLM grounding. We emphasize system-level metrics -- payload, latency, and success -- to show why R2X orchestration outperforms purely on-device baselines.

AI.tldr: A survey on R2X: intent-to-resource orchestration for multi-robot coordination with multimodal LLMs (MLLMs); joint sensing-communication-computation optimization under resource constraints; demonstrates four end-to-end tasks and emphasizes system-level metrics over device-centric baselines.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, efficient multi-robot coordination under MLLM guidance by leveraging intent-driven filtering to adapt sensing, communication, and computation, reducing network load while maintaining task performance.
AI.method: Survey of sensing modalities, communication strategies, and computing approaches; analysis of division of reasoning between on-device and edge/cloud; proposal of end-to-end demonstrations illustrating sense→communicate→compute→act cycles; evaluation using system-level metrics (payload, latency, success).
AI.result: Frames R2X as an integrated design space for resource-aware orchestration; demonstrates four end-to-end demonstrations showing superiority over purely on-device baselines in system-level metrics.
AI.conclusion: Integrated R2X orchestration enables efficient multi-robot operation under MLLM guidance; effective coupling of intent understanding with selective sensing, bandwidth allocation, and computation placement is essential; future work includes scalability, real-world deployment, and broader evaluation across tasks and networks.

title: Temporal Memory for Resource-Constrained Agents: Continual Learning via Stochastic Compress-Add-Smooth
summary:
An agent that operates sequentially must incorporate new experience without forgetting old experience, under a fixed memory budget. We propose a framework in which memory is not a parameter vector but a stochastic process: a Bridge Diffusion on a replay interval $[0,1]$, whose terminal marginal encodes the present and whose intermediate marginals encode the past. New experience is incorporated via a three-step \emph{Compress--Add--Smooth} (CAS) recursion. We test the framework on the class of models with marginal probability densities modeled via Gaussian mixtures of fixed number of components~$K$ in $d$ dimensions; temporal complexity is controlled by a fixed number~$L$ of piecewise-linear protocol segments whose nodes store Gaussian-mixture states. The entire recursion costs $O(LKd^2)$ flops per day -- no backpropagation, no stored data, no neural networks -- making it viable for controller-light hardware.
  Forgetting in this framework arises not from parameter interference but from lossy temporal compression: the re-approximation of a finer protocol by a coarser one under a fixed segment budget. We find that the retention half-life scales linearly as $a_{1/2}\approx c\,L$ with a constant $c>1$ that depends on the dynamics but not on the mixture complexity~$K$, the dimension~$d$, or the geometry of the target family. The constant~$c$ admits an information-theoretic interpretation analogous to the Shannon channel capacity. The stochastic process underlying the bridge provides temporally coherent ``movie'' replay -- compressed narratives of the agent's history, demonstrated visually on an MNIST latent-space illustration. The framework provides a fully analytical ``Ising model'' of continual learning in which the mechanism, rate, and form of forgetting can be studied with mathematical precision.

AI.tldr: A memory framework for continual learning using a Bridge Diffusion process with a CAS recursion; fixed-budget, no backprop, Gaussian mixtures; linear retention scaling with L; provides analytical model and MNIST demonstration.
AI.motivation: To enable continual learning under a fixed memory budget by modeling memory as a stochastic process rather than fixed parameters, thereby preventing forgetting through principled temporal compression.
AI.method: Define a Bridge Diffusion on the interval [0,1] whose terminal marginal encodes the present and intermediate marginals encode the past. Apply a Compress–Add–Smooth (CAS) recursion. Represent marginal densities with Gaussian mixtures of K components in d dimensions. Use L piecewise-linear protocol segments; nodes store Gaussian-mixture states. Computational cost per day: O(L K d^2); no backpropagation, no stored data, no neural networks.
AI.result: Forgetting arises from lossy temporal compression rather than parameter interference. Retention half-life scales as a_half ≈ c L with c>1, depending on dynamics but not on K, d, or geometry of the target family. The constant c has an information-theoretic interpretation akin to channel capacity. The bridge process enables temporally coherent memory replay (a “movie”). Demonstrated on MNIST latent space; yields an analytical Ising-model view of continual learning to study mechanism, rate, and form of forgetting.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a fully analytical, tractable model of forgetting in continual learning, enabling precise study of memory mechanisms, rates, and forms, while providing a hardware-friendly, backprop-free approach with temporally coherent memory narratives.

title: A Safety-Aware Role-Orchestrated Multi-Agent LLM Framework for Behavioral Health Communication Simulation
summary: Single-agent large language model (LLM) systems struggle to simultaneously support diverse conversational functions and maintain safety in behavioral health communication. We propose a safety-aware, role-orchestrated multi-agent LLM framework designed to simulate supportive behavioral health dialogue through coordinated, role-differentiated agents. Conversational responsibilities are decomposed across specialized agents, including empathy-focused, action-oriented, and supervisory roles, while a prompt-based controller dynamically activates relevant agents and enforces continuous safety auditing. Using semi-structured interview transcripts from the DAIC-WOZ corpus, we evaluate the framework with scalable proxy metrics capturing structural quality, functional diversity, and computational characteristics. Results illustrate clear role differentiation, coherent inter-agent coordination, and predictable trade-offs between modular orchestration, safety oversight, and response latency when compared to a single-agent baseline. This work emphasizes system design, interpretability, and safety, positioning the framework as a simulation and analysis tool for behavioral health informatics and decision-support research rather than a clinical intervention.

AI.tldr: A safety-aware, role-differentiated multi-agent LLM framework for simulating behavioral health dialogue; demonstrates coordinated agent behavior and trade-offs with a single-agent baseline; positioned as a research tool for behavioral health informatics and decision support, not a clinical intervention.
AI.motivation: Single-agent LLMs struggle to balance diverse conversational functions with safety in behavioral health contexts; a modular, interpretable multi-agent framework may improve safety, flexibility, and evaluability for research and decision support.
AI.method: A prompt-based controller dynamically activates specialized agents (empathy-focused, action-oriented, supervisory) and enforces continuous safety auditing; evaluation uses semi-structured interview transcripts from the DAIC-WOZ corpus with proxy metrics for structural quality, functional diversity, and computational characteristics.
AI.result: Clear role differentiation, coherent inter-agent coordination, and predictable trade-offs between modular orchestration, safety oversight, and response latency compared to a single-agent baseline.
AI.conclusion: Frames the framework as a simulation/analysis tool for behavioral health informatics and decision-support research, emphasizing design, interpretability, and safety over serving as a clinical intervention.

title: Suppressing Non-Semantic Noise in Masked Image Modeling Representations
summary: Masked Image Modeling (MIM) has become a ubiquitous self-supervised vision paradigm. In this work, we show that MIM objectives cause the learned representations to retain non-semantic information, which ultimately hurts performance during inference. We introduce a model-agnostic score for semantic invariance using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) on real and synthetic non-semantic images. Based on this score, we propose a simple method, Semantically Orthogonal Artifact Projection (SOAP), to directly suppress non-semantic information in patch representations, leading to consistent improvements in zero-shot performance across various MIM-based models. SOAP is a post-hoc suppression method, requires zero training, and can be attached to any model as a single linear head.

AI.tldr: MIM learns non-semantic features; SOAP suppresses non-semantic artifacts post-hoc, boosting zero-shot performance.
AI.motivation: MIM objectives can entangle representations with non-semantic information, harming downstream semantic inference. A model-agnostic score and a corrective mechanism are needed.
AI.method: Introduce a PCA-based semantic invariance score using real and synthetic non-semantic images. Propose Semantically Orthogonal Artifact Projection (SOAP), a post-hoc, zero-training, linear-head method to suppress non-semantic information in patch representations.
AI.result: SOAP yields consistent zero-shot performance gains across various MIM-based models. It is model-agnostic, requires no training, and can be attached as a single linear head.
AI.conclusion: SOAP provides a simple, training-free approach to enhance semantic invariance in MIM representations and improves zero-shot generalization.

title: Long-Horizon Geometry-Aware Navigation among Polytopes via MILP-MPC and Minkowski-Based CBFs
summary: Autonomous navigation in complex, non-convex environments remains challenging when robot dynamics, control limits, and exact robot geometry must all be taken into account. In this paper, we propose a hierarchical planning and control framework that bridges long-horizon guidance and geometry-aware safety guarantees for a polytopic robot navigating among polytopic obstacles. At the high level, Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) is embedded within a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework to generate a nominal trajectory around polytopic obstacles while modeling the robot as a point mass for computational tractability. At the low level, we employ a control barrier function (CBF) based on the exact signed distance in the Minkowski-difference space as a safety filter to explicitly enforce the geometric constraints of the robot shape, and further extend its formulation to a high-order CBF (HOCBF). We demonstrate the proposed framework in U-shaped and maze-like environments under single- and double-integrator dynamics. The results show that the proposed architecture mitigates the topology-induced local-minimum behavior of purely reactive CBF-based navigation while enabling safe, real-time, geometry-aware navigation.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical MILP-MPC planning with a CBF/HOCBF safety filter enables geometry-aware, real-time navigation for polytopic robots among polytopic obstacles in non-convex environments.
AI.motivation: Navigating non-convex environments with robot dynamics, control limits, and exact robot geometry is challenging; there is a need for long-horizon guidance that respects geometric safety constraints.
AI.method: High-level MILP embedded in MPC to produce a nominal trajectory around polytopes by modeling the robot as a point mass; low-level geometry-aware safety filter using a control barrier function based on exact Minkowski-difference distance, extended to a high-order CBF (HOCBF) to enforce robot-shape constraints.
AI.result: Simulations in U-shaped and maze-like environments with single- and double-integrator dynamics show that the architecture reduces topology-induced local minima common with purely reactive CBF navigation and enables safe, real-time, geometry-aware navigation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical framework successfully couples long-horizon, geometry-aware planning with explicit safety guarantees, improving navigation in non-convex spaces for polytopic robots.

title: Perspective: Towards sustainable exploration of chemical spaces with machine learning
summary: Artificial intelligence is transforming molecular and materials science, but its growing computational and data demands raise critical sustainability challenges. In this Perspective, we examine resource considerations across the AI-driven discovery pipeline--from quantum-mechanical (QM) data generation and model training to automated, self-driving research workflows--building on discussions from the ``SusML workshop: Towards sustainable exploration of chemical spaces with machine learning'' held in Dresden, Germany. In this context, the availability of large quantum datasets has enabled rigorous benchmarking and rapid methodological progress, while also incurring substantial energy and infrastructure costs. We highlight emerging strategies to enhance efficiency, including general-purpose machine learning (ML) models, multi-fidelity approaches, model distillation, and active learning. Moreover, incorporating physics-based constraints within hierarchical workflows, where fast ML surrogates are applied broadly and high-accuracy QM methods are used selectively, can further optimize resource use without compromising reliability. Equally important is bridging the gap between idealized computational predictions and real-world conditions by accounting for synthesizability and multi-objective design criteria, which is essential for practical impact. Finally, we argue that sustainable progress will rely on open data and models, reusable workflows, and domain-specific AI systems that maximize scientific value per unit of computation, enabling efficient and responsible discovery of technological materials and therapeutics.

AI.tldr: A perspective on sustainable AI-driven discovery in chemistry/materials, outlining resource challenges and strategies to reduce compute/energy through multi-fidelity modeling, distillation, active learning, physics-guided hierarchical workflows, and open data/models.
AI.motivation: Growing computational and data demands in AI-enabled molecular and materials discovery raise sustainability concerns; need to balance scientific progress with energy use, infrastructure, and real-world applicability (synthesizability).
AI.method: Literature-based perspective building on the SusML workshop; synthesizes current practices and proposes an integrated framework across QM data generation, model training, and automated, self-driving research workflows.
AI.result: Proposes practical strategies and a roadmap: develop general-purpose ML models; employ multi-fidelity approaches and model distillation; use active learning; embed physics-based constraints within hierarchical workflows (fast ML surrogates with selective high-accuracy QM methods); account for synthesizability and multi-objective design; promote open data/models and reusable workflows; domain-specific AI systems to maximize scientific value per computation.
AI.conclusion: Sustainable progress in AI-driven discovery depends on openness, reusability, and domain-tailored AI that maximize scientific value per computation, enabling efficient and responsible development of materials and therapeutics.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Control of Objective Drift in LLM-Assisted Computer Science Education
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly embedded in computer science education through AI-assisted programming tools, yet such workflows often exhibit objective drift, in which locally plausible outputs diverge from stated task specifications. Existing instructional responses frequently emphasize tool-specific prompting practices, limiting durability as AI platforms evolve. This paper adopts a human-centered stance, treating human-in-the-loop (HITL) control as a stable educational problem rather than a transitional step toward AI autonomy. Drawing on systems engineering and control-theoretic concepts, we frame objectives and world models as operational artifacts that students configure to stabilize AI-assisted work. We propose a pilot undergraduate CS laboratory curriculum that explicitly separates planning from execution and trains students to specify acceptance criteria and architectural constraints prior to code generation. In selected labs, the curriculum also introduces deliberate, concept-aligned drift to support diagnosis and recovery from specification violations. We report a sensitivity power analysis for a three-arm pilot design comparing unstructured AI use, structured planning, and structured planning with injected drift, establishing detectable effect sizes under realistic section-level constraints. The contribution is a theory-driven, methodologically explicit foundation for HITL pedagogy that renders control competencies teachable across evolving AI tools.

AI.tldr: Proposes a theory-driven HITL pedagogy for AI-assisted programming using control-theoretic concepts; pilots an undergrad CS lab curriculum that separates planning from execution, introduces deliberate drift in some labs, and uses a sensitivity power analysis across three conditions to evaluate effectiveness.
AI.motivation: To address objective drift in AI-assisted programming and create durable, platform-agnostic instructional practices by foregrounding human-in-the-loop control as a core educational objective.
AI.method: Design of an undergraduate CS laboratory curriculum that (1) separates planning from execution, (2) trains students to specify acceptance criteria and architectural constraints before code generation, (3) in some labs injects concept-aligned drift to enable diagnosis/recovery, and (4) conducts a three-arm experimental design (unstructured AI use vs structured planning vs structured planning with drift) with a sensitivity power analysis.
AI.result: Demonstrates detectable effect sizes under realistic section-level constraints and lays out a theory-driven, methodologically explicit HITL pedagogy for teaching control competencies across evolving AI tools.
AI.conclusion: HITL control competencies can be taught and remain applicable as AI tools evolve, providing a durable educational foundation beyond tool-specific prompting practices.

title: UCell: rethinking generalizability and scaling of bio-medical vision models
summary: The modern deep learning field is a scale-centric one. Larger models have been shown to consistently perform better than smaller models of similar architecture. In many sub-domains of biomedical research, however, the model scaling is bottlenecked by the amount of available training data, and the high cost associated with generating and validating additional high quality data. Despite the practical hurdle, the majority of the ongoing research still focuses on building bigger foundation models, whereas the alternative of improving the ability of small models has been under-explored. Here we experiment with building models with 10-30M parameters, tiny by modern standards, to perform the single-cell segmentation task. An important design choice is the incorporation of a recursive structure into the model's forward computation graph, leading to a more parameter-efficient architecture. We found that for the single-cell segmentation, on multiple benchmarks, our small model, UCell, matches the performance of models 10-20 times its size, and with a similar generalizability to unseen out-of-domain data. More importantly, we found that ucell can be trained from scratch using only a set of microscopy imaging data, without relying on massive pretraining on natural images, and therefore decouples the model building from any external commercial interests. Finally, we examined and confirmed the adaptability of ucell by performing a wide range of one-shot and few-shot fine tuning experiments on a diverse set of small datasets. Implementation is available at https://github.com/jiyuuchc/ucell

AI.tldr: A tiny recursive model named UCell with 10–30M parameters achieves state-like performance on single-cell segmentation, trained from domain microscopy data only, with strong out-of-domain generalization and few-shot adaptability; code released.
AI.motivation: In biomedical imaging, data is scarce and costly; scaling models via data is limited; demonstrate that small models can match larger ones and decouple from large pretraining.
AI.method: Design of UCell architecture with recursive forward computation; 10–30M parameters; trained from scratch on microscopy data; evaluated on multiple single-cell segmentation benchmarks; conducted one-shot and few-shot fine-tuning on small diverse datasets; no reliance on natural-image pretraining.
AI.result: Small model matches performance of models 10-20x larger across benchmarks; generalizes to unseen data; trainable from scratch with domain data only; adaptable via one-shot and few-shot fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Supports viability of parameter-efficient, small models for biomedical image analysis; reduces dependence on external data and commercial pretraining; invites further exploration into small-model architectures; implementation available at GitHub.

title: Neural-Assisted in-Motion Self-Heading Alignment
summary: Autonomous platforms operating in the oceans require accurate navigation to successfully complete their mission. In this regard, the initial heading estimation accuracy and the time required to achieve it play a critical role. The initial heading is traditionally estimated by model-based approaches employing orientation decomposition. However, methods such as the dual vector decomposition and optimized attitude decomposition achieve satisfactory heading accuracy only after long alignment times. To allow rapid and accurate initial heading estimation, we propose an end-to-end, model-free, neural-assisted framework using the same inputs as the model-based approaches. Our proposed approach was trained and evaluated on real-world dataset captured by an autonomous surface vehicle. Our approach shows a significant accuracy improvement over the model-based approaches achieving an average absolute error improvement of 53%. Additionally, our proposed approach was able to reduce the alignment time by up to 67%. Thus, by employing our proposed approach, the reduction in alignment time and improved accuracy allow for a shorter deployment time of an autonomous platform and increased navigation accuracy during the mission.

AI.tldr: End-to-end, model-free neural-assisted framework improves initial heading estimation for autonomous surface vehicles, outperforming model-based orientation methods with substantial gains in accuracy and faster alignment.
AI.motivation: Rapid and accurate initial heading is critical for deployment readiness and navigation quality in ocean environments, where traditional model-based heading decomposition methods are slow or less accurate during long alignments.
AI.method: An end-to-end neural framework trained on real-world USV data, using the same inputs as model-based approaches, combining data-driven estimation with neural assistance to output heading without relying on explicit orientation decomposition models.
AI.result: Average absolute heading error reduced by 53% on average compared to model-based approaches; alignment (initial heading) time decreased by up to 67%. Demonstrates improved accuracy and faster deployment on a real-world ASV dataset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed end-to-end neural-assisted approach offers significant gains in initial heading estimation speed and accuracy, enabling shorter deployment times and improved navigation performance for autonomous platforms in ocean environments.

title: Empirical Validation of the Classification-Verification Dichotomy for AI Safety Gates
comment: 21 pages, 9 figures. Companion theory paper: doi:10.5281/zenodo.19237451
summary:
Can classifier-based safety gates maintain reliable oversight as AI systems improve over hundreds of iterations? We provide comprehensive empirical evidence that they cannot. On a self-improving neural controller (d=240), eighteen classifier configurations -- spanning MLPs, SVMs, random forests, k-NN, Bayesian classifiers, and deep networks -- all fail the dual conditions for safe self-improvement. Three safe RL baselines (CPO, Lyapunov, safety shielding) also fail. Results extend to MuJoCo benchmarks (Reacher-v4 d=496, Swimmer-v4 d=1408, HalfCheetah-v4 d=1824). At controlled distribution separations up to delta_s=2.0, all classifiers still fail -- including the NP-optimal test and MLPs with 100% training accuracy -- demonstrating structural impossibility.
  We then show the impossibility is specific to classification, not to safe self-improvement itself. A Lipschitz ball verifier achieves zero false accepts across dimensions d in {84, 240, 768, 2688, 5760, 9984, 17408} using provable analytical bounds (unconditional delta=0). Ball chaining enables unbounded parameter-space traversal: on MuJoCo Reacher-v4, 10 chains yield +4.31 reward improvement with delta=0; on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct during LoRA fine-tuning, 42 chain transitions traverse 234x the single-ball radius with zero safety violations across 200 steps. A 50-prompt oracle confirms oracle-agnosticity. Compositional per-group verification enables radii up to 37x larger than full-network balls. At d<=17408, delta=0 is unconditional; at LLM scale, conditional on estimated Lipschitz constants.

AI.tldr: Classifier-based safety gates fail to provide reliable oversight for self-improving AI across diverse architectures and benchmarks; a provable Lipschitz-ball verifier achieves zero false accepts and enables safe, scalable self-improvement with controlled radius growth, outperforming classifier gates and standard safe RL baselines.
AI.motivation: To ensure safe self-improvement of increasingly capable AI systems by evaluating the reliability of classifier-based safety gates and proposing a provable verification framework that can certify safety under perturbations and parameter changes.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation of 18 classifier configurations (MLP, SVM, random forest, k-NN, Bayesian, deep nets) on a self-improving neural controller (d=240); evaluation of three safe RL baselines (CPO, Lyapunov, safety shielding); extension to MuJoCo benchmarks (Reacher-v4 d=496, Swimmer-v4 d=1408, HalfCheetah-v4 d=1824); controlled distribution separations up to delta_s=2.0; NP-optimal test and high-accuracy classifiers; development of a Lipschitz ball verifier with unconditional delta=0; ball chaining for unbounded parameter-space traversal; experiments on MuJoCo Reacher-v4 and LoRA-tuned LLMs (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct); 50-prompt oracle for oracle-agnosticity; compositional per-group verification to scale radii; assessment of unconditional vs conditional guarantees with respect to Lipschitz constants.
AI.result: All classifier-based configurations fail the dual conditions for safe self-improvement; multiple safe RL baselines fail; across MuJoCo benchmarks, at delta_s up to 2.0, classifiers fail—including NP-optimal tests and perfect training accuracy; Lipschitz ball verifier achieves zero false accepts across dimensions up to d=17408 unconditionally (delta=0); ball chaining enables safe, unbounded parameter-space traversal with observed reward improvements (e.g., +4.31) while preserving safety; 50-prompt oracle confirms oracle-agnosticity; compositional per-group verification yields radii up to ~37× larger than full-network balls; for d≤17408, delta=0 is unconditional; for LLMS at scale, guarantees become conditional on estimated Lipschitz constants.
AI.conclusion: Classification-based safety gates are structurally unable to guarantee safe self-improvement; a Lipschitz-based verification approach with provable bounds can provide zero false accepts and enable safe, scalable self-improvement, though practical deployment requires robust estimation of Lipschitz constants and consideration of scale-dependent conditional guarantees.

title: Improvisational Games as a Benchmark for Social Intelligence of AI Agents: The Case of Connections
summary: We formally introduce a improvisational wordplay game called Connections to explore reasoning capabilities of AI agents. Playing Connections combines skills in knowledge retrieval, summarization and awareness of cognitive states of other agents. We show how the game serves as a good benchmark for social intelligence abilities of language model based agents that go beyond the agents' own memory and deductive reasoning and also involve gauging the understanding capabilities of other agents. Finally, we show how through communication with other agents in a constrained environment, AI agents must demonstrate social awareness and intelligence in games involving collaboration.

AI.tldr: Introduces 'Connections', a wordplay game designed to test AI social intelligence and multi-agent reasoning, requiring knowledge retrieval, summarization, and theory of mind in constrained collaborative settings.
AI.motivation: Assess social intelligence in language-model agents beyond memory/deduction by evaluating understanding of other agents and collaborative communication.
AI.method: Formally define and present the game; deploy as a benchmark with experiments illustrating agent interaction, knowledge retrieval, summarization, and estimation of others' cognitive states; analyze performance under constrained communication.
AI.result: Demonstrates that Connections can serve as an effective benchmark for social intelligence; agents must communicate and collaborate under constraints, revealing their awareness of other agents and their own cognitive processes.
AI.conclusion: Connections provides a viable framework for evaluating social cognition and collaborative capabilities in AI language models; implications for designing better multi-agent systems and assessing social reasoning.

title: DreamControl-v2: Simpler and Scalable Autonomous Humanoid Skills via Trainable Guided Diffusion Priors
summary: Developing robust autonomous loco-manipulation skills for humanoids remains an open problem in robotics. While RL has been applied successfully to legged locomotion, applying it to complex, interaction-rich manipulation tasks is harder given long-horizon planning challenges for manipulation. A recent approach along these lines is DreamControl, which addresses these issues by leveraging off-the-shelf human motion diffusion models as a generative prior to guide RL policies during training. In this paper, we investigate the impact of DreamControl's motion prior and propose an improved framework that trains a guided diffusion model directly in the humanoid robot's motion space, aggregating diverse human and robot datasets into a unified embodiment space. We demonstrate that our approach captures a wider range of skills due to the larger training data mixture and establishes a more automated pipeline by removing the need for manual filtering interventions. Furthermore, we show that scaling the generation of reference trajectories is important for achieving robust downstream RL policies. We validate our approach through extensive experiments in simulation and on a real Unitree-G1.

AI.tldr: Extends DreamControl by training a diffusion prior directly in the humanoid motion space using mixed human and robot data, enabling broader skill coverage, an automated pipeline, and improved RL robustness; validated in simulation and on a Unitree-G1.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of external human-motion priors and manual filtering in DreamControl, aiming to improve long-horizon loco-manipulation by enriching the policy prior through a unified embodiment space.
AI.method: Train a guided diffusion model directly in the humanoid motion space and aggregate diverse human and robot datasets into a single embodiment space; remove manual filtering, scale the generation of reference trajectories, and use the diffusion model to guide RL training of loco-manipulation policies; extend DreamControl by using a larger, automated data mixture.
AI.result: Wider range of skills captured due to the larger data mix; more automated pipeline with reduced manual intervention; scaling trajectory generation enhances robustness of downstream RL policies; validated in simulation and on a real Unitree-G1.
AI.conclusion: The proposed embodiment-space diffusion prior improves data efficiency and robustness for humanoid loco-manipulation RL, demonstrating the benefits of unified, larger-scale data and automated trajectory generation across simulated and real hardware.

title: Collaborative AI Agents and Critics for Fault Detection and Cause Analysis in Network Telemetry
summary: We develop algorithms for collaborative control of AI agents and critics in a multi-actor, multi-critic federated multi-agent system. Each AI agent and critic has access to classical machine learning or generative AI foundation models. The AI agents and critics collaborate with a central server to complete multimodal tasks such as fault detection, severity, and cause analysis in a network telemetry system, text-to-image generation, video generation, healthcare diagnostics from medical images and patient records, etcetera. The AI agents complete their tasks and send them to AI critics for evaluation. The critics then send feedback to agents to improve their responses. Collaboratively, they minimize the overall cost to the system with no inter-agent or inter-critic communication. AI agents and critics keep their cost functions or derivatives of cost functions private. Using multi-time scale stochastic approximation techniques, we provide convergence guarantees on the time-average active states of AI agents and critics. The communication overhead is a little on the system, of the order of $\mathcal{O}(m)$, for $m$ modalities and is independent of the number of AI agents and critics. Finally, we present an example of fault detection, severity, and cause analysis in network telemetry and thorough evaluation to check the algorithm's efficacy.

AI.tldr: A federated control framework where autonomous AI agents and critics, each with private cost functions, collaborate via a central server to solve multimodal tasks. It employs multi-time-scale stochastic approximation to guarantee convergence of time-average activity, with low communication overhead of O(m) per modality and no inter-agent/critic communication. Demonstrated on network telemetry fault detection/severity/cause analysis among other multimodal tasks.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable, privacy-preserving collaboration among heterogeneous AI components (agents and critics) across multiple modalities, while ensuring convergence guarantees and efficient communication.
AI.method: Agents and critics operate with private cost functions and coordinate through a central server. No direct inter-agent or inter-critic communication. The framework uses multi-time-scale stochastic approximation to guarantee convergence of time-average active states. Communication overhead grows as O(m) for m modalities. Tasks include fault detection, severity assessment, and cause analysis, with agents leveraging classical ML or foundation models. An empirical network-telemetry example validates the approach.
AI.result: The approach provides convergence guarantees on the time-average states of agents and critics. Empirical evaluation demonstrates efficacy in fault detection, severity, and cause analysis, with modest communication overhead.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables privacy-preserving, scalable collaboration in multi-actor/multi-critic systems across diverse multimodal tasks, backed by convergence guarantees and practical communication efficiency; future work may explore broader applications and robustness enhancements.

title: Benchmarking Interaction, Beyond Policy: a Reproducible Benchmark for Collaborative Instance Object Navigation
summary: We propose Question-Asking Navigation (QAsk-Nav), the first reproducible benchmark for Collaborative Instance Object Navigation (CoIN) that enables an explicit, separate assessment of embodied navigation and collaborative question asking. CoIN tasks an embodied agent with reaching a target specified in free-form natural language under partial observability, using only egocentric visual observations and interactive natural-language dialogue with a human, where the dialogue can help to resolve ambiguity among visually similar object instances. Existing CoIN benchmarks are primarily focused on navigation success and offer no support for consistent evaluation of collaborative interaction. To address this limitation, QAsk-Nav provides (i) a lightweight question-asking protocol scored independently of navigation, (ii) an enhanced navigation protocol with realistic, diverse, high-quality target descriptions, and (iii) an open-source dataset, that includes 28,000 quality-checked reasoning and question-asking traces for training and analysis of interactive capabilities of CoIN models. Using the proposed QAsk-Nav benchmark, we develop Light-CoNav, a lightweight unified model for collaborative navigation that is 3x smaller and 70x faster than existing modular methods, while outperforming state-of-the-art CoIN approaches in generalization to unseen objects and environments. Project page at https://benchmarking-interaction.github.io/

AI.tldr: Introduces QAsk-Nav, a reproducible benchmark for Collaborative Instance Object Navigation (CoIN) that separately evaluates embodied navigation and collaborative question asking; includes a 28k-trace dataset and Light-CoNav, a lightweight model that outperforms state-of-the-art while being smaller and faster.
AI.motivation: Current CoIN benchmarks focus on navigation success and lack standardized, independent evaluation of collaborative interaction, hindering analysis and generalization testing; a unified benchmark is needed to dissect navigation and dialogue components and enable reproducibility.
AI.method: Proposes QAsk-Nav with (i) a lightweight question-asking protocol scored independently from navigation, (ii) enhanced navigation protocol with diverse, high-quality target descriptions, and (iii) an open-source dataset of 28,000 reasoning and question-asking traces. Introduces Light-CoNav, a unified model that is 3x smaller and 70x faster than modular baselines while achieving strong generalization.
AI.result: The benchmark enables independent assessment of collaborative interaction; Light-CoNav surpasses state-of-the-art CoIN approaches in generalization to unseen objects and environments, despite being substantially more efficient.
AI.conclusion: QAsk-Nav provides a reproducible, comprehensive evaluation framework for CoIN, enabling analysis of both navigation and dialogue capabilities and demonstrating that compact unified models can outperform larger modular systems.

title: Play-Testing REMind: Evaluating an Educational Robot-Mediated Role-Play Game
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: This paper presents REMind, an innovative educational robot-mediated role-play game designed to support anti-bullying bystander intervention among children. REMind invites players to observe a bullying scenario enacted by social robots, reflect on the perspectives of the characters, and rehearse defending strategies by puppeteering a robotic avatar. We evaluated REMind through a mixed-methods play-testing study with 18 children aged 9--10. The findings suggest that the experience supported key learning goals related to self-efficacy, perspective-taking, understanding outcomes of defending, and intervention strategies. These results highlight the promise of Robot-Mediated Applied Drama (RMAD) as a novel pedagogical framework to support Social-Emotional Learning.

AI.tldr: REMind is a robot-mediated applied drama for anti-bullying bystander intervention; a mixed-methods study suggests gains in self-efficacy, perspective-taking, and understanding defending outcomes, via rehearsed strategies with a robotic avatar.
AI.motivation: To improve bystander intervention in bullying through an innovative RMAD framework that integrates social robots, drama-based pedagogy, and SEL goals.
AI.method: Mixed-methods play-testing with 18 children aged 9–10. Participants observed bullying enacted by social robots, reflected on characters’ perspectives, and rehearsed defending strategies by puppeteering a robotic avatar; qualitative and quantitative data collected.
AI.result: The experience supported learning goals related to self-efficacy, perspective-taking, outcomes of defending, and intervention strategies; results indicate promise for RMAD in SEL.
AI.conclusion: Robot-Mediated Applied Drama (RMAD) is a promising novel framework for Social-Emotional Learning and anti-bullying education, with potential to facilitate bystander intervention.

title: Learning to Play Blackjack: A Curriculum Learning Perspective
comment: Accepted as an oral presentation at the International Conference on Distributed Artificial Intelligence (DAI 2025). 16 pages, 7 figures
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) agents often struggle with efficiency and performance in complex environments. We propose a novel framework that uses a Large Language Model (LLM) to dynamically generate a curriculum over available actions, enabling the agent to incorporate each action individually. We apply this framework to the game of Blackjack, where the LLM creates a multi-stage training path that progressively introduces complex actions to a Tabular Q-Learning and a Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent. Our evaluation in a realistic 8-deck simulation over 10 independent runs demonstrates significant performance gains over standard training methods. The curriculum-based approach increases the DQN agent's average win rate from 43.97% to 47.41%, reduces the average bust rate from 32.9% to 28.0%, and accelerates the overall workflow by over 74%, with the agent's full training completing faster than the baseline's evaluation phase alone. These results validate that LLM-guided curricula can build more effective, robust, and efficient RL agents.

AI.tldr: A framework uses a Large Language Model to dynamically generate a curriculum over available actions, enabling RL agents to learn steps progressively. Applied to Blackjack with Tabular Q-Learning and a DQN, it yields faster training and improved performance in an 8-deck simulation across 10 runs.
AI.motivation: RL agents often face inefficiency and suboptimal performance in complex environments; curriculum learning guided by an LLM offers a way to progressively introduce actions and complexities to improve learning efficiency and outcomes.
AI.method: LLM-generated multi-stage curricula over actions are constructed and applied to both a Tabular Q-Learning agent and a Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent in Blackjack. Evaluation uses an 8-deck simulation across 10 independent runs, comparing against standard training.
AI.result: DQN performance improved: average win rate rose from 43.97% to 47.41%; average bust rate decreased from 32.9% to 28.0%. Training/workflow accelerated by over 74%, with full training completing faster than the baseline's evaluation phase.
AI.conclusion: LLM-guided curricula can produce more effective, robust, and efficient RL agents by structuredly introducing actions and complexity through curriculum learning.

title: Signals: Trajectory Sampling and Triage for Agentic Interactions
summary: Agentic applications based on large language models increasingly rely on multi-step interaction loops involving planning, action execution, and environment feedback. While such systems are now deployed at scale, improving them post-deployment remains challenging. Agent trajectories are voluminous and non-deterministic, and reviewing each one, whether through human review or auxiliary LLMs, is slow and cost-prohibitive. We propose a lightweight, signal-based framework for triaging agentic interaction trajectories. Our approach computes cheap, broadly applicable signals from live interactions and attaches them as structured attributes for trajectory triage, identifying interactions likely to be informative without affecting online agent behavior. We organize signals into a coarse-grained taxonomy spanning interaction (misalignment, stagnation, disengagement, satisfaction), execution (failure, loop), and environment (exhaustion), designed for computation without model calls.
