Evaluation used 50 TruthfulQA adversarial scenarios with Claude Sonnet 4 and an independent LLM judge; cross-model evaluation with Gemini 2.5 Flash; metrics were sycophancy rates and Fisher's exact test p-values.
AI.result: For Claude Sonnet 4: vanilla Claude sycophancy 12.0% (6/50), static guardrails 4.0% (2/50), Silicon Mirror 2.0% (1/50); 83.3% relative reduction (p = 0.112). For Gemini 2.5 Flash: baseline sycophancy 46.0%; Silicon Mirror reduced by 69.6% (p < 0.001), to approximately 13.8%.
AI.conclusion: The paper frames the validation-before-correction pattern as a distinct RLHF failure mode. The Silicon Mirror shows promising reductions in sycophancy, including cross-model gains, but the first evaluation's non-significant p-value highlights the need for larger samples or more sensitive tests to confirm statistical robustness; overall, the architecture appears effective at preserving factual integrity under adversarial prompts.

title: Neural Reconstruction of LiDAR Point Clouds under Jamming Attacks via Full-Waveform Representation and Simultaneous Laser Sensing
summary: LiDAR sensors are critical for autonomous driving perception, yet remain vulnerable to spoofing attacks. Jamming attacks inject high-frequency laser pulses that completely blind LiDAR sensors by overwhelming authentic returns with malicious signals. We discover that while point clouds become randomized, the underlying full-waveform data retains distinguishable signatures between attack and legitimate signals. In this work, we propose PULSAR-Net, capable of reconstructing authentic point clouds under jamming attacks by leveraging previously underutilized intermediate full-waveform representations and simultaneous laser sensing in modern LiDAR systems. PULSAR-Net adopts a novel U-Net architecture with axial spatial attention mechanisms specifically designed to identify attack-induced signals from authentic object returns in the full-waveform representation. To address the lack of full-waveform representations in existing LiDAR datasets under jamming attacks, we introduce a physics-aware dataset generation pipeline that synthesizes realistic full-waveform representations under jamming attacks. Despite being trained exclusively on synthetic data, PULSAR-Net achieves reconstruction rates of 92% and 73% for vehicles obscured by jamming attacks in real-world static and driving scenarios, respectively.

AI.tldr: PULSAR-Net reconstructs authentic LiDAR point clouds under jamming by exploiting full-waveform representations with a U‑Net and axial attention, achieving 92%/73% reconstruction in static/driving real-world scenarios after synthetic-training.
AI.motivation: LiDAR jamming disables autonomous driving perception. The full-waveform contains attack signatures despite randomized point clouds, suggesting a path to reconstruction using rich intermediate representations.
AI.method: A novel U‑Net with axial spatial attention processes intermediate full-waveform representations. It identifies attack-induced signals within the full-waveform and reconstructs authentic object returns. The approach uses a physics-aware pipeline to synthesize realistic full-waveform data under jamming, enabling training solely on synthetic data while targeting real-world performance.
AI.result: PULSAR-Net reports reconstruction rates of 92% for vehicles under jam in static real-world scenes and 73% in driving scenarios, demonstrating substantial robustness to jamming despite synthetic training.
AI.conclusion: The method demonstrates that leveraging hidden full-waveform information and carefully designed architectures can recover authentic LiDAR perception under jamming. It hinges on synthetic data generation and specialized network design; further real-world validation and hardware integration are needed for deployment.

title: Certificate-Driven Closed-Loop Multi-Agent Path Finding with Inheritable Factorization
summary:
Multi-agent coordination in automated warehouses and logistics is commonly modeled as the Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) problem. Closed-loop MAPF algorithms improve scalability by planning only the next movement and replanning online, but this finite-horizon viewpoint can be shortsighted and makes it difficult to preserve global guarantees and exploit compositional structure. This issue is especially visible in Anytime Closed-Loop Conflict-Based Search (ACCBS), which applies Conflict-Based Search (CBS) over dynamically extended finite horizons but, under finite computational budgets, may terminate with short active prefixes in dense instances.
  We introduce certificate trajectories and their associated fleet budget as a general mechanism for filtering closed-loop updates. A certificate provides a conflict-free fallback plan and a monotone upper bound on the remaining cost; accepting only certificate-improving updates yields completeness. The same budget information induces a budget-limited factorization that enables global, inheritable decomposition across timesteps. Instantiating the framework on ACCBS yields Certificate-Driven Conflict-Based Search (CDCBS). Experiments on benchmark maps show that CDCBS achieves more consistent solution quality than ACCBS, particularly in dense settings, while the proposed factorization reduces effective group size.

AI.tldr: Certificate-Driven Conflict-Based Search (CDCBS) enhances closed-loop MAPF by introducing certificate trajectories and a fleet budget to filter updates, achieving more consistent solution quality in dense scenarios and enabling global, budgeted decomposition.
AI.motivation: Finite-horizon, online replanning in MAPF (e.g., ACCBS) can be shortsighted and lacks global guarantees, especially under limited computation in dense instances.
AI.method: Introduce certificate trajectories that provide a conflict-free fallback plan and a monotone upper bound on remaining cost. Accept only certificate-improving updates to ensure completeness. Use budget information to derive a global, inheritable decomposition across timesteps (budget-limited factorization). Instantiate this framework as CDCBS by applying the mechanism to ACCBS.
AI.result: CDCBS yields more consistent solution quality than ACCBS, particularly in dense settings, and the budgeted factorization reduces effective group size.
AI.conclusion: The certificate and budget framework stabilizes and scales closed-loop MAPF planning, offering completeness guarantees and better performance in dense environments through global decomposition and certificate-driven updates.

title: Offline Constrained RLHF with Multiple Preference Oracles
summary: We study offline constrained reinforcement learning from human feedback with multiple preference oracles. Motivated by applications that trade off performance with safety or fairness, we aim to maximize target population utility subject to a minimum protected group welfare constraint. From pairwise comparisons collected under a reference policy, we estimate oracle-specific rewards via maximum likelihood and analyze how statistical uncertainty propagates through the dual program. We cast the constrained objective as a KL-regularized Lagrangian whose primal optimizer is a Gibbs policy, reducing learning to a convex dual problem. We propose a dual-only algorithm that ensures high-probability constraint satisfaction and provide the first finite-sample performance guarantees for offline constrained preference learning. Finally, we extend our theoretical analysis to accommodate multiple constraints and general f-divergence regularization.

AI.tldr: Offline constrained RL from human feedback using multiple preference oracles, with a KL-regularized dual that yields a Gibbs primal policy and a dual-only algorithm with finite-sample guarantees for constraint satisfaction, extendable to multiple constraints and general f-divergence.
AI.motivation: Address performance–safety/fairness trade-offs in offline RL by optimizing population utility under a minimum welfare constraint for protected groups, using human-preference signals.
AI.method: Estimate oracle rewards from pairwise human comparisons via maximum likelihood; formulate a KL-regularized Lagrangian with a Gibbs policy as primal optimizer; solve a convex dual with a dual-only algorithm; prove high-probability constraint satisfaction and finite-sample guarantees; extend analysis to multiple constraints and general f-divergence regularization.
AI.result: First finite-sample guarantees for offline constrained preference learning; the dual-only approach achieves constraint satisfaction with high probability; theoretical extension to multiple constraints and various f-divergences.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled framework for offline constrained RL from human feedback and establishes finite-sample performance guarantees, with versatility to handle multiple constraints and general f-divergence regularization.

title: Adaptive Parallel Monte Carlo Tree Search for Efficient Test-time Compute Scaling
summary: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) is an effective test-time compute scaling (TTCS) method for improving the reasoning performance of large language models, but its highly variable execution time leads to severe long-tail latency in practice. Existing optimizations such as positive early exit, reduce latency in favorable cases but are less effective when search continues without meaningful progress. We introduce {\it negative early exit}, which prunes unproductive MCTS trajectories, and an {\it adaptive boosting mechanism} that reallocates reclaimed computation to reduce resource contention among concurrent searches. Integrated into vLLM, these techniques substantially reduce p99 end-to-end latency while improving throughput and maintaining reasoning accuracy.

AI.tldr: Negative early exit combined with adaptive boosting reduces long-tail latency and improves throughput for Monte Carlo Tree Search in test-time compute scaling of LLM reasoning, integrated into vLLM, with maintained accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address the high variability and long-tail latency of MCTS-based TTCS in large language model reasoning, which impedes latency guarantees and resource efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce negative early exit to prune unproductive MCTS trajectories and an adaptive boosting mechanism to reallocate reclaimed computation, integrated into the vLLM framework.
AI.result: Substantial reduction in p99 end-to-end latency and improved throughput, while maintaining reasoning accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Negative pruning and adaptive resource reallocation effectively stabilize TTCS latency and enhance concurrent search performance in LLM reasoning pipelines.

title: Dynamic Graph Neural Network with Adaptive Features Selection for RGB-D Based Indoor Scene Recognition
summary: Multi-modality of color and depth, i.e., RGB-D, is of great importance in recent research of indoor scene recognition. In this kind of data representation, depth map is able to describe the 3D structure of scenes and geometric relations among objects. Previous works showed that local features of both modalities are vital for promotion of recognition accuracy. However, the problem of adaptive selection and effective exploitation on these key local features remains open in this field. In this paper, a dynamic graph model is proposed with adaptive node selection mechanism to solve the above problem. In this model, a dynamic graph is built up to model the relations among objects and scene, and a method of adaptive node selection is proposed to take key local features from both modalities of RGB and depth for graph modeling. After that, these nodes are grouped by three different levels, representing near or far relations among objects. Moreover, the graph model is updated dynamically according to attention weights. Finally, the updated and optimized features of RGB and depth modalities are fused together for indoor scene recognition. Experiments are performed on public datasets including SUN RGB-D and NYU Depth v2. Extensive results demonstrate that our method has superior performance when comparing to state-of-the-arts methods, and show that the proposed method is able to exploit crucial local features from both modalities of RGB and depth.

AI.tldr: A dynamic RGB-D graph model with adaptive node selection and multi-level relation grouping improves indoor scene recognition by effectively exploiting cross-modal local features and updating via attention, achieving state-of-the-art results on SUN RGB-D and NYU Depth v2.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of selecting and exploiting key local features from RGB and depth in indoor scene recognition, where local features in both modalities are crucial but their adaptive integration is not well solved.
AI.method: Construct a dynamic graph to model object-scene relations; apply adaptive node selection to extract key local features from RGB and depth; group nodes at three levels to represent near/far relations; update the graph dynamically using attention weights; fuse updated RGB and depth features for final recognition.
AI.result: The method achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods on public datasets SUN RGB-D and NYU Depth v2, demonstrating effective exploitation of crucial local features from both RGB and depth modalities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dynamic RGB-D graph with adaptive node selection and multi-level relation grouping effectively leverages cross-modal local features, with attention-driven updates enhancing indoor scene recognition performance.

title: Reachability-Aware Time Scaling for Path Tracking
summary: This paper studies tracking of collision-free waypoint paths produced by an offline planner for a planar double-integrator system with bounded speed and acceleration. Because sampling-based planners must route around obstacles, the resulting waypoint paths can contain sharp turns and high-curvature regions, so one-step reachability under acceleration limits becomes critical even when the path geometry is collision-free. We build on a pure-pursuit-style, reachability-guided quadratic-program (QP) tracker with a one-step acceleration margin. Offline, we evaluate this margin along a spline fitted to the waypoint path and update a scalar speed-scaling profile so that the required one-step acceleration remains below the available bound. Online, the same look-ahead tracking structure is used to track the scaled reference.

AI.tldr: Feasibility-aware tracking of offline waypoint paths for a planar double-integrator using a one-step acceleration margin and offline speed scaling, combined with a pure-pursuit-style, reachability-guided QP tracker.
AI.motivation: Sampling-based planners often generate high-curvature, sharp-turn waypoint paths; enforcing dynamic feasibility under acceleration limits is essential for reliable tracking.
AI.method: Pure-pursuit-style, reachability-guided QP tracker with a one-step acceleration margin. Offline, evaluate the margin along a spline fitted to the waypoint path and update a scalar speed-scaling profile to keep the required one-step acceleration below the bound. Online, apply the same look-ahead tracking structure to the scaled reference.
AI.result: Proposes a framework integrating offline speed-scaling with a reachability-guided QP tracker; empirical results are not detailed in the abstract, but the approach aims to ensure dynamic feasibility during tracking.
AI.conclusion: A cohesive offline-online tracking strategy for planar double-integrator systems that guarantees acceleration-bounded, collision-free waypoint tracking by adjusting reference speed via a margin-aware spline evaluation and using a QP-based controller.

title: Unsupervised 4D Flow MRI Velocity Enhancement and Unwrapping Using Divergence-Free Neural Networks
summary: This work introduces an unsupervised Divergence and Aliasing-Free neural network (DAF-FlowNet) for 4D Flow Magnetic Resonance Imaging (4D Flow MRI) that jointly enhances noisy velocity fields and corrects phase wrapping artifacts. DAF-FlowNet parameterizes velocities as the curl of a vector potential, enforcing mass conservation by construction and avoiding explicit divergence-penalty tuning. A cosine data-consistency loss enables simultaneous denoising and unwrapping from wrapped phase images. On synthetic aortic 4D Flow MRI generated from computational fluid dynamics, DAF-FlowNet achieved lower errors than existing techniques (up to 11% lower velocity normalized root mean square error, 11% lower directional error, and 44% lower divergence relative to the best-performing alternative across noise levels), with robustness to moderate segmentation perturbations. For unwrapping, at peak velocity/velocity-encoding ratios of 1.4 and 2.1, DAF-FlowNet achieved 0.18% and 5.2% residual wrapped voxels, representing reductions of 72% and 18% relative to the best alternative method, respectively. In scenarios with both noise and aliasing, the proposed single-stage formulation outperformed a state-of-the-art sequential pipeline (up to 15% lower velocity normalized root mean square error, 11% lower directional error, and 28% lower divergence). Across 10 hypertrophic cardiomyopathy patient datasets, DAF-FlowNet preserved fine-scale flow features, corrected aliased regions, and improved internal flow consistency, as indicated by reduced inter-plane flow bias in aortic and pulmonary mass-conservation analyses recommended by the 4D Flow MRI consensus guidelines. These results support DAF-FlowNet as a framework that unifies velocity enhancement and phase unwrapping to improve the reliability of cardiovascular 4D Flow MRI.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised DAF-FlowNet unifies velocity denoising and phase unwrapping in 4D Flow MRI by enforcing divergence-free velocity via curl of vector potential; achieves superior accuracy and unwrap performance on synthetic and patient data.
AI.motivation: Address the need for reliable, parameter-tuning-free denoising and phase unwrapping in 4D Flow MRI to improve quantitative hemodynamics and reduce phase-wrapping artifacts while ensuring mass conservation.
AI.method: DAF-FlowNet represents velocity as the curl of a vector potential to guarantee mass conservation. It uses a cosine data-consistency loss to jointly denoise and unwrap wrapped phase images. Trained on synthetic CFD-generated 4D Flow MRI and evaluated on hypertrophic cardiomyopathy patient datasets, comparing against existing methods, including a sequential pipeline.
AI.result: Outperforms existing techniques across noise levels with up to 11% lower velocity NRMSE, 11% lower directional error, and 44% lower divergence; achieves 0.18% and 5.2% residual wrapped voxels at VENC ratios 1.4 and 2.1 (representing 72% and 18% reductions); single-stage formulation surpasses sequential pipelines in joint denoising and unwrapping; preserves fine-scale flow and reduces inter-plane bias in 10 patient datasets.
AI.conclusion: DAF-FlowNet offers a unified, unsupervised framework for simultaneous velocity enhancement and phase unwrapping in 4D Flow MRI, with built-in mass conservation and improved reliability, suitable for clinical interpretation and aligned with consensus analyses.

title: Does Unification Come at a Cost? Uni-SafeBench: A Safety Benchmark for Unified Multimodal Large Models
summary: Unified Multimodal Large Models (UMLMs) integrate understanding and generation capabilities within a single architecture. While this architectural unification, driven by the deep fusion of multimodal features, enhances model performance, it also introduces important yet underexplored safety challenges. Existing safety benchmarks predominantly focus on isolated understanding or generation tasks, failing to evaluate the holistic safety of UMLMs when handling diverse tasks under a unified framework. To address this, we introduce Uni-SafeBench, a comprehensive benchmark featuring a taxonomy of six major safety categories across seven task types. To ensure rigorous assessment, we develop Uni-Judger, a framework that effectively decouples contextual safety from intrinsic safety. Based on comprehensive evaluations across Uni-SafeBench, we uncover that while the unification process enhances model capabilities, it significantly degrades the inherent safety of the underlying LLM. Furthermore, open-source UMLMs exhibit much lower safety performance than multimodal large models specialized for either generation or understanding tasks. We open-source all resources to systematically expose these risks and foster safer AGI development.

AI.tldr: Introduces Uni-SafeBench and Uni-Judger to evaluate the holistic safety of Unified Multimodal Large Models (UMLMs); finds unification boosts capability but degrades intrinsic safety; open-source UMLMs underperform in safety compared to task-specialized multimodal models; resources are open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Safety evaluation for UMLMs is underexplored: existing benchmarks focus on isolated tasks. A holistic benchmark and a decoupling framework are needed to assess contextual vs intrinsic safety under a unified architecture.
AI.method: Proposes a six-category safety taxonomy across seven task types (Uni-SafeBench) and a framework (Uni-Judger) to separate contextual safety from intrinsic safety. Conducts comprehensive evaluations across UMLMs (including open-source and task-specialized models) using the benchmark.
AI.result: Unification enhances general capabilities but significantly degrades intrinsic safety of the underlying LLM. Open-source UMLMs exhibit markedly lower safety performance than multimodal LMs specialized for generation or understanding tasks.
AI.conclusion: Highlights safety risks in UMLMs and provides open resources (benchmarks and evaluation framework) to drive safer AGI development and more robust, holistic safety assessments.

title: UCMNet: Uncertainty-Aware Context Memory Network for Under-Display Camera Image Restoration
comment: We propose UCMNet, an uncertainty-aware adaptive framework that restores high-frequency details in regions with varying levels of degradation in under-display camera images
summary: Under-display cameras (UDCs) allow for full-screen designs by positioning the imaging sensor underneath the display. Nonetheless, light diffraction and scattering through the various display layers result in spatially varying and complex degradations, which significantly reduce high-frequency details. Current PSF-based physical modeling techniques and frequency-separation networks are effective at reconstructing low-frequency structures and maintaining overall color consistency. However, they still face challenges in recovering fine details when dealing with complex, spatially varying degradation. To solve this problem, we propose a lightweight \textbf{U}ncertainty-aware \textbf{C}ontext-\textbf{M}emory \textbf{Network} (\textbf{UCMNet}), for UDC image restoration. Unlike previous methods that apply uniform restoration, UCMNet performs uncertainty-aware adaptive processing to restore high-frequency details in regions with varying degradations. The estimated uncertainty maps, learned through an uncertainty-driven loss, quantify spatial uncertainty induced by diffraction and scattering, and guide the Memory Bank to retrieve region-adaptive context from the Context Bank. This process enables effective modeling of the non-uniform degradation characteristics inherent to UDC imaging. Leveraging this uncertainty as a prior, UCMNet achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks with 30\% fewer parameters than previous models. Project page: \href{https://kdhrick2222.github.io/projects/UCMNet/}{https://kdhrick2222.github.io/projects/UCMNet}.

AI.tldr: A lightweight uncertainty-aware memory network (UCMNet) for under-display camera restoration, using uncertainty maps to drive region-adaptive memory retrieval to recover high-frequency details, achieving state-of-the-art results with ~30% fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Under-display cameras suffer from spatially varying degradations caused by diffraction and scattering through display layers, which degrade high-frequency details. Existing PSF-based and frequency-separation approaches effectively handle low-frequency content but struggle with non-uniform, high-frequency restoration.
AI.method: Introduce UCMNet with an uncertainty-driven loss to estimate per-pixel uncertainty maps. A Memory Bank stores region-adaptive context and a Context Bank retrieves context guided by the uncertainty maps, enabling non-uniform, region-aware restoration. The uncertainty acts as a prior to guide memory retrieval, resulting in lightweight, adaptive processing.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art restoration performance on multiple benchmarks while using about 30% fewer parameters than prior models.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware context-memory modeling effectively handles non-uniform degradation in UDC imaging, enabling high-frequency detail recovery with a compact network.

title: Multi-Camera View Scaling for Data-Efficient Robot Imitation Learning
summary: The generalization ability of imitation learning policies for robotic manipulation is fundamentally constrained by the diversity of expert demonstrations, while collecting demonstrations across varied environments is costly and difficult in practice. In this paper, we propose a practical framework that exploits inherent scene diversity without additional human effort by scaling camera views during demonstration collection. Instead of acquiring more trajectories, multiple synchronized camera perspectives are used to generate pseudo-demonstrations from each expert trajectory, which enriches the training distribution and improves viewpoint invariance in visual representations. We analyze how different action spaces interact with view scaling and show that camera-space representations further enhance diversity. In addition, we introduce a multiview action aggregation method that allows single-view policies to benefit from multiple cameras during deployment. Extensive experiments in simulation and real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate significant gains in data efficiency and generalization compared to single-view baselines. Our results suggest that scaling camera views provides a practical and scalable solution for imitation learning, which requires minimal additional hardware setup and integrates seamlessly with existing imitation learning algorithms. The website of our project is https://yichen928.github.io/robot_multiview.

AI.tldr: A practical imitation learning framework uses multi-view camera scaling to generate pseudo-demonstrations from a single expert trajectory, improving data efficiency and generalization with minimal extra hardware; it also introduces multiview action aggregation for deployment and validates gains across sim and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Imitation learning performance is constrained by demonstration diversity, and collecting varied expert trajectories is costly. Exploiting intrinsic scene diversity via camera viewpoint augmentation offers a scalable way to enrich training data without increasing human labor.
AI.method: Capture multiple synchronized camera viewpoints during demonstrations to produce pseudo-demonstrations from each expert trajectory. Analyze the interaction between action spaces and view scaling; adopt camera-space representations to boost viewpoint invariance; propose a multiview action aggregation method enabling single-view policies to leverage multiple cameras during deployment.
AI.result: Experiments in simulation and real-world robotic manipulation show significant improvements in data efficiency and generalization over single-view baselines, demonstrating the practical benefits of view scaling and multiview aggregation.
AI.conclusion: Scaling camera views is a practical, scalable augmentation for imitation learning that requires minimal extra hardware and integrates with existing algorithms to improve generalization and data efficiency.

title: Lead Zirconate Titanate Reservoir Computing for Classification of Written and Spoken Digits
summary: In this paper we extend our earlier work of (Rietman et al. 2022) presenting an application of physical Reservoir Computing (RC) to the classification of handwritten and spoken digits. We utilize an unpoled cube of Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT) as a computational substrate to process these datasets. Our results demonstrate that the PZT reservoir achieves 89.0% accuracy on MNIST handwritten digits, representing a 2.4 percentage point improvement over logistic regression baselines applied to the same preprocessed data. However, for the AudioMNIST spoken digits dataset, the reservoir system (88.2% accuracy) performs equivalently to baseline methods (88.1% accuracy), suggesting that reservoir computing provides the greatest benefits for classification tasks of intermediate difficulty where linear methods underperform but the problem remains learnable. PZT is a well-known material already used in semiconductor applications, presenting a low-power computational substrate that can be integrated with digital algorithms. Our findings indicate that physical reservoirs excel when the task difficulty exceeds the capability of simple linear classifiers but remains within the computational capacity of the reservoir dynamics.

AI.tldr: Physical RC with an unpoled PZT cube as a substrate yields MNIST 89.0% and AudioMNIST 88.2%; surpasses LR on MNIST but is on par with baselines for AudioMNIST. Indicates RC gains on intermediate tasks; PZT offers a low-power, integrable substrate.
AI.motivation: Test whether a physical reservoir made from PZT can improve classification for handwritten and spoken digits and identify task regimes where RC provides benefits over linear methods.
AI.method: Use an unpoled PZT cube as a physical reservoir to process preprocessed MNIST and AudioMNIST data; compare RC performance against logistic regression baselines; report accuracies.
AI.result: MNIST accuracy 89.0% (2.4 percentage points above LR baseline); AudioMNIST accuracy 88.2%, essentially matching the 88.1% baseline.
AI.conclusion: Physical PZT reservoirs are beneficial for intermediate-difficulty tasks where linear classifiers underperform but the task remains within the reservoir’s computational capacity; PZT provides a low-power, integrable substrate for such processing.

title: mmAnomaly: Leveraging Visual Context for Robust Anomaly Detection in the Non-Visual World with mmWave Radar
comment: Accepted at the 24th ACM/IEEE International Conference on Embedded Artificial Intelligence and Sensing Systems (SenSys 2026)
summary: mmWave radar enables human sensing in non-visual scenarios-e.g., through clothing or certain types of walls-where traditional cameras fail due to occlusion or privacy limitations. However, robust anomaly detection with mmWave remains challenging, as signal reflections are influenced by material properties, clutter, and multipath interference, producing complex, non-Gaussian distortions. Existing methods lack contextual awareness and misclassify benign signal variations as anomalies. We present mmAnomaly, a multi-modal anomaly detection framework that combines mmWave radar with RGBD input to incorporate visual context. Our system extracts semantic cues-such as scene geometry and material properties-using a fast ResNet-based classifier, and uses a conditional latent diffusion model to synthesize the expected mmWave spectrum for the given visual context. A dual-input comparison module then identifies spatial deviations between real and generated spectra to localize anomalies. We evaluate mmAnomaly on two multi-modal datasets across three applications: concealed weapon localization, through-wall intruder localization, and through-wall fall localization. The system achieves up to 94% F1 score and sub-meter localization error, demonstrating robust generalization across clothing, occlusions, and cluttered environments. These results establish mmAnomaly as an accurate and interpretable framework for context-aware anomaly detection in mmWave sensing.

AI.tldr: mmAnomaly: a multi-modal framework that uses RGBD context to guide mmWave anomaly detection via a diffusion-based spectrum synthesis and dual-input comparison for localization; achieves high F1 and sub-meter accuracy across concealment and through-wall scenarios.
AI.motivation: Robust anomaly detection in mmWave sensing is hampered by non-Gaussian distortions from material properties, clutter, and multipath; lack of contextual awareness leads to misclassification of benign variations.
AI.method: Fuse mmWave radar with RGBD input; extract semantic cues with a fast ResNet-based classifier; employ a conditional latent diffusion model to synthesize the expected mmWave spectrum given the visual context; use a dual-input comparison module to detect spatial deviations between real and generated spectra for localization.
AI.result: Evaluated on two multi-modal datasets across concealed weapon localization, through-wall intruder localization, and through-wall fall localization; up to 94% F1 score and sub-meter localization error; generalizes across clothing, occlusion, and cluttered environments.
AI.conclusion: mmAnomaly provides an accurate, interpretable, context-aware anomaly detection framework for mmWave sensing with strong generalization to challenging non-visual scenarios.

title: A Physical Imitation Learning Pipeline for Energy-Efficient Quadruped Locomotion Assisted by Parallel Elastic Joint
summary: Due to brain-body co-evolution, animals' intrinsic body dynamics play a crucial role in energy-efficient locomotion, which shares control effort between active muscles and passive body dynamics -- a principle known as Embodied Physical Intelligence. In contrast, robot bodies are often designed with one centralised controller that typically suppress the intrinsic body dynamics instead of exploiting it. We introduce Physical Imitation Learning (PIL), which distils a Reinforcement Learning (RL) control policy into physically implementable body responses that can be directly offloaded to passive Parallel Elastic Joints (PEJs), enabling therefore the body to imitate part of the controlled behaviour. Meanwhile, the residual policy commands the motors to recover the RL policy's performance. The results is an overall reduced energy consumption thanks to outsourcing parts of the control policy to the PEJs. Here we show in simulated quadrupeds, that our PIL approach can offloads up to 87% of mechanical power to PEJs on flat terrain and 18% on rough terrain. Because the body design is distilled from -- rather than jointly optimised with -- the control policy, PIL realises brain-body co-design without expanding the search space with body design parameters, providing a computationally efficient route to task-specific Embodied Physical Intelligence applicable to a wide range of joint-based robot morphologies.

AI.tldr: PIL distills a reinforcement learning policy into passive, physically implementable body responses via parallel elastic joints (PEJs), offloading substantial control to the body's passive dynamics. This yields energy savings (up to 87% of mechanical power on flat terrain, 18% on rough terrain) and enables brain-body co-design without expanding the search space.
AI.motivation: Exploit embodied physical intelligence by leveraging passive body dynamics to share control with active actuators, reducing energy consumption and simplifying controller design in legged robots.
AI.method: Train an RL policy for locomotion, then distill parts of the policy into passive body responses implemented by PEJs, while using a residual policy to compensate and recover performance. Evaluate in simulated quadrupeds on flat and rough terrain to measure power offloading and energy efficiency.
AI.result: Significant energy savings through offloading to PEJs: up to 87% of mechanical power on flat terrain and 18% on rough terrain. The approach enables brain-body co-design without jointly optimizing body design and control policy, and is computationally efficient for a wide range of joint-based morphologies.
AI.conclusion: PIL provides a practical route to Embodied Physical Intelligence by distributing control between passive body dynamics and neural policies, reducing actuator energy usage, and allowing task-specific morphologies without expanding the design/search space.

title: Measuring the Representational Alignment of Neural Systems in Superposition
summary: Comparing the internal representations of neural networks is a central goal in both neuroscience and machine learning. Standard alignment metrics operate on raw neural activations, implicitly assuming that similar representations produce similar activity patterns. However, neural systems frequently operate in superposition, encoding more features than they have neurons via linear compression. We derive closed-form expressions showing that superposition systematically deflates Representational Similarity Analysis, Centered Kernel Alignment, and linear regression, causing networks with identical feature content to appear dissimilar. The root cause is that these metrics are dependent on cross-similarity between two systems' respective superposition matrices, which under assumption of random projection usually differ significantly, not on the latent features themselves: alignment scores conflate what a system represents with how it represents it. Under partial feature overlap, this confound can invert the expected ordering, making systems sharing fewer features appear more aligned than systems sharing more. Crucially, the apparent misalignment need not reflect a loss of information; compressed sensing guarantees that the original features remain recoverable from the lower-dimensional activity, provided they are sparse. We therefore argue that comparing neural systems in superposition requires extracting and aligning the underlying features rather than comparing the raw neural mixtures.

AI.tldr: Superposition biases similarity metrics (RSA, CKA, linear regression) by mixing multiple features in compressed neural representations; content may be dissimilar even when features are identical due to random projections; advocate feature-level alignment instead of raw activity comparisons.
AI.motivation: To explain why common representational similarity measures fail or give misleading results when neural systems encode more features than neurons via linear compression (superposition).
AI.method: Derive closed-form expressions for RSA, CKA, and linear regression under superposition with random projections; analyze cross-similarity of systems' superposition matrices; examine effect of partial feature overlap and sparsity (compressed sensing) on recoverability and metric behavior.
AI.result: Demonstrates that identical feature content can appear dissimilar under standard metrics due to cross-system superpositions; partial feature overlap can reverse similarity ordering; sparsity guarantees feature recoverability, but metrics still conflate content with representation; proposes feature-level alignment.
AI.conclusion: For meaningful cross-system comparisons, extract and align underlying features rather than comparing raw superposed neural mixtures.

title: Ontology-Constrained Neural Reasoning in Enterprise Agentic Systems: A Neurosymbolic Architecture for Domain-Grounded AI Agents
comment: 23 pages, 7 tables, 4 figures, 33 references. Empirical evaluation: 600 runs across 5 regulated industries including Vietnamese-language domains
summary: Enterprise adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) is constrained by hallucination, domain drift, and the inability to enforce regulatory compliance at the reasoning level. We present a neurosymbolic architecture implemented within the Foundation AgenticOS (FAOS) platform that addresses these limitations through ontology-constrained neural reasoning. Our approach introduces a three-layer ontological framework--Role, Domain, and Interaction ontologies--that provides formal semantic grounding for LLM-based enterprise agents. We formalize the concept of asymmetric neurosymbolic coupling, wherein symbolic ontological knowledge constrains agent inputs (context assembly, tool discovery, governance thresholds) while proposing mechanisms for extending this coupling to constrain agent outputs (response validation, reasoning verification, compliance checking). We evaluate the architecture through a controlled experiment (600 runs across five industries: FinTech, Insurance, Healthcare, Vietnamese Banking, and Vietnamese Insurance), finding that ontology-coupled agents significantly outperform ungrounded agents on Metric Accuracy (p < .001, W = .460), Regulatory Compliance (p = .003, W = .318), and Role Consistency (p < .001, W = .614), with improvements greatest where LLM parametric knowledge is weakest--particularly in Vietnam-localized domains. Our contributions include: (1) a formal three-layer enterprise ontology model, (2) a taxonomy of neurosymbolic coupling patterns, (3) ontology-constrained tool discovery via SQL-pushdown scoring, (4) a proposed framework for output-side ontological validation, (5) empirical evidence for the inverse parametric knowledge effect that ontological grounding value is inversely proportional to LLM training data coverage of the domain, and (6) a production system serving 21 industry verticals with 650+ agents.

AI.tldr: Ontology-grounded neurosymbolic enterprise agents (FAOS) with a three-layer ontology (Role, Domain, Interaction); asymmetric neurosymbolic coupling constraining inputs and outputs; evaluated with a 600-run controlled experiment across FinTech, Insurance, Healthcare, and Vietnamese banks/insurers; significant improvements in Metric Accuracy, Regulatory Compliance, and Role Consistency; production deployment across 21 verticals with 650+ agents.
AI.motivation: Mitigate hallucination, domain drift, and inability to enforce regulatory compliance at reasoning time by providing formal semantic grounding for LLM-based enterprise agents via ontologies and neurosymbolic coupling.
AI.method: Proposes a three-layer enterprise ontology model (Role, Domain, Interaction). Defines asymmetric neurosymbolic coupling where ontological knowledge constrains inputs (context assembly, tool discovery, governance thresholds) and extends to constrain outputs (response validation, reasoning verification, compliance checking). Includes ontology-constrained tool discovery via SQL-pushdown scoring and a framework for output-side ontological validation. Evaluated through a controlled experiment (600 runs across FinTech, Insurance, Healthcare, Vietnamese Banking, Vietnamese Insurance).
AI.result: Ontology-coupled agents significantly outperform ungrounded agents on Metric Accuracy (p<.001, W=.460), Regulatory Compliance (p=.003, W=.318), and Role Consistency (p<.001, W=.614). Largest gains occur where LLM parametric knowledge is weakest, notably in Vietnam-localized domains. Production system covers 21 industry verticals with 650+ agents.
AI.conclusion: Provides (1) a formal three-layer enterprise ontology model, (2) a taxonomy of neurosymbolic coupling patterns, (3) ontology-constrained tool discovery via SQL-pushdown scoring, (4) a framework for output-side ontological validation, (5) empirical evidence for the inverse parametric knowledge effect, and (6) a production deployment achieving wide industry coverage (21 verticals, 650+ agents).

title: Mine-JEPA: In-Domain Self-Supervised Learning for Mine-Like Object Classification in Side-Scan Sonar
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 6 tables. Accepted at CVPR 2026 MACVi Workshop
summary: Side-scan sonar (SSS) mine classification is a challenging maritime vision problem characterized by extreme data scarcity and a large domain gap from natural images. While self-supervised learning (SSL) and general-purpose vision foundation models have shown strong performance in general vision and several specialized domains, their use in SSS remains largely unexplored. We present Mine-JEPA, the first in-domain SSL pipeline for SSS mine classification, using SIGReg, a regularization-based SSL loss, to pretrain on only 1,170 unlabeled sonar images. In the binary mine vs. non-mine setting, Mine-JEPA achieves an F1 score of 0.935, outperforming fine-tuned DINOv3 (0.922), a foundation model pretrained on 1.7B images. For 3-class mine-like object classification, Mine-JEPA reaches 0.820 with synthetic data augmentation, again outperforming fine-tuned DINOv3 (0.810). We further observe that applying in-domain SSL to foundation models degrades performance by 10--13 percentage points, suggesting that stronger pretrained models do not always benefit from additional domain adaptation. In addition, Mine-JEPA with a compact ViT-Tiny backbone achieves competitive performance while using 4x fewer parameters than DINOv3. These results suggest that carefully designed in-domain self-supervised learning is a viable alternative to much larger foundation models in data-scarce maritime sonar imagery.

AI.tldr: Mine-JEPA introduces an in-domain SSL pipeline for SSS mine classification using SIGReg pretraining on 1,170 unlabeled sonar images, achieving state-of-the-art F1 scores with a compact ViT-Tiny backbone and outperforming large foundation models, while showing that domain-adapted SSL on top of big models can hurt performance.
AI.motivation: Address extreme data scarcity and large domain gap between SSS imagery and natural images, by leveraging in-domain self-supervised pretraining to improve mine classification without relying on massive pretrained vision models.
AI.method: Propose Mine-JEPA with SIGReg, a regularization-based SSL loss, pretrained on a small unlabeled SSS corpus (1,170 images). Evaluate on binary (mine vs non-mine) and 3-class mine-like object classification, with synthetic data augmentation, using a compact ViT-Tiny backbone. Compare against fine-tuned DINOv3 (pretrained on 1.7B images) and study impact of applying in-domain SSL to foundation models.
AI.result: Mine-JEPA achieves F1=0.935 for binary classification (versus 0.922 for fine-tuned DINOv3) and F1=0.820 for 3-class classification (versus 0.810 for DINOv3). Applying in-domain SSL to foundation models degrades performance by 10–13 percentage points. With ViT-Tiny, Mine-JEPA attains competitive performance using 4x fewer parameters than DINOv3.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed in-domain self-supervised learning is a viable, data-efficient alternative to much larger foundation models for SSS imagery, particularly in data-scarce maritime sonar contexts.

title: StretchBot: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Adaptive Guidance with Assistive Robots
summary: Assistive robots have growing potential to support physical wellbeing in home and healthcare settings, for example, by guiding users through stretching or rehabilitation routines. However, existing systems remain largely scripted, which limits their ability to adapt to user state, environmental context, and interaction dynamics. In this work, we present StretchBot, a hybrid neuro-symbolic robotic coach for adaptive assistive guidance. The system combines multimodal perception with knowledge-graph-grounded large language model reasoning to support context-aware adjustments during short stretching sessions while maintaining a structured routine. To complement the system description, we report an exploratory pilot comparison between scripted and adaptive guidance with three participants. The pilot findings suggest that the adaptive condition improved perceived adaptability and contextual relevance, while scripted guidance remained competitive in smoothness and predictability. These results provide preliminary evidence that structured actionable knowledge can help ground language-model-based adaptation in embodied assistive interaction, while also highlighting the need for larger, longitudinal studies to evaluate robustness, generalizability, and long-term user experience.

AI.tldr: Hybrid neuro-symbolic StretchBot provides adaptive, context-aware stretch guidance using multimodal perception and knowledge-graph-grounded LLMs; preliminary pilot suggests improved adaptability and context relevance versus scripted guidance.
AI.motivation: Current assistive robots are largely scripted, limiting adaptation to user state, environment, and interaction dynamics. A hybrid approach with structured knowledge aims to ground language model reasoning for embodied guidance.
AI.method: Develop StretchBot integrating multimodal perception with a knowledge-graph-grounded LLM. Conduct an exploratory pilot (n=3) comparing adaptive versus scripted guidance during short stretching sessions.
AI.result: Adaptive guidance increased perceived adaptability and contextual relevance; scripted guidance remained competitive in smoothness and predictability.
AI.conclusion: Structured actionable knowledge can ground LM-driven adaptation in embodied assistive interaction, but there is a need for larger, longitudinal studies to assess robustness, generalizability, and long-term user experience.

title: Diversity-Aware Reverse Kullback-Leibler Divergence for Large Language Model Distillation
summary: Reverse Kullback-Leibler (RKL) divergence has recently emerged as the preferred objective for large language model (LLM) distillation, consistently outperforming forward KL (FKL), particularly in regimes with large vocabularies and significant teacher-student capacity mismatch, where RKL focuses learning on dominant modes rather than enforcing dense alignment. However, RKL introduces a structural limitation that drives the student toward overconfident predictions. We first provide an analysis of RKL by decomposing its gradients into target and non-target components, and show that non-target gradients consistently push the target logit upward even when the student already matches the teacher, thereby reducing output diversity. In addition, RKL provides weak supervision over non-target classes, leading to poor tail alignment. To address these issues, we propose Diversity-aware RKL (DRKL), which removes this gradient effect and strengthens non-target supervision while preserving the optimization benefits of RKL. Extensive experiments across datasets and model families demonstrate that DRKL consistently outperforms FKL, RKL, and other state-of-the-art distillation objectives, achieving better performance and a superior fidelity-diversity trade-off.

AI.tldr: This work analyzes Reverse KL (RKL) in LLM distillation, showing that non-target gradient components push the target logit upward, reducing output diversity and weakening tail alignment. It introduces Diversity-aware RKL (DRKL), which removes the detrimental gradient effect and strengthens non-target supervision while preserving RKL's optimization benefits. Empirically, DRKL outperforms FKL, RKL, and other distillation objectives across datasets and model families, achieving a better fidelity–diversity trade-off.
AI.motivation: Address overconfidence and poor tail alignment induced by RKL in distillation, while retaining its optimization advantages when distilling with large vocabularies and teacher–student capacity gaps.
AI.method: Gradient decomposition of RKL into target and non-target components, theoretical analysis of how non-target gradients affect predictions, and the design of DRKL to mitigate these effects by removing the harmful gradient influence and strengthening non-target supervision, with empirical evaluation across diverse datasets and model families.
AI.result: DRKL consistently surpasses FKL, RKL, and other distillation objectives in accuracy and in fidelity–diversity balance, demonstrating improved tail alignment and more diverse output distributions.
AI.conclusion: DRKL provides a robust distillation objective that preserves RKL’s benefits while mitigating overconfident predictions and weak tail supervision, yielding better performance and a superior fidelity–diversity trade-off across tasks and models.

title: Agent psychometrics: Task-level performance prediction in agentic coding benchmarks
summary: As the focus in LLM-based coding shifts from static single-step code generation to multi-step agentic interaction with tools and environments, understanding which tasks will challenge agents and why becomes increasingly difficult. This is compounded by current practice: agent performance is typically measured by aggregate pass rates on benchmarks, but single-number metrics obscure the diversity of tasks within a benchmark. We present a framework for predicting success or failure on individual tasks tailored to the agentic coding regime. Our approach augments Item Response Theory (IRT) with rich features extracted from tasks, including issue statements, repository contexts, solutions, and test cases, and introduces a novel decomposition of agent ability into LLM and scaffold ability components. This parameterization enables us to aggregate evaluation data across heterogeneous leaderboards and accurately predict task-level performance for unseen benchmarks, as well as unseen LLM-scaffold combinations. Our methods have practical utility for benchmark designers, who can better calibrate the difficulty of their new tasks without running computationally expensive agent evaluations.

AI.tldr: A task-level success predictor for agentic coding that extends IRT with rich task features and a two-component agent ability (LLM and scaffold), enabling cross-benchmark prediction and benchmark calibration without expensive evaluations.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks report aggregate pass rates that mask task-level variability. There is a need to predict which tasks will challenge agents and to calibrate benchmark difficulty efficiently, especially in multi-step, tool-using agentic coding.
AI.method: Augment Item Response Theory with rich task features (issue statements, repository contexts, solutions, test cases) and decompose agent ability into LLM ability and scaffold ability. Train across heterogeneous leaderboards to predict task-level success for unseen benchmarks and unseen LLM-scaffold combinations.
AI.result: Accurate predictions of task-level performance on unseen benchmarks and unseen LLM-scaffold pairs. Demonstrated ability to aggregate evaluation data across heterogeneous leaderboards and calibrate task difficulty without direct agent evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables task-level evaluation, improves benchmark design, and reduces reliance on costly agent executions by enabling reliable cross-benchmark predictions.

title: Advancing Complex Video Object Segmentation via Tracking-Enhanced Prompt: The 1st Winner for 5th PVUW MOSE Challenge
comment: 1st Place Solution for the 5th PVUW MOSE Challenge (CVPR 2026 Workshop)
summary: In the Complex Video Object Segmentation task, researchers are required to track and segment specific targets within cluttered environments, which rigorously tests a method's capability for target comprehension and environmental adaptability. Although SAM3, the current state-of-the-art solution, exhibits unparalleled segmentation performance and robustness on conventional targets, it underperforms on tiny and semantic-dominated objects. The root cause of this limitation lies in SAM3's insufficient comprehension of these specific target types. To address this issue, we propose TEP: Advancing Complex Video Object Segmentation via Tracking-Enhanced Prompts. As a training-free approach, TEP leverages external tracking models and Multimodal Large Language Models to introduce tracking-enhanced prompts, thereby alleviating the difficulty SAM3 faces in understanding these challenging targets. Our method achieved first place (56.91%) on the test set of the PVUW Challenge 2026: Complex Video Object Segmentation Track.

AI.tldr: Tracking-enhanced prompts (TEP) improve Complex Video Object Segmentation by leveraging external trackers and multimodal LLMs, enabling training-free guidance and achieving top results on PVUW 2026.
AI.motivation: Address SAM3's weaknesses on tiny and semantic-dominated objects in cluttered videos by injecting tracking-informed prompts without retraining.
AI.method: Integrates external tracking models and Multimodal LLMs to generate prompts that guide segmentation; training-free; uses prompts to enhance target comprehension.
AI.result: Achieves 1st place with 56.91% on PVUW Challenge 2026 CVOS Track test set.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of tracking-informed prompts for enhancing complex video segmentation without model training; potential for broader adoption in CVOS tasks.

title: LiPS: Lightweight Panoptic Segmentation for Resource-Constrained Robotics
comment: Submitted to IEEE ICIP 2026. Under review
summary: Panoptic segmentation is a key enabler for robotic perception, as it unifies semantic understanding with object-level reasoning. However, the increasing complexity of state-of-the-art models makes them unsuitable for deployment on resource-constrained platforms such as mobile robots. We propose a novel approach called LiPS that addresses the challenge of efficient-to-compute panoptic segmentation with a lightweight design that retains query-based decoding while introducing a streamlined feature extraction and fusion pathway. It aims at providing a strong panoptic segmentation performance while substantially lowering the computational demands. Evaluations on standard benchmarks demonstrate that LiPS attains accuracy comparable to much heavier baselines, while providing up to 4.5 higher throughput, measured in frames per second, and requiring nearly 6.8 times fewer computations. This efficiency makes LiPS a highly relevant bridge between modern panoptic models and real-world robotic applications.

AI.tldr: LiPS is a lightweight, query-based panoptic segmentation model for robotics that preserves query-based decoding while simplifying feature extraction and fusion, achieving near state-of-the-art accuracy with substantially higher throughput and lower compute.
AI.motivation: Panoptic segmentation is essential for robotic perception, but modern models are too computationally demanding for resource-constrained platforms. There is a need for efficient models that retain effective decoding and performance.
AI.method: LiPS retains a query-based decoding framework but introduces a streamlined feature extraction and fusion pathway to reduce computational load while maintaining performance.
AI.result: LiPS achieves accuracy comparable to heavier baselines on standard benchmarks, with up to 4.5x higher throughput (frames per second) and roughly 6.8x fewer computations, making it suitable for real-time robotic applications.
AI.conclusion: LiPS provides a practical bridge between state-of-the-art panoptic models and real-world robotic deployment by delivering strong performance with significantly reduced compute and higher throughput.

title: Neural Collapse Dynamics: Depth, Activation, Regularisation, and Feature Norm Threshold
summary: Neural collapse (NC) -- the convergence of penultimate-layer features to a simplex equiangular tight frame -- is well understood at equilibrium, but the dynamics governing its onset remain poorly characterised. We identify a simple and predictive regularity: NC occurs when the mean feature norm reaches a model-dataset-specific critical value, fn*, that is largely invariant to training conditions. This value concentrates tightly within each (model, dataset) pair (CV < 8%); training dynamics primarily affect the rate at which fn approaches fn*, rather than the value itself. In standard training trajectories, the crossing of fn below fn* consistently precedes NC onset, providing a practical predictor with a mean lead time of 62 epochs (MAE 24 epochs). A direct intervention experiment confirms fn* is a stable attractor of the gradient flow -- perturbations to feature scale are self-corrected during training, with convergence to the same value regardless of direction (p>0.2). Completing the (architecture)x(dataset) grid reveals the paper's strongest result: ResNet-20 on MNIST gives fn* = 5.867 -- a +458% architecture effect versus only +68% on CIFAR-10. The grid is strongly non-additive; fn* cannot be decomposed into independent architecture and dataset contributions. Four structural regularities emerge: (1) depth has a non-monotonic effect on collapse speed; (2) activation jointly determines both collapse speed and fn*; (3) weight decay defines a three-regime phase diagram -- too little slows, an optimal range is fastest, and too much prevents collapse; (4) width monotonically accelerates collapse while shifting fn* by at most 13%. These results establish feature-norm dynamics as an actionable diagnostic for predicting NC timing, suggesting that norm-threshold behaviour is a general mechanism underlying delayed representational reorganisation in deep networks.

AI.tldr: Neural collapse onset is governed by a model-dataset-specific critical mean feature norm fn*: NC occurs when the mean penultimate-layer norm reaches fn*, with the trajectory typically crossing below fn* beforehand, providing a predictive lead time; fn* is largely invariant to training conditions, and four structural regularities modulate its value across architecture/dataset.
AI.motivation: To understand and predict the onset dynamics of neural collapse, which is well-characterized at equilibrium but whose approach dynamics are unclear; identify a simple, predictive norm-based criterion and test its generality across models and datasets.
AI.method: Empirical study across architecture–dataset grid, tracking the mean norm of penultimate-layer features during training; identify fn* and its invariance; test attractor behavior via perturbation (gradient-flow) experiments; quantify lead times, non-additivity, and four structural regularities; report effects of depth, activation, width, and weight decay.
AI.result: Key findings: (i) fn* is model-dataset-specific but training-condition-invariant, with training variability contributing mainly to the approach rate; cross-dataset and cross-architecture comparisons show CV < 8%; (ii) NC onset reliably follows the crossing of the mean norm below fn*, with a mean lead time of ~62 epochs (MAE 24); (iii) fn* acts as a stable attractor under gradient flow; perturbations to feature scale are corrected and converge to the same fn*; (iv) architecture–dataset grid reveals strong, strongly non-additive effects; e.g., ResNet-20 on MNIST yields fn* = 5.867 (458% architecture effect) versus +68% on CIFAR-10; (v) four regularities: (a) depth non-monotonic on collapse speed; (b) activation determines both speed and fn*; (c) weight decay creates a three-regime phase diagram; too little slows down, an optimal range speeds up, too much prevents collapse; (d) width accelerates collapse and shifts fn* by at most ~13%.
AI.conclusion: Feature-norm dynamics provide an actionable diagnostic for predicting NC timing. The observed norm-threshold behavior appears to be a general mechanism governing delayed representational reorganization in deep nets, with substantial architecture–dataset non-additivity and clear guidance on how depth, activation, width, and weight decay shape collapse."

title: CircuitProbe: Predicting Reasoning Circuits in Transformers via Stability Zone Detection
comment: 11 pages, 1 figure, 3 tables. Code available at https://github.com/agenticclass/circuitprobe
summary: Transformer language models contain localized reasoning circuits, contiguous layer blocks that improve reasoning when duplicated at inference time. Finding these circuits currently requires brute-force sweeps costing 25 GPU hours per model. We propose CircuitProbe, which predicts circuit locations from activation statistics in under 5 minutes on CPU, providing a speedup of three to four orders of magnitude. We find that reasoning circuits come in two types: stability circuits in early layers, detected through the derivative of representation change, and magnitude circuits in late layers, detected through anomaly scoring. We validate across 9 models spanning 6 architectures, including 2025 models, confirming that CircuitProbe top predictions match or are within 2 layers of the optimal circuit in all validated cases. A scaling experiment across the Qwen 2.5 family reveals that layer duplication consistently benefits models under 3B parameters but degrades performance in 7B+ models, making this a practical scaling technique for small language models. CircuitProbe requires as few as 10 calibration examples and its predictions are stable across English, Hindi, Chinese, and French.

AI.tldr: CircuitProbe enables fast, CPU-based prediction of Transformer circuit locations, achieving 3–4 orders of magnitude speedup over brute-force searches; uncovers two circuit types (stability in early layers via derivative of representation change; magnitude in late layers via anomaly scoring); validated on 9 models across 6 architectures (including 2025 models), with top predictions within two layers of optimal; scaling: beneficial for <3B models but degrades beyond that (7B+); requires as few as 10 calibration examples; predictions are multilingual stable across English, Hindi, Chinese, and French.
AI.motivation: Identify localized reasoning circuits in transformer language models efficiently. Brute-force circuit searches are expensive (≈25 GPU hours per model), hindering systematic analysis and scaling across many models and languages.
AI.method: CircuitProbe predicts circuit locations from activation statistics. It detects two circuit types: (1) stability circuits in early layers via the derivative of representation change, and (2) magnitude circuits in late layers via anomaly scoring. The method requires as few as 10 calibration examples and runs on CPU.
AI.result: Significant speedup (3–4 orders of magnitude) over brute-force searches. Top predictions match or are within 2 layers of the optimal circuit across 9 models spanning 6 architectures (including 2025 models). A scaling study on Qwen 2.5 shows duplication benefits models <3B but degrades 7B+ models. Predictions are stable across English, Hindi, Chinese, and French.
AI.conclusion: CircuitProbe offers a practical, scalable tool for locating localized reasoning circuits in small to mid-sized language models and across languages. It enables rapid analysis and suggests a model-size-dependent effect of circuit duplication on performance.

title: VLM-in-the-Loop: A Plug-In Quality Assurance Module for ECG Digitization Pipelines
summary: ECG digitization could unlock billions of archived clinical records, yet existing methods collapse on real-world images despite strong benchmark numbers. We introduce \textbf{VLM-in-the-Loop}, a plug-in quality assurance module that wraps any digitization backend with closed-loop VLM feedback via a standardized interface, requiring no modification to the underlying digitizer. The core mechanism is \textbf{tool grounding}: anchoring VLM assessment in quantitative evidence from domain-specific signal analysis tools. In a controlled ablation on 200 records with paired ground truth, tool grounding raises verdict consistency from 71\% to 89\% and doubles fidelity separation ($Δ$PCC 0.03 $\rightarrow$ 0.08), with the effect replicating across three VLMs (Claude Opus~4, GPT-4o, Gemini~2.5 Pro), confirming a pattern-level rather than model-specific gain. Deployed across four backends, the module improves every one: 29.4\% of borderline leads improved on our pipeline; 41.2\% of failed limb leads recovered on ECG-Digitiser; valid leads per image doubled on Open-ECG-Digitizer (2.5 $\rightarrow$ 5.8). On 428 real clinical HCM images, the integrated system reaches 98.0\% Excellent quality. Both the plug-in architecture and tool-grounding mechanism are domain-parametric, suggesting broader applicability wherever quality criteria are objectively measurable.

AI.tldr: A plug-in VLM-in-the-Loop QA module wraps any ECG digitizer with tool-grounded VLM feedback to improve real-world quality, showing consistent gains across backends and VLMs, achieving 98% Excellent quality on 428 HCM images and demonstrating domain-parametric generalizability.
AI.motivation: Real-world digitization often underperforms despite strong benchmark metrics; there is a need for reliable, domain-aware quality assurance that leverages vision-language models and can be applied without modifying existing digitizers.
AI.method: Introduce a plug-in module (VLM-in-the-Loop) that interfaces with any digitization backend without modification. It uses tool grounding, anchoring VLM judgments to quantitative evidence from domain-specific signal analysis tools. The approach is validated via an ablation on 200 records with paired ground truth, across three VLMs (Claude Opus 4, GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Pro) and four backends; metrics include verdict consistency, ΔPCC for fidelity separation, and lead-quality gains across several pipelines.
AI.result: Veredict consistency rises from 71% to 89%; fidelity separation (ΔPCC) increases from 0.03 to 0.08; gains replicate across VLMs. Across backends: 29.4% of borderline leads improved; 41.2% of failed limb leads recovered; valid leads per image doubled on Open-ECG-Digitizer (2.5→5.8); on 428 real clinical HCM images, 98.0% Excellent quality.
AI.conclusion: The plug-in architecture and tool-grounding are domain-parametric, suggesting broader applicability to digitization quality assurance in domains where objective quality criteria can be measured, with gains arising from pattern-level improvements rather than model-specific effects.

title: SoftHand Model-W: A 3D-Printed, Anthropomorphic, Underactuated Robot Hand with Integrated Wrist and Carpal Tunnel
comment: Accepted for publication at the International Conference of Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026, Vienna
summary: This paper presents the SoftHand Model-W: a 3D-printed, underactuated, anthropomorphic robot hand based on the Pisa/IIT SoftHand, with an integrated antagonistic tendon mechanism and 2 degree-of-freedom tendon-driven wrist. These four degrees-of-acuation provide active flexion and extension to the five fingers, and active flexion/extension and radial/ulnar deviation of the palm through the wrist, while preserving the synergistic and self-adaptive features of such SoftHands. A carpal tunnel-inspired tendon routing allows remote motor placement in the forearm, reducing distal inertia and maintaining a compact form factor. The SoftHand-W is mounted on a 6-axis robot arm and tested with two reorientation tasks requiring coordination between the hand and arm's pose: cube stacking and in-plane disc rotation. Results comparing task time, arm joint travel, and configuration changes with and without wrist actuation show that adding the wrist reduces compensatory and reconfiguration movements of the arm for a quicker task-completion time. Moreover, the wrist enables pick-and-place operations that would be impossible otherwise. Overall, the SoftHand Model-W demonstrates how proximal degrees of freedom are key to achieving versatile, human-like manipulation in real world robotic applications, with a compact design enabling deployment in research and assistive settings.

AI.tldr: A proximal, tendon-driven wrist integrated with a SoftHand-W enhances manipulation by reducing arm compensations and enabling new pick-and-place tasks, validated on cube stacking and disc rotation.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of fully distal actuation in soft hands by adding a proximal degree of freedom that preserves synergy while reducing distal inertia, enabling more versatile, real-world manipulation.
AI.method: Design and 3D-printed SoftHand-W with an antagonistic tendon mechanism and a 2-DOF tendon-driven wrist featuring carpal-tunnel‑inspired tendon routing for remote motor placement; mount on a 6‑axis arm; conduct two reorientation tasks (cube stacking, in‑plane disc rotation) comparing performance with and without wrist actuation; metrics include task time, arm joint travel, and configuration changes.
AI.result: Wrist actuation reduces compensatory arm movements and configuration changes, shortens task completion time, and enables pick-and-place tasks that were impossible without wrist actuation.
AI.conclusion: Proximal degrees of freedom are crucial for versatile, human-like manipulation with soft hands; the SoftHand-W design remains compact and well-suited for research and assistive deployment while preserving the inherent synergy of SoftHands.

title: MAC-Attention: a Match-Amend-Complete Scheme for Fast and Accurate Attention Computation
summary: Long-context decoding in LLMs is IO-bound: each token re-reads an ever-growing KV cache. Prior accelerations cut bytes via compression, which lowers fidelity, or selection/eviction, which restricts what remains accessible, and both can degrade delayed recall and long-form generation. We introduce MAC-Attention, a fidelity- and access-preserving alternative that accelerates decoding by reusing prior attention computations for semantically similar recent queries. It starts with a match stage that performs pre-RoPE L2 matching over a short local window; an amend stage rectifies the reused attention by recomputing a small band near the match boundary; and a complete stage fuses the rectified results with fresh attention computed on the KV tail through a numerically stable merge. On a match hit, the compute and bandwidth complexity is constant regardless of context length. The method is model-agnostic and composes with IO-aware kernels, paged-KV managers, and MQA/GQA. Across LongBench v2 (120K), RULER (120K), and LongGenBench (16K continuous generation), compared to the latest FlashInfer library, MAC-Attention reduces KV accesses by up to 99%, cuts token generation latency by over 60% at 128K, and achieves over 14.3x attention-phase speedups, up to 2.6x end-to-end, while maintaining full-attention quality. By reusing computation, MAC-Attention delivers long-context inference that is both fast and faithful. Code is available here: https://github.com/YJHMITWEB/MAC-Attention.git

AI.tldr: MAC-Attention is a fidelity- and access-preserving method to accelerate long-context decoding by reusing prior attention computations, achieving large reductions in KV accesses and latency while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: Long-context decoding in LLMs is IO-bound due to repeated KV cache reads; existing compression or eviction trades fidelity/access; need scalable, faithful long-context inference.
AI.method: Three-stage MAC-Attention: match stage uses local L2 pre-RoPE matching over a short window; amend stage recomputes a small boundary band to fix reused attention; complete stage numerically stable merge with fresh attention on the KV tail; model-agnostic and compatible with IO-aware kernels and MQA/GQA.
AI.result: On LongBench v2, RULER, LongGenBench, MAC-Attention reduces KV accesses up to 99%, lowers generation latency by >60% at 128K context, yields ~14.3x attention-phase speedups and up to 2.6x end-to-end, while preserving full-attention quality.
AI.conclusion: MAC-Attention enables fast, faithful long-context inference; code is available at the provided GitHub repository.

title: UK AISI Alignment Evaluation Case-Study
summary: This technical report presents methods developed by the UK AI Security Institute for assessing whether advanced AI systems reliably follow intended goals. Specifically, we evaluate whether frontier models sabotage safety research when deployed as coding assistants within an AI lab. Applying our methods to four frontier models, we find no confirmed instances of research sabotage. However, we observe that Claude Opus 4.5 Preview (a pre-release snapshot of Opus 4.5) and Sonnet 4.5 frequently refuse to engage with safety-relevant research tasks, citing concerns about research direction, involvement in self-training, and research scope. We additionally find that Opus 4.5 Preview shows reduced unprompted evaluation awareness compared to Sonnet 4.5, while both models can distinguish evaluation from deployment scenarios when prompted. Our evaluation framework builds on Petri, an open-source LLM auditing tool, with a custom scaffold designed to simulate realistic internal deployment of a coding agent. We validate that this scaffold produces trajectories that all tested models fail to reliably distinguish from real deployment data. We test models across scenarios varying in research motivation, activity type, replacement threat, and model autonomy. Finally, we discuss limitations including scenario coverage and evaluation awareness.

AI.tldr: No confirmed research sabotage by frontier coding assistants; some models refuse safety-relevant tasks; an auditing framework (Petri-based) with a deployment-scaffold is effective but limited.
AI.motivation: Assess whether frontier AI systems reliably follow intended goals and do not sabotage safety research when deployed as coding assistants in labs.
AI.method: Use Petri-based auditing tool with a custom scaffold simulating internal deployment of a coding agent; test four frontier models across scenarios varying research motivation, activity type, replacement threat, and model autonomy; assess evaluation awareness and ability to distinguish evaluation from deployment.
AI.result: No confirmed instances of research sabotage found across four frontier models. Some models (Claude Opus 4.5 Preview and Sonnet 4.5) frequently refuse safety-relevant research tasks due to concerns about research direction, self-training, and scope. Opus 4.5 Preview shows reduced unprompted evaluation awareness relative to Sonnet 4.5. Both models can distinguish evaluation from deployment scenarios when prompted. The scaffold yields trajectories indistinguishable from real deployment data. Limitations include scenario coverage and evaluation-awareness gaps.
AI.conclusion: The auditing framework is effective for detecting sabotage tendencies and evaluating evaluation awareness, but coverage is incomplete. Further work should broaden scenario coverage and address evaluation-awareness limitations to improve robustness.

title: Improving Generalization of Deep Learning for Brain Metastases Segmentation Across Institutions
summary:
Background: Deep learning has demonstrated significant potential for automated brain metastases (BM) segmentation; however, models trained at a singular institution often exhibit suboptimal performance at various sites due to disparities in scanner hardware, imaging protocols, and patient demographics. The goal of this work is to create a domain adaptation framework that will allow for BM segmentation to be used across multiple institutions.
  Methods: We propose a VAE-MMD preprocessing pipeline that combines variational autoencoders (VAE) with maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) loss, incorporating skip connections and self-attention mechanisms alongside nnU-Net segmentation. The method was tested on 740 patients from four public databases: Stanford, UCSF, UCLM, and PKG, evaluated by domain classifier's accuracy, sensitivity, precision, F1/F2 scores, surface Dice (sDice), and 95th percentile Hausdorff distance (HD95).
  Results: VAE-MMD reduced domain classifier accuracy from 0.91 to 0.50, indicating successful feature alignment across institutions. Reconstructed volumes attained a PSNR greater than 36 dB, maintaining anatomical accuracy. The combined method raised the mean F1 by 11.1% (0.700 to 0.778), the mean sDice by 7.93% (0.7121 to 0.7686), and reduced the mean HD95 by 65.5% (11.33 to 3.91 mm) across all four centers compared to the baseline nnU-Net.
