Introduces DVGT-2, a streaming model that outputs dense 3D geometry and current-frame trajectory planning using temporal causal attention, sliding-window streaming, and historical caches, enabling cross-camera generic planning without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Dense 3D geometry provides comprehensive information for 3D driving planning and decision-making. Existing geometry methods are batch-based and unsuitable for online planning, while end-to-end VLA models emphasize language tasks that may not optimally support geometry-driven planning.
AI.method: DVGT-2 processes inputs in an online fashion with temporal causal attention, caches historical features, and employs a sliding-window streaming strategy to produce dense geometry and planning for the current frame. It aims to be robust across diverse camera configurations without fine-tuning and is evaluated on NAVSIM (closed-loop) and nuScenes (open-loop).
AI.result: DVGT-2 achieves faster inference and superior geometry reconstruction across datasets, and demonstrates cross-configuration generalization without fine-tuning, applying directly to planning on NAVSIM and nuScenes.
AI.conclusion: Streaming, geometry-centric planning via DVGT-2 offers real-time, online autonomous driving guidance with broad configurational generalization, advocating a shift from language-auxiliary tasks to dense geometric cues in planning.

title: Deep Networks Favor Simple Data
summary:
Estimated density is often interpreted as indicating how typical a sample is under a model. Yet deep models trained on one dataset can assign \emph{higher} density to simpler out-of-distribution (OOD) data than to in-distribution test data. We refer to this behavior as the OOD anomaly. Prior work typically studies this phenomenon within a single architecture, detector, or benchmark, implicitly assuming certain canonical densities. We instead separate the trained network from the density estimator built from its representations or outputs. We introduce two estimators: Jacobian-based estimators and autoregressive self-estimators, making density analysis applicable to a wide range of models.
  Applying this perspective to a range of models, including iGPT, PixelCNN++, Glow, score-based diffusion models, DINOv2, and I-JEPA, we find the same striking regularity that goes beyond the OOD anomaly: \textbf{lower-complexity samples receive higher estimated density, while higher-complexity samples receive lower estimated density}. This ordering appears within a test set and across OOD pairs such as CIFAR-10 and SVHN, and remains highly consistent across independently trained models. To quantify these orderings, we introduce Spearman rank correlation and find striking agreement both across models and with external complexity metrics. Even when trained only on the lowest-density (most complex) samples or \textbf{even a single such sample} the resulting models still rank simpler images as higher density.
  These observations lead us beyond the original OOD anomaly to a more general conclusion: deep networks consistently favor simple data. Our goal is not to close this question, but to define and visualize it more clearly. We broaden its empirical scope and show that it appears across architectures, objectives, and density estimators.

AI.tldr: Deep nets trained on one dataset assign higher density to simpler OOD data than to in-distribution data, a phenomenon dubbed the OOD anomaly. The paper decouples density estimation from architecture, introducing two estimator families (Jacobian-based and autoregressive self-estimators) and evaluating across diverse models. It reports a consistent ordering: lower-complexity samples receive higher estimated density across test sets and OOD pairs, quantified with Spearman correlation and robust to training on only the lowest-density samples. The authors conclude that deep networks consistently favor simple data and broaden this observation across architectures, objectives, and density estimators.
AI.motivation: to understand why neural networks exhibit a density bias where simpler data appear denser, challenging the idea that higher density always corresponds to typical/in-distribution data, and to generalize this analysis beyond single architectures or benchmarks by decoupling the density estimator from the model.
AI.method: introduce two density estimators—Jacobian-based estimators and autoregressive self-estimators—applied to a range of models (iGPT, PixelCNN++, Glow, score-based diffusion models, DINOv2, I-JEPA). Assess density-ordering of samples by complexity, use Spearman rank correlation to quantify cross-model and cross-dataset consistency, and show that training on the lowest-density (most complex) samples still yields a higher density ranking for simpler images.
AI.result: across architectures and datasets, simpler samples consistently receive higher estimated density than more complex ones; the ordering persists within a test set and across OOD pairs (e.g., CIFAR-10 vs SVHN) and is highly consistent across independently trained models; Spearman correlation shows strong agreement with external complexity metrics; even modestly trained on only the lowest-density samples preserves the simple-vs-dense ranking.
AI.conclusion: the observed tendency is not limited to a single anomaly but reveals a general bias of deep networks toward simpler data; this warrants broader empirical scope and visualization across architectures and objectives, and challenges naive interpretations of density as a proxy for typicality.

title: AceTone: Bridging Words and Colors for Conditional Image Grading
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026. Project Page: github.com/martian422/AceTone
summary: Color affects how we interpret image style and emotion. Previous color grading methods rely on patch-wise recoloring or fixed filter banks, struggling to generalize across creative intents or align with human aesthetic preferences. In this study, we propose AceTone, the first approach that supports multimodal conditioned color grading within a unified framework. AceTone formulates grading as a generative color transformation task, where a model directly produces 3D-LUTs conditioned on text prompts or reference images. We develop a VQ-VAE based tokenizer which compresses a $3\times32^3$ LUT vector to 64 discrete tokens with $ΔE<2$ fidelity. We further build a large-scale dataset, AceTone-800K, and train a vision-language model to predict LUT tokens, followed by reinforcement learning to align outputs with perceptual fidelity and aesthetics. Experiments show that AceTone achieves state-of-the-art performance on both text-guided and reference-guided grading tasks, improving LPIPS by up to 50% over existing methods. Human evaluations confirm that AceTone's results are visually pleasing and stylistically coherent, demonstrating a new pathway toward language-driven, aesthetic-aligned color grading.

AI.tldr: Multimodal LUT-based color grading via VQ-VAE tokenization and RL-aligned vision-language modeling; achieves SOTA in text- and reference-guided grading.
AI.motivation: Need for generalizable color grading that matches human aesthetics across creative intents, outperforming patch-wise recoloring and fixed filters.
AI.method: AceTone treats grading as a generative color transformation. It outputs 3D-LUTs conditioned on text prompts or reference images. A VQ-VAE tokenizer compresses a 3×32^3 LUT vector to 64 discrete tokens with ΔE<2 fidelity. Builds AceTone-800K dataset; trains a vision-language model to predict LUT tokens; uses reinforcement learning to align outputs with perceptual fidelity and aesthetics.
AI.result: SOTA performance on both text-guided and reference-guided grading tasks; LPIPS improvements up to 50% over existing methods; human evaluations indicate visually pleasing and stylistically coherent results.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable pathway for language-driven, aesthetic-aligned color grading within a unified multimodal framework; enables versatile, user-driven styling through text or reference inputs.

title: ProOOD: Prototype-Guided Out-of-Distribution 3D Occupancy Prediction
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/7uHeng/ProOOD
summary: 3D semantic occupancy prediction is central to autonomous driving, yet current methods are vulnerable to long-tailed class bias and out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs, often overconfidently assigning anomalies to rare classes. We present ProOOD, a lightweight, plug-and-play method that couples prototype-guided refinement with training-free OOD scoring. ProOOD comprises (i) prototype-guided semantic imputation that fills occluded regions with class-consistent features, (ii) prototype-guided tail mining that strengthens rare-class representations to curb OOD absorption, and (iii) EchoOOD, which fuses local logit coherence with local and global prototype matching to produce reliable voxel-level OOD scores. Extensive experiments on five datasets demonstrate that ProOOD achieves state-of-the-art performance on both in-distribution 3D occupancy prediction and OOD detection. On SemanticKITTI, it surpasses baselines by +3.57% mIoU overall and +24.80% tail-class mIoU; on VAA-KITTI, it improves AuPRCr by +19.34 points, with consistent gains across benchmarks. These improvements yield more calibrated occupancy estimates and more reliable OOD detection in safety-critical urban driving. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/7uHeng/ProOOD.

AI.tldr: ProOOD is a lightweight, plug-and-play framework to improve 3D semantic occupancy prediction by addressing long-tailed class bias and OOD inputs through prototype-guided refinements and a training-free OOD scoring mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: 3D occupancy prediction in autonomous driving suffers from long-tailed class distributions and out-of-distribution inputs, leading to overconfident misclassifications for rare/unknown classes. A robust, calibrated, and efficient method is needed to improve both in-distribution accuracy and OOD detection.
AI.method: Three components: (i) prototype-guided semantic imputation to fill occluded regions with class-consistent features, (ii) prototype-guided tail mining to strengthen rare-class representations and reduce OOD absorption, and (iii) EchoOOD, which fuses local logit coherence with local and global prototype matching to produce voxel-level OOD scores. The approach is lightweight and training-free for OOD scoring, and is designed as a plug-and-play module.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five datasets show state-of-the-art performance in in-distribution 3D occupancy prediction and OOD detection. On SemanticKITTI, +3.57% mIoU overall and +24.80% tail-class mIoU vs. baselines; on VAA-KITTI, AuPRCr improved by +19.34 points. Gains are consistent across benchmarks, yielding better-calibrated occupancy estimates and more reliable OOD detection.
AI.conclusion: ProOOD delivers calibrated, reliable voxel-level occupancy predictions and improved OOD detection in safety-critical urban driving, with publicly available source code.

title: A Cross-graph Tuning-free GNN Prompting Framework
summary: GNN prompting aims to adapt models across tasks and graphs without requiring extensive retraining. However, most existing graph prompt methods still require task-specific parameter updates and face the issue of generalizing across graphs, limiting their performance and undermining the core promise of prompting. In this work, we introduce a Cross-graph Tuning-free Prompting Framework (CTP), which supports both homogeneous and heterogeneous graphs, can be directly deployed to unseen graphs without further parameter tuning, and thus enables a plug-and-play GNN inference engine. Extensive experiments on few-shot prediction tasks show that, compared to SOTAs, CTP achieves an average accuracy gain of 30.8% and a maximum gain of 54%, confirming its effectiveness and offering a new perspective on graph prompt learning.

AI.tldr: CTP is a tuning-free cross-graph prompting framework for GNNs that works on both homogeneous and heterogeneous graphs, enabling plug-and-play inference on unseen graphs without parameter updates, and delivering substantial few-shot performance gains over SOTAs.
AI.motivation: Graph prompting often requires task-specific parameter updates and struggles to generalize across graphs, undermining the core idea of prompting. A tuning-free, graph-general prompting method could enable true cross-graph transfer.
AI.method: Introduce Cross-graph Tuning-free Prompting Framework (CTP) that supports homogeneous and heterogeneous graphs and can be deployed to unseen graphs without further parameter tuning, functioning as a plug-and-play GNN inference engine.
AI.result: In few-shot prediction tasks, CTP achieves an average accuracy gain of 30.8% and a maximum gain of 54% over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: CTP offers a new perspective on graph prompt learning by delivering tuning-free, cross-graph promptability and strong performance, addressing generalization and retraining concerns.

title: FreqPhys: Repurposing Implicit Physiological Frequency Prior for Robust Remote Photoplethysmography
summary: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) enables contactless physiological monitoring by capturing subtle skin-color variations from facial videos. However, most existing methods predominantly rely on time-domain modeling, making them vulnerable to motion artifacts and illumination fluctuations, where weak physiological clues are easily overwhelmed by noise. To address these challenges, we propose FreqPhys, a frequency-guided rPPG framework that explicitly leverages physiological frequency priors for robust signal recovery. Specifically, FreqPhys first applies a Physiological Bandpass Filtering module to suppress out-of-band interference, and then performs Physiological Spectrum Modulation together with adaptive spectral selection to emphasize pulse-related frequency components while suppress residual in-band noise. A Cross-domain Representation Learning module further fuses these spectral priors with deep time-domain features to capture informative spatial--temporal dependencies. Finally, a frequency-aware conditional diffusion process progressively reconstructs high-fidelity rPPG signals. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks demonstrate that FreqPhys yields significant improvements over state-of-the-art approaches, particularly under challenging motion conditions. It highlights the importance of explicitly modeling physiological frequency priors. The source code will be released.

AI.tldr: Proposes FreqPhys, a frequency-guided rPPG framework that leverages physiological priors via bandpass filtering, spectrum modulation with adaptive spectral selection, cross-domain representation learning, and a frequency-aware diffusion process to robustly recover rPPG signals; achieves state-of-the-art gains on six benchmarks, especially under motion; code release planned.
AI.motivation: Existing rPPG methods rely heavily on time-domain modeling, making them vulnerable to motion artifacts and illumination changes which obscure weak physiological cues. Explicitly incorporating physiological frequency priors can guide signal recovery and improve robustness.
AI.method: 1) Physiological Bandpass Filtering to suppress out-of-band interference; 2) Physiological Spectrum Modulation with adaptive spectral selection to emphasize pulse-related frequencies and suppress residual in-band noise; 3) Cross-domain Representation Learning that fuses spectral priors with deep time-domain features to capture spatial–temporal dependencies; 4) frequency-aware conditional diffusion process to progressively reconstruct high-fidelity rPPG signals.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six benchmarks show significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods, particularly under challenging motion conditions, demonstrating the value of explicitly modeling physiological frequency priors.
AI.conclusion: Modeling physiological frequency priors is beneficial for robust rPPG recovery. FreqPhys achieves strong performance under motion, with a diffusion-based reconstruction mechanism and a plan to release source code.

title: G-Drift MIA: Membership Inference via Gradient-Induced Feature Drift in LLMs
comment: 14 pages, 3 figures and tables. Accepted in ICPR-2026 conference, to appear in the Springer LNCS proceedings
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are trained on massive web-scale corpora, raising growing concerns about privacy and copyright. Membership inference attacks (MIAs) aim to determine whether a given example was used during training. Existing LLM MIAs largely rely on output probabilities or loss values and often perform only marginally better than random guessing when members and non-members are drawn from the same distribution. We introduce G-Drift MIA, a white-box membership inference method based on gradient-induced feature drift. Given a candidate (x,y), we apply a single targeted gradient-ascent step that increases its loss and measure the resulting changes in internal representations, including logits, hidden-layer activations, and projections onto fixed feature directions, before and after the update. These drift signals are used to train a lightweight logistic classifier that effectively separates members from non-members. Across multiple transformer-based LLMs and datasets derived from realistic MIA benchmarks, G-Drift substantially outperforms confidence-based, perplexity-based, and reference-based attacks. We further show that memorized training samples systematically exhibit smaller and more structured feature drift than non-members, providing a mechanistic link between gradient geometry, representation stability, and memorization. In general, our results demonstrate that small, controlled gradient interventions offer a practical tool for auditing the membership of training-data and assessing privacy risks in LLMs.

AI.tldr: G-Drift MIA is a white-box membership inference attack using a single gradient ascent step to induce feature drift, training a lightweight classifier to separate training members from non-members; it outperforms existing output-based MIAs and links gradient geometry to memorization.
AI.motivation: Improve membership inference for LLMs beyond probability-based signals, addressing privacy and copyright concerns by exploiting how gradient-driven representation changes reflect memorization.
AI.method: For a candidate (x,y), perform a single targeted gradient ascent step to increase its loss. Measure changes in internal representations (logits, hidden activations, and projections onto fixed feature directions) before and after the update. Use these drift signals to train a logistic classifier that distinguishes members from non-members.
AI.result: G-Drift substantially outperforms confidence-based, perplexity-based, and reference-based attacks across multiple transformer LLMs and realistic MIA benchmark datasets. Memorized samples show smaller, more structured feature drift. Gradient-induced drift provides a mechanistic link between optimization geometry, representation stability, and memorization.
AI.conclusion: Small, controlled gradient interventions are a practical tool for auditing training-data membership and assessing privacy risks in LLMs, with gradient geometry offering explanatory insight into memorization and potential defenses.

title: Convergence of Byzantine-Resilient Gradient Tracking via Probabilistic Edge Dropout
summary: We study distributed optimization over networks with Byzantine agents that may send arbitrary adversarial messages. We propose \emph{Gradient Tracking with Probabilistic Edge Dropout} (GT-PD), a stochastic gradient tracking method that preserves the convergence properties of gradient tracking under adversarial communication. GT-PD combines two complementary defense layers: a universal self-centered projection that clips each incoming message to a ball of radius $τ$ around the receiving agent, and a fully decentralized probabilistic dropout rule driven by a dual-metric trust score in the decision and tracking channels. This design bounds adversarial perturbations while preserving the doubly stochastic mixing structure, a property often lost under robust aggregation in decentralized settings. Under complete Byzantine isolation ($p_b=0$), GT-PD converges linearly to a neighborhood determined solely by stochastic gradient variance. For partial isolation ($p_b>0$), we introduce \emph{Gradient Tracking with Probabilistic Edge Dropout and Leaky Integration} (GT-PD-L), which uses a leaky integrator to control the accumulation of tracking errors caused by persistent perturbations and achieves linear convergence to a bounded neighborhood determined by the stochastic variance and the clipping-to-leak ratio. We further show that under two-tier dropout with $p_h=1$, isolating Byzantine agents introduces no additional variance into the honest consensus dynamics. Experiments on MNIST under Sign Flip, ALIE, and Inner Product Manipulation attacks show that GT-PD-L outperforms coordinate-wise trimmed mean by up to 4.3 percentage points under stealth attacks.

AI.tldr: A gradient-tracking method with probabilistic edge dropout robust to Byzantine agents, achieving linear convergence to a variance-bound neighborhood; enhanced with leaky integration and two-tier dropout to handle partial isolation; empirically improves robustness against stealth attacks on MNIST over trimmed mean methods.
AI.motivation: To enable reliable distributed optimization in networks where Byzantine agents may send arbitrary adversarial messages, while preserving the doubly stochastic mixing structure and convergence guarantees of gradient tracking.
AI.method: GT-PD: gradient tracking with universal self-centered projection (clipping messages to radius τ) and decentralized probabilistic dropout driven by a dual-metric trust score. GT-PD-L: extends GT-PD with a leaky integrator to bound accumulation of tracking errors under persistent perturbations. Two-tier dropout with p_h=1 isolates Byzantine nodes with no additional variance in honest consensus.
AI.result: If p_b=0 (complete isolation), GT-PD converges linearly to a neighborhood determined by gradient variance. If p_b>0 (partial isolation), GT-PD-L achieves linear convergence to a neighborhood determined by gradient variance and the clipping-leak ratio. Two-tier dropout with p_h=1 yields no extra variance in honest consensus. Empirically, GT-PD-L outperforms coordinate-wise trimmed mean by up to 4.3 percentage points under stealth attacks (MNIST: Sign Flip, ALIE, Inner Product Manipulation).
AI.conclusion: GT-PD and GT-PD-L provide provable robustness for gradient-tracking distributed optimization under Byzantine faults, preserving mixing structure and achieving fast convergence to variance-bound neighborhoods; probabilistic dropout and clipping effectively bound adversarial influence, with leaky integration further stabilizing persistent perturbations.

title: TRiGS: Temporal Rigid-Body Motion for Scalable 4D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Recent 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) methods achieve impressive dynamic scene reconstruction but often rely on piecewise linear velocity approximations and short temporal windows. This disjointed modeling leads to severe temporal fragmentation, forcing primitives to be repeatedly eliminated and regenerated to track complex nonlinear dynamics. This makeshift approximation eliminates the long-term temporal identity of objects and causes an inevitable proliferation of Gaussians, hindering scalability to extended video sequences. To address this, we propose TRiGS, a novel 4D representation that utilizes unified, continuous geometric transformations. By integrating $SE(3)$ transformations, hierarchical Bezier residuals, and learnable local anchors, TRiGS models geometrically consistent rigid motions for individual primitives. This continuous formulation preserves temporal identity and effectively mitigates unbounded memory growth. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TRiGS achieves high fidelity rendering on standard benchmarks while uniquely scaling to extended video sequences (e.g., 600 to 1200 frames) without severe memory bottlenecks, significantly outperforming prior works in temporal stability.

AI.tldr: A continuous SE(3)-based 4D representation (TRiGS) preserves temporal identity and scales to long sequences by unifying geometric transformations, Bezier residuals, and local anchors, addressing memory fragmentation in 4D Gaussian Splatting.
AI.motivation: 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) relies on piecewise linear velocity models and short temporal windows, causing temporal fragmentation, repeated elimination/regeneration of primitives, loss of long-term object identity, and unbounded memory growth when extending sequences.
AI.method: TRiGS employs unified, continuous geometric transformations per primitive using SE(3) transforms, hierarchical Bezier residuals, and learnable local anchors to model geometrically consistent rigid motions, yielding a continuous, identity-preserving representation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show high-fidelity rendering and the ability to scale to extended sequences (e.g., 600–1200 frames) without severe memory bottlenecks, outperforming prior methods in temporal stability.
AI.conclusion: TRiGS provides a stable, memory-efficient, continuous 4D representation that maintains temporal identity over long sequences, enabling scalable dynamic scene reconstruction.

title: Phase space integrity in neural network models of Hamiltonian dynamics: A Lagrangian descriptor approach
summary: We propose Lagrangian Descriptors (LDs) as a diagnostic framework for evaluating neural network models of Hamiltonian systems beyond conventional trajectory-based metrics. Standard error measures quantify short-term predictive accuracy but provide little insight into global geometric structures such as orbits and separatrices. Existing evaluation tools in dissipative systems are inadequate for Hamiltonian dynamics due to fundamental differences in the systems. By constructing probability density functions weighted by LD values, we embed geometric information into a statistical framework suitable for information-theoretic comparison. We benchmark physically constrained architectures (SympNet, HénonNet, Generalized Hamiltonian Neural Networks) against data-driven Reservoir Computing across two canonical systems. For the Duffing oscillator, all models recover the homoclinic orbit geometry with modest data requirements, though their accuracy near critical structures varies. For the three-mode nonlinear Schrödinger equation, however, clear differences emerge: symplectic architectures preserve energy but distort phase-space topology, while Reservoir Computing, despite lacking explicit physical constraints, reproduces the homoclinic structure with high fidelity. These results demonstrate the value of LD-based diagnostics for assessing not only predictive performance but also the global dynamical integrity of learned Hamiltonian models.

AI.tldr: LDs provide a geometry-aware diagnostic for Hamiltonian NN models, using LD-weighted PDFs and information-theoretic metrics to compare physics-constrained nets with reservoir computing across Duffing and a three-mode NLS; reveals energy preservation vs topology fidelity trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Shortcomings of trajectory-based error metrics for Hamiltonian systems; need a diagnostic capturing global phase-space structures (orbits, separatrices) and assess whether learned models preserve dynamical integrity.
AI.method: Introduce Lagrangian Descriptors (LDs) and LD-weighted PDFs as a statistical, geometry-informed evaluation tool. Benchmark physically constrained architectures (SympNet, HénonNet, Generalized Hamiltonian NNs) against data-driven Reservoir Computing on two canonical systems (Duffing oscillator; three-mode nonlinear Schrödinger equation). Analyze global structures (homoclinic orbits) and energy/topology preservation.
AI.result: Duffing: all models recover homoclinic geometry with modest data; varying accuracy near critical structures. Three-mode NLS: symplectic models preserve energy but distort phase-space topology; RC reproduces homoclinic structure with high fidelity despite lacking explicit constraints.
AI.conclusion: LD-based diagnostics effectively assess not only predictive accuracy but also global dynamical integrity of learned Hamiltonian models, exposing trade-offs between physical constraints and data-driven flexibility and guiding model selection and evaluation.

title: Neuropsychiatric Deviations From Normative Profiles: An MRI-Derived Marker for Early Alzheimer's Disease Detection
comment: Accepted and to be presented (ORAL) in ISBI 2026
summary: Neuropsychiatric symptoms (NPS) such as depression and apathy are common in Alzheimer's disease (AD) and often precede cognitive decline. NPS assessments hold promise as early detection markers due to their correlation with disease progression and their non-invasive nature. Yet current tools cannot distinguish whether NPS are part of aging or early signs of AD, limiting their utility. We present a deep learning-based normative modelling framework to identify atypical NPS burden from structural MRI. A 3D convolutional neural network was trained on cognitively stable participants from the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative, learning the mapping between brain anatomy and Neuropsychiatric Inventory Questionnaire (NPIQ) scores. Deviations between predicted and observed scores defined the Divergence from NPIQ scores (DNPI). Higher DNPI was associated with future AD conversion (adjusted OR=2.5; p < 0.01) and achieved predictive accuracy comparable to cerebrospinal fluid AB42 (AUC=0.74 vs 0.75). Our approach supports scalable, non-invasive strategies for early AD detection.

AI.tldr: A deep learning normative modelling framework uses a 3D CNN to map brain structure to NPIQ scores; DNPI deviations predict AD conversion with AUC ~0.74, comparable to CSF AB42.
AI.motivation: NPS are common in AD and may signal disease progression, but current tools can't separate aging from early AD; non-invasive early detection is needed.
AI.method: Train a 3D CNN on cognitively stable ADNI participants to predict NPIQ from MRI; define DNPI as deviation between observed and predicted NPIQ; assess association with conversion and compare to CSF AB42.
AI.result: Higher DNPI associated with future AD conversion (adjusted OR=2.5; p<0.01); AUC 0.74 vs CSF AB42 AUC 0.75.
AI.conclusion: Normative modelling of NPS from structural MRI provides a scalable, non-invasive tool for early AD detection; DNPI can serve as a biomarker for disease progression.

title: The Rashomon Effect for Visualizing High-Dimensional Data
comment: The paper is accepted in AISTATS 2026
summary: Dimension reduction (DR) is inherently non-unique: multiple embeddings can preserve the structure of high-dimensional data equally well while differing in layout or geometry. In this paper, we formally define the Rashomon set for DR -- the collection of `good' embedding -- and show how embracing this multiplicity leads to more powerful and trustworthy representations. Specifically, we pursue three goals. First, we introduce PCA-informed alignment to steer embeddings toward principal components, making axes interpretable without distorting local neighborhoods. Second, we design concept-alignment regularization that aligns an embedding dimension with external knowledge, such as class labels or user-defined concepts. Third, we propose a method to extract common knowledge across the Rashomon set by identifying trustworthy and persistent nearest-neighbor relationships, which we use to construct refined embeddings with improved local structure while preserving global relationships. By moving beyond a single embedding and leveraging the Rashomon set, we provide a flexible framework for building interpretable, robust, and goal-aligned visualizations.

AI.tldr: A Rashomon-set-based framework for dimensionality reduction that yields multiple good embeddings and combines PCA-informed alignment, concept-alignment regularization, and persistent-neighbor extraction to produce interpretable, robust, and goal-aligned visualizations.
AI.motivation: DR is inherently non-unique; many embeddings can preserve structure similarly well. Exploiting this multiplicity can improve interpretability, robustness, and integration of external knowledge in low-dimensional representations.
AI.method: 1) Formalize the Rashomon set for DR as the collection of good embeddings; 2) PCA-informed alignment to bias embeddings toward principal components without harming local neighborhood structure; 3) concept-alignment regularization to align a latent dimension with external knowledge such as class labels or user-defined concepts; 4) extract common knowledge across the Rashomon set by identifying trustworthy and persistent nearest-neighbor relationships to construct refined embeddings that preserve global structure while improving local relationships.
AI.result: Proposes a flexible framework and concrete components (alignment, regularization, and cross-embedding knowledge extraction) to produce interpretable, robust DR visualizations. The abstract emphasizes framework development rather than reported empirical results.
AI.conclusion: Embracing the Rashomon set enables leveraging multiple good embeddings to build interpretable, robust, and goal-aligned dimensionality-reduction visualizations, moving beyond reliance on a single embedding.

title: Reliev3R: Relieving Feed-forward Reconstruction from Multi-View Geometric Annotations
summary: With recent advances, Feed-forward Reconstruction Models (FFRMs) have demonstrated great potential in reconstruction quality and adaptiveness to multiple downstream tasks. However, the excessive reliance on multi-view geometric annotations, e.g. 3D point maps and camera poses, makes the fully-supervised training scheme of FFRMs difficult to scale up. In this paper, we propose Reliev3R, a weakly-supervised paradigm for training FFRMs from scratch without cost-prohibitive multi-view geometric annotations. Relieving the reliance on geometric sensory data and compute-exhaustive structure-from-motion preprocessing, our method draws 3D knowledge directly from monocular relative depths and image sparse correspondences given by zero-shot predictions of pretrained models. At the core of Reliev3R, we design an ambiguity-aware relative depth loss and a trigonometry-based reprojection loss to facilitate supervision for multi-view geometric consistency. Training from scratch with the less data, Reliev3R catches up with its fully-supervised sibling models, taking a step towards low-cost 3D reconstruction supervisions and scalable FFRMs.

AI.tldr: Reliev3R introduces a weakly-supervised framework for training feed-forward reconstruction models from scratch without multi-view geometry annotations, using monocular relative depths and sparse correspondences from zero-shot pretrained models; it introduces ambiguity-aware relative depth loss and a trig-reprojection loss to enforce multi-view consistency, achieving competitive performance with less data.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on costly 3D annotations and structure-from-motion preprocessing to scale up training of FFRMs for 3D reconstruction.
AI.method: Leverage monocular relative depths and sparse image correspondences derived from zero-shot pretrained models. Propose two losses: (1) ambiguity-aware relative depth loss to cope with depth uncertainty; (2) trigonometry-based reprojection loss to enforce multi-view geometric consistency. Train from scratch with limited data.
AI.result: Reliev3R closes the gap with fully-supervised models despite reduced data, demonstrating feasibility of low-cost supervision for scalable FFRMs.
AI.conclusion: Weakly-supervised learning via the proposed losses enables scalable 3D reconstruction models without heavy multi-view annotations, offering a practical pathway to reduce annotation cost while maintaining performance.

title: Scheduling LLM Inference with Uncertainty-Aware Output Length Predictions
summary: To schedule LLM inference, the \textit{shortest job first} (SJF) principle is favorable by prioritizing requests with short output lengths to avoid head-of-line (HOL) blocking. Existing methods usually predict a single output length for each request to facilitate scheduling. We argue that such a \textit{point estimate} does not match the \textit{stochastic} decoding process of LLM inference, where output length is \textit{uncertain} by nature and determined by when the end-of-sequence (EOS) token is sampled. Hence, the output length of each request should be fitted with a distribution rather than a single value. With an in-depth analysis of empirical data and the stochastic decoding process, we observe that output length follows a heavy-tailed distribution and can be fitted with the log-t distribution. On this basis, we propose a simple metric called Tail Inflated Expectation (TIE) to replace the output length in SJF scheduling, which adjusts the expectation of a log-t distribution with its tail probabilities to account for the risk that a request generates long outputs. To evaluate our TIE scheduler, we compare it with three strong baselines, and the results show that TIE reduces the per-token latency by $2.31\times$ for online inference and improves throughput by $1.42\times$ for offline data generation.

AI.tldr: Model output length as a log-t distribution and use Tail Inflated Expectation (TIE) to guide SJF-based scheduling for LLM inference, yielding substantial latency and throughput gains.
AI.motivation: Point estimates for output length fail to capture the stochastic, heavy-tailed nature of end-of-sequence in LLM decoding; SJF on such estimates leads to HOL blocking and suboptimal performance.
AI.method: Empirically fit output lengths with a log-t distribution; define TIE as the expected length adjusted by tail probabilities; integrate into a scheduling policy replacing raw length in SJF; compare against three strong baselines on online/offline tasks.
AI.result: TIE reduces per-token latency by 2.31x for online inference and increases throughput by 1.42x for offline data generation over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Treating decoding length as a distribution and leveraging tail-aware metrics improves LLM inference scheduling; TIE is a simple, effective enhancement to SJF for stochastic decoding.

title: TF-SSD: A Strong Pipeline via Synergic Mask Filter for Training-free Co-salient Object Detection
summary: Co-salient Object Detection (CoSOD) aims to segment salient objects that consistently appear across a group of related images. Despite the notable progress achieved by recent training-based approaches, they still remain constrained by the closed-set datasets and exhibit limited generalization. However, few studies explore the potential of Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) to address CoSOD, which demonstrate a strong generalized ability and robust saliency understanding. In this paper, we investigate and leverage VFMs for CoSOD, and further propose a novel training-free method, TF-SSD, through the synergy between SAM and DINO. Specifically, we first utilize SAM to generate comprehensive raw proposals, which serve as a candidate mask pool. Then, we introduce a quality mask generator to filter out redundant masks, thereby acquiring a refined mask set. Since this generator is built upon SAM, it inherently lacks semantic understanding of saliency. To this end, we adopt an intra-image saliency filter that employs DINO's attention maps to identify visually salient masks within individual images. Moreover, to extend saliency understanding across group images, we propose an inter-image prototype selector, which computes similarity scores among cross-image prototypes to select masks with the highest score. These selected masks serve as final predictions for CoSOD. Extensive experiments show that our TF-SSD outperforms existing methods (e.g., 13.7\% gains over the recent training-free method). Codes are available at https://github.com/hzz-yy/TF-SSD.

AI.tldr: A training-free CoSOD approach (TF-SSD) that leverages vision foundation models by using SAM-generated proposals and DINO-based saliency cues, with intra-image filtering and inter-image prototype selection to produce co-salient masks without training; achieves strong generalization and outperforms prior training-free methods.
AI.motivation: Co-salient object detection (CoSOD) is hampered by closed-set datasets and limited generalization. Vision foundation models (VFMs) like SAM and DINO offer broad saliency understanding and generalization. The work explores training-free TF-SSD to harness VFMs for CoSOD.
AI.method: 1) Generate a comprehensive pool of candidate masks from SAM proposals. 2) Apply a quality mask generator to filter redundant masks. 3) Use an intra-image saliency filter based on DINO attention maps to identify visually salient masks within each image. 4) Apply an inter-image prototype selector to compute cross-image prototype similarities and select masks with the highest scores across the image group. The resulting masks are the CoSOD predictions. This is a training-free pipeline that synergizes SAM and DINO.
AI.result: TF-SSD achieves strong performance, outperforming existing methods, including a reported 13.7% gain over the recent training-free method. Extensive experiments validate effectiveness, and the authors provide code at the referenced GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: Training-free TF-SSD demonstrates that Vision Foundation Models can be effectively leveraged for CoSOD without further training, achieving robust cross-image saliency understanding through intra- and inter-image filtering of SAM/DINO-derived cues.

title: Towards Initialization-dependent and Non-vacuous Generalization Bounds for Overparameterized Shallow Neural Networks
summary: Overparameterized neural networks often show a benign overfitting property in the sense of achieving excellent generalization behavior despite the number of parameters exceeding the number of training examples. A promising direction to explain benign overfitting is to relate generalization to the norm of distance from initialization, motivated by the empirical observations that this distance is often significantly smaller than the norm itself. However, the existing initialization-dependent complexity analyses cannot fully exploit the power of initialization since the associated bounds depend on the spectral norm of the initialization matrix, which can scale as a square-root function of the width and are therefore not effective for overparameterized models. In this paper, we develop the first \emph{fully} initialization-dependent complexity bounds for shallow neural networks with general Lipschitz activation functions, which enjoys a logarithmic dependency on the width. Our bounds depend on the path-norm of the distance from initialization, which are derived by introducing a new peeling technique to handle the challenge along with the initialization-dependent constraint. We also develop a lower bound tight up to a constant factor. Finally, we conduct empirical comparisons and show that our generalization analysis implies non-vacuous bounds for overparameterized networks.

AI.tldr: Fully initialization-dependent generalization bounds for shallow nets with Lipschitz activations; introduces path-norm of distance from initialization and a peeling technique; achieves logarithmic width-dependency and tight lower bound; empirically non-vacuous bounds in overparameterized regimes.
AI.motivation: Explain benign overfitting by tying generalization to the distance from initialization, addressing the limitation of spectral-norm scaling with width in existing bounds.
AI.method: Develop a new initialization-dependent complexity framework using a peeling technique to bound the path-norm distance from initialization; extend to shallow networks with general Lipschitz activations; derive a lower bound and validate with experiments.
AI.result: First fully initialization-dependent complexity bounds with logarithmic width dependency; bounds based on the path-norm of distance from initialization; tight lower bound (up to constants); empirical results show non-vacuous generalization bounds for overparameterized networks.
AI.conclusion: Initialization-dependent analyses via the distance-from-initialization (path-norm) can capture generalization behavior in overparameterized networks; supports benign overfitting explanations and offers practical, non-vacuous bounds with empirical support.

title: STAR: Mitigating Cascading Errors in Spatial Reasoning via Turn-point Alignment and Segment-level DPO
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables, Accepted by ICME 2026
summary: Structured spatial navigation is a core benchmark for Large Language Models (LLMs) spatial reasoning. Existing paradigms like Visualization-of-Thought (VoT) are prone to cascading errors in complex topologies. To solve this, we propose STAR, a two-stage framework grounded on topological anchors, and introduce the RedMaze-23K dataset with human-inspired turnpoint annotations. The first stage uses supervised fine-tuning to help models internalize spatial semantics and prune redundant paths. The second adopts Spatial-aware Segment-level Direct Preference Optimization (SDPO) to refine self-correction in long-horizon navigation. Experiments show STAR achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models: its 32B variant outperforms DeepSeek-V3 (29.27% vs. 25.00%) and reaches 82.4% of GPT-4's performance.

AI.tldr: STAR: a two-stage topological navigation framework with SDPO and RedMaze-23K, achieving open-source state-of-the-art performance and nearing GPT-4 results.
AI.motivation: Structured spatial navigation is a key benchmark for LLM spatial reasoning, but existing approaches like Visualization-of-Thought (VoT) incur cascading errors in complex topologies; there is a need for robust, long-horizon planning and better datasets.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) supervised fine-tuning to internalize spatial semantics and prune redundant paths guided by topological anchors; (2) Spatial-aware Segment-level Direct Preference Optimization (SDPO) to refine self-correction in long-horizon navigation. Introduces RedMaze-23K with human-inspired turnpoint annotations, and leverages an anchor-based topology to support robust planning.
AI.result: STAR (32B) achieves state-of-the-art among open-source models, surpassing DeepSeek-V3 (29.27% vs. 25.00%) and reaching about 82.4% of GPT-4’s performance on the evaluation task.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage, topological-anchor framework with SDPO and a dedicated RedMaze-23K dataset markedly improves structured spatial navigation, mitigating cascading errors and narrowing the gap to strong closed-source models.

title: A Decoupled Basis-Vector-Driven Generative Framework for Dynamic Multi-Objective Optimization
summary: Dynamic multi-objective optimization requires continuous tracking of moving Pareto fronts. Existing methods struggle with irregular mutations and data sparsity, primarily facing three challenges: the non-linear coupling of dynamic modes, negative transfer from outdated historical data, and the cold-start problem during environmental switches. To address these issues, this paper proposes a decoupled basis-vector-driven generative framework (DB-GEN). First, to resolve non-linear coupling, the framework employs the discrete wavelet transform to separate evolutionary trajectories into low-frequency trends and high-frequency details. Second, to mitigate negative transfer, it learns transferable basis vectors via sparse dictionary learning rather than directly memorizing historical instances. Recomposing these bases under a topology-aware contrastive constraint constructs a structured latent manifold. Finally, to overcome the cold-start problem, a surrogate-assisted search paradigm samples initial populations from this manifold. Pre-trained on 120 million solutions, DB-GEN performs direct online inference without retraining or fine-tuning. This zero-shot generation process executes in milliseconds, requiring approximately 0.2 seconds per environmental change. Experimental results demonstrate that DB-GEN improves tracking accuracy across various dynamic benchmarks compared to existing algorithms.

AI.tldr: A zero-shot, decoupled, basis-vector framework (DB-GEN) for dynamic MO optimization that uses wavelet-based decoupling, sparse dictionary-learned transferable bases, and topology-aware contrastive learning to construct a structured latent manifold, enabling fast surrogate-assisted, online inference without retraining.
AI.motivation: Address three core problems in dynamic MO: (i) non-linear coupling of dynamic modes causing poor adaptation, (ii) negative transfer from stale historical data, and (iii) cold-start during environmental switches.
AI.method: 1) Apply discrete wavelet transform to split trajectories into low-frequency trends and high-frequency details to decouple dynamics. 2) Learn transferable basis vectors via sparse dictionary learning instead of memorizing data. 3) Recompose bases under a topology-aware contrastive constraint to form a structured latent manifold. 4) Use a surrogate-assisted search to sample initial populations from the manifold. 5) Zero-shot online inference, pretrained on 120M solutions, enabling ~0.2s per environmental change without retraining.
AI.result: DB-GEN yields improved tracking accuracy on diverse dynamic benchmarks compared with existing algorithms, and supports direct online inference in milliseconds without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling evolutionary dynamics via wavelet-based decomposition and learning transferable bases with topology-aware constraints yields a structured latent manifold from which fast, robust initialization can be drawn for dynamic MO optimization; the model achieves near-instantaneous adaptation (0.2s per change) and avoids negative transfer and cold-start issues through zero-shot, pre-trained generation.

title: FecalFed: Privacy-Preserving Poultry Disease Detection via Federated Learning
comment: Accepted to the CVPR 2026 Workshop on Vision for Agriculture
summary: Early detection of highly pathogenic avian influenza (HPAI) and endemic poultry diseases is critical for global food security. While computer vision models excel at classifying diseases from fecal imaging, deploying these systems at scale is bottlenecked by farm data privacy concerns and institutional data silos. Furthermore, existing open-source agricultural datasets frequently suffer from severe, undocumented data contamination. In this paper, we introduce $\textbf{FecalFed}$, a privacy-preserving federated learning framework for poultry disease classification. We first curate and release $\texttt{poultry-fecal-fl}$, a rigorously deduplicated dataset of 8,770 unique images across four disease classes, revealing and eliminating a 46.89$\%$ duplication rate in popular public repositories. To simulate realistic agricultural environments, we evaluate FecalFed under highly heterogeneous, non-IID conditions (Dirichlet $α=0.5$). While isolated single-farm training collapses under this data heterogeneity, yielding only 64.86$\%$ accuracy, our federated approach recovers performance without centralizing sensitive data. Specifically, utilizing server-side adaptive optimization (FedAdam) with a Swin-Small architecture achieves 90.31$\%$ accuracy, closely approaching the centralized upper bound of 95.10\%. Furthermore, we demonstrate that an edge-optimized Swin-Tiny model maintains highly competitive performance at 89.74$\%$, establishing a highly efficient, privacy-first blueprint for on-farm avian disease monitoring.

AI.tldr: Federated, privacy-preserving poultry disease classification using deduplicated fecal image dataset; FedAdam with Swin-Small nearly matches centralized performance under non-IID conditions; edge Swin-Tiny offers efficient, competitive results.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, privacy-preserving poultry disease classification in farms, addressing data privacy, silos, and data contamination in open agricultural datasets.
AI.method: Create FecalFed framework; curate and deduplicate poultry-fecal-fl dataset (8,770 images, 4 classes); simulate non-IID farm data (Dirichlet α=0.5); train with server-side adaptive optimization (FedAdam) using Swin-Small; also evaluate an edge-optimized Swin-Tiny model; compare with centralized upper bound.
AI.result: Single-farm training collapses under heterogeneity (64.86%); FedAdam + Swin-Small achieves 90.31% accuracy, near centralized 95.10% upper bound; Swin-Tiny edge model achieves 89.74% accuracy.
AI.conclusion: FecalFed delivers a privacy-first, scalable framework for on-farm avian disease monitoring, demonstrates substantial gains under non-IID data thanks to federated optimization, and highlights the importance of deduplication for data quality.

title: MOON3.0: Reasoning-aware Multimodal Representation Learning for E-commerce Product Understanding
summary: With the rapid growth of e-commerce, exploring general representations rather than task-specific ones has attracted increasing attention. Although recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have driven significant progress in product understanding, they are typically employed as feature extractors that implicitly encode product information into global embeddings, thereby limiting their ability to capture fine-grained attributes. Therefore, we argue that leveraging the reasoning capabilities of MLLMs to explicitly model fine-grained product attributes holds significant potential. Nevertheless, achieving this goal remains non-trivial due to several key challenges: (i) long-context reasoning tends to dilute the model's attention to salient information in the raw input; (ii) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) primarily encourages rigid imitation, limiting the exploration of effective reasoning strategies; and (iii) fine-grained details are progressively attenuated during forward propagation. To address these issues, we propose MOON3.0, the first reasoning-aware MLLM-based model for product representation learning. Our method (1) employs a multi-head modality fusion module to adaptively integrate raw signals; (2) incorporates a joint contrastive and reinforcement learning framework to autonomously explore more effective reasoning strategies; and (3) introduces a fine-grained residual enhancement module to progressively preserve local details throughout the network. Additionally, we release a large-scale multimodal e-commerce benchmark MBE3.0. Experimentally, our model demonstrates state-of-the-art zero-shot performance across various downstream tasks on both our benchmark and public datasets.

AI.tldr: MOON3.0: a reasoning-aware multimodal LLM for product representation that uses multi-head modality fusion, joint contrastive + RL learning, and a fine-grained residual module, plus MBE3.0 benchmark; achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot results.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of current MLLMs that produce only global embeddings and to enable explicit reasoning for fine-grained product attributes, addressing long-context distraction, over-guided SFT, and attenuation of details.
AI.method: Introduce three components: (1) multi-head modality fusion to adaptively fuse raw signals; (2) joint contrastive and reinforcement learning to explore effective reasoning strategies; (3) fine-grained residual enhancement to preserve local details through propagation; release MBE3.0 benchmark.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on multiple downstream tasks on MBE3.0 and public datasets.
AI.conclusion: MOON3.0 demonstrates the viability of reasoning-aware MLLMs for comprehensive, fine-grained product representations and offers a valuable benchmark for future research.

title: HarassGuard: Detecting Harassment Behaviors in Social Virtual Reality with Vision-Language Models
comment: To appear in the 2026 TVCG Special Issue on the 2026 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces (VR)
summary: Social Virtual Reality (VR) platforms provide immersive social experiences but also expose users to serious risks of online harassment. Existing safety measures are largely reactive, while proactive solutions that detect harassment behavior during an incident often depend on sensitive biometric data, raising privacy concerns. In this paper, we present HarassGuard, a vision-language model (VLM) based system that detects physical harassment in social VR using only visual input. We construct an IRB-approved harassment vision dataset, apply prompt engineering, and fine-tune VLMs to detect harassment behavior by considering contextual information in social VR. Experimental results demonstrate that HarassGuard achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines (i.e., LSTM/CNN, Transformer), reaching an accuracy of up to 88.09% in binary classification and 68.85% in multi-class classification. Notably, HarassGuard matches these baselines while using significantly fewer fine-tuning samples (200 vs. 1,115), offering unique advantages in contextual reasoning and privacy-preserving detection.

AI.tldr: HarassGuard is a vision-language model-based system that detects physical harassment in social VR from visual input, achieving up to 88.09% binary accuracy and 68.85% multiclass accuracy, with significantly fewer fine-tuning samples (200 vs 1,115).
AI.motivation: Safety in social VR platforms is hampered by reactive measures and privacy concerns when using biometric data; a privacy-preserving, context-aware solution is needed to detect harassment during incidents.
AI.method: Construct an IRB-approved harassment vision dataset; apply prompt engineering; fine-tune vision-language models to detect harassment using visual input plus contextual VR cues.
AI.result: HarassGuard achieves competitive performance compared to LSTM/CNN and Transformer baselines, with binary accuracy up to 88.09% and multiclass accuracy up to 68.85%, using far fewer fine-tuning samples.
AI.conclusion: HarassGuard offers effective, privacy-preserving harassment detection in social VR with strong contextual reasoning, achieving competitive results while reducing data requirements.

title: Lipschitz Dueling Bandits over Continuous Action Spaces
summary: We study for the first time, stochastic dueling bandits over continuous action spaces with Lipschitz structure, where feedback is purely comparative. While dueling bandits and Lipschitz bandits have been studied separately, their combination has remained unexplored. We propose the first algorithm for Lipschitz dueling bandits, using round-based exploration and recursive region elimination guided by an adaptive reference arm. We develop new analytical tools for relative feedback and prove a regret bound of $\tilde O\left(T^{\frac{d_z+1}{d_z+2}}\right)$, where $d_z$ is the zooming dimension of the near-optimal region. Further, our algorithm takes only logarithmic space in terms of the total time horizon, best achievable by any bandit algorithm over a continuous action space.

AI.tldr: First algorithm for Lipschitz dueling bandits on continuous action spaces; achieves sublinear regret with a zooming-dimension dependent exponent and logarithmic space usage.
AI.motivation: Combines dueling bandits (purely comparative feedback) with Lipschitz bandits (continuous action spaces with smoothness). This enables efficient learning in continuous-action settings where only comparisons are available.
AI.method: Round-based exploration with recursive region elimination guided by an adaptive reference arm; introduces new analytical tools for relative feedback; leverages Lipschitz structure to transfer information across actions.
AI.result: Regret bound: ~O(T^{(d_z+1)/(d_z+2)}) where d_z is the zooming dimension of the near-optimal region. The algorithm uses logarithmic space in the total time horizon.
AI.conclusion: Presents the first integration of Lipschitz and dueling bandits; establishes sublinear regret with a dimension-dependent exponent and ensures space efficiency.

title: Towards Viewpoint-Robust End-to-End Autonomous Driving with 3D Foundation Model Priors
comment: Accepted at CVPR Workshop on Simulation for Autonomous Driving 2026
summary: Robust trajectory planning under camera viewpoint changes is important for scalable end-to-end autonomous driving. However, existing models often depend heavily on the camera viewpoints seen during training. We investigate an augmentation-free approach that leverages geometric priors from a 3D foundation model. The method injects per-pixel 3D positions derived from depth estimates as positional embeddings and fuses intermediate geometric features through cross-attention. Experiments on the VR-Drive camera viewpoint perturbation benchmark show reduced performance degradation under most perturbation conditions, with clear improvements under pitch and height perturbations. Gains under longitudinal translation are smaller, suggesting that more viewpoint-agnostic integration is needed for robustness to camera viewpoint changes.

AI.tldr: An augmentation-free trajectory planning method uses depth-derived 3D positional embeddings and cross-attention-based fusion of intermediate geometric features from a 3D foundation model to improve robustness to camera viewpoint changes, with notable gains for pitch and height perturbations on VR-Drive, but limited improvements for longitudinal translation.
AI.motivation: Reduce sensitivity to camera viewpoint changes in end-to-end autonomous driving without data augmentation by leveraging geometric priors from a 3D foundation model.
AI.method: Extract per-pixel 3D positions from depth estimates to form positional embeddings; fuse intermediate geometric features via cross-attention; integrate geometry priors from a 3D foundation model in an augmentation-free setting.
AI.result: On the VR-Drive viewpoint perturbation benchmark, the method reduces performance degradation under most perturbations, with clear improvements for pitch and height perturbations; improvements for longitudinal translation are smaller.
AI.conclusion: Geometric priors from a 3D foundation model can enhance robustness to camera viewpoint changes, but further work is needed to achieve full viewpoint-agnostic robustness across perturbation types.

title: MF-QAT: Multi-Format Quantization-Aware Training for Elastic Inference
summary: Quantization-aware training (QAT) is typically performed for a single target numeric format, while practical deployments often need to choose numerical precision at inference time based on hardware support or runtime constraints. We study multi-format QAT, where a single model is trained to be robust across multiple quantization formats. We find that multi-format QAT can match single-format QAT at each target precision, yielding one model that performs well overall across different formats, even formats that were not seen during training. To enable practical deployment, we propose the Slice-and-Scale conversion procedure for both MXINT and MXFP that converts a high-precision representation into lower-precision formats without re-training. Building on this, we introduce a pipeline that (i) trains a model with multi-format QAT, (ii) stores a single anchor format checkpoint (MXINT8/MXFP8), and (iii) allows on-the-fly conversion to lower MXINT or MXFP formats at runtime with negligible-or no-additional accuracy degradation. Together, these components provide a practical path to elastic precision scaling and allow selecting the runtime format at inference time across diverse deployment targets.

AI.tldr: Multi-format QAT trains a single model to perform well across multiple quantization formats, enabling post-training runtime format selection via a Slice-and-Scale conversion for MXINT and MXFP.
AI.motivation: Practical deployments require flexible precision due to diverse hardware support and runtime constraints; single-format QAT is insufficient for broad deployment.
AI.method: Train a model with multi-format QAT; develop Slice-and-Scale conversion for MXINT and MXFP to map high-precision representations to lower-precision formats without retraining; store a single anchor format checkpoint (MXINT8/MXFP8); enable on-the-fly conversion to lower MXINT or MXFP at inference.
AI.result: Multi-format QAT achieves comparable accuracy to single-format QAT at each target precision, including unseen formats; the conversion pipeline maintains accuracy with negligible or no degradation and requires no retraining.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical path to elastic precision scaling with runtime format selection, reducing retraining needs and enabling deployment across diverse hardware targets.

title: KG-CMI: Knowledge graph enhanced cross-Mamba interaction for medical visual question answering
summary: Medical visual question answering (Med-VQA) is a crucial multimodal task in clinical decision support and telemedicine. Recent methods fail to fully leverage domain-specific medical knowledge, making it difficult to accurately associate lesion features in medical images with key diagnostic criteria. Additionally, classification-based approaches typically rely on predefined answer sets. Treating Med-VQA as a simple classification problem limits its ability to adapt to the diversity of free-form answers and may overlook detailed semantic information in those answers. To address these challenges, we propose a knowledge graph enhanced cross-Mamba interaction (KG-CMI) framework, which consists of a fine-grained cross-modal feature alignment (FCFA) module, a knowledge graph embedding (KGE) module, a cross-modal interaction representation (CMIR) module, and a free-form answer enhanced multi-task learning (FAMT) module. The KG-CMI learns cross-modal feature representations for images and texts by effectively integrating professional medical knowledge through a graph, establishing associations between lesion features and disease knowledge. Moreover, FAMT leverages auxiliary knowledge from open-ended questions, improving the model's capability for open-ended Med-VQA. Experimental results demonstrate that KG-CMI outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on three Med-VQA datasets, i.e., VQA-RAD, SLAKE, and OVQA. Additionally, we conduct interpretability experiments to further validate the framework's effectiveness.

AI.tldr: KG-CMI is a knowledge-graph enhanced cross-modal Med-VQA framework that combines fine-grained cross-modal alignment, knowledge graph embeddings, cross-modal interaction representations, and free-form answer–aware multi-task learning to improve open-ended Med-VQA performance and interpretability; it achieves state-of-the-art on VQA-RAD, SLAKE, and OVQA.
AI.motivation: Current Med-VQA methods underutilize domain-specific medical knowledge and often rely on predefined answer sets, limiting the ability to capture rich semantic details and to adapt to open-ended responses; there is a need to better align lesion features with disease knowledge through explicit knowledge graphs.
AI.method: Propose KG-CMI with four components: (1) FCFA for fine-grained cross-modal feature alignment between images and text; (2) KGE module to embed medical knowledge graphs; (3) CMIR for cross-modal interaction representation; (4) FAMT for free-form answer enhanced multi-task learning that leverages auxiliary knowledge from open-ended questions; evaluated on three Med-VQA datasets (VQA-RAD, SLAKE, OVQA) with interpretability analyses.
AI.result: KG-CMI outperforms state-of-the-art Med-VQA methods on VQA-RAD, SLAKE, and OVQA and is supported by interpretability experiments validating the framework.
AI.conclusion: Integrating knowledge graphs with cross-modal feature learning and free-form answer modeling yields improved accuracy and interpretability for open-ended Med-VQA, facilitating better connections between image lesions and diagnostic knowledge.

title: Learning Shared Representations for Multi-Task Linear Bandits
summary: Multi-task representation learning is an approach that learns shared latent representations across related tasks, facilitating knowledge transfer and improving sample efficiency. This paper introduces a novel approach to multi-task representation learning in linear bandits. We consider a setting with T concurrent linear bandit tasks, each with feature dimension d, that share a common latent representation of dimension r \ll min{d,T}$, capturing their underlying relatedness. We propose a new Optimism in the Face of Uncertainty Linear (OFUL) algorithm that leverages shared low-rank representations to enhance decision-making in a sample-efficient manner. Our algorithm first collects data through an exploration phase, estimates the shared model via spectral initialization, and then conducts OFUL based learning over a newly constructed confidence set. We provide theoretical guarantees for the confidence set and prove that the unknown reward vectors lie within the confidence set with high probability. We derive cumulative regret bounds and show that the proposed approach achieves \tilde{O}(\sqrt{drNT}), a significant improvement over solving the T tasks independently, resulting in a regret of \tilde{O}(dT\sqrt{N}). We performed numerical simulations to validate the performance of our algorithm for different problem sizes.

AI.tldr: Proposes a multi-task linear bandit framework exploiting a shared low-rank latent representation across T tasks. Introduces an Optimism in the Face of Uncertainty Linear (OFUL) algorithm with spectral initialization and a tailored confidence set, achieving improved regret of ~O~(sqrt(d r N T)) compared to solving tasks independently (~O~(d T sqrt(N))). Validates with simulations.
AI.motivation: To improve sample efficiency in multi-task linear bandits by leveraging common latent structure across related tasks, reducing effective ambient dimension from dT to dr.
AI.method: Assume T tasks with feature dimension d share a latent representation of rank r. Use an exploration phase to collect data, perform spectral initialization to estimate the shared model, and then apply OFUL within a confidence set constructed for the low-rank representation. Provide high-probability containment of reward vectors and derive cumulative regret bounds ~O~(sqrt(d r N T)).
AI.result: The proposed algorithm achieves a regret bound of ~O~(sqrt(d r N T)) (up to logarithmic factors), significantly better than independent task solutions ~O~(d T sqrt(N)). Numerical simulations corroborate the theoretical gains across varying problem sizes.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting shared low-rank representations across related bandit tasks yields substantial sample efficiency and regret reduction. The approach integrates spectral initialization with OFUL in a structured latent space and suggests extensions to more general multi-task settings or non-linear representations.

title: Fluently Lying: Adversarial Robustness Can Be Substrate-Dependent
summary: The primary tools used to monitor and defend object detectors under adversarial attack assume that when accuracy degrades, detection count drops in tandem. This coupling was assumed, not measured. We report a counterexample observed on a single model: under standard PGD, EMS-YOLO, a spiking neural network (SNN) object detector, retains more than 70% of its detections while mAP collapses from 0.528 to 0.042. We term this count-preserving accuracy collapse Quality Corruption (QC), to distinguish it from the suppression that dominates untargeted evaluation. Across four SNN architectures and two threat models (l-infinity and l-2), QC appears only in one of the four detectors tested (EMS-YOLO). On this model, all five standard defense components fail to detect or mitigate QC, suggesting the defense ecosystem may rely on a shared assumption calibrated on a single substrate. These results provide, to our knowledge, the first evidence that adversarial failure modes can be substrate-dependent.

AI.tldr: Under adversarial PGD, EMS-YOLO SNN detector shows quality corruption: high detection persistence with drastic mAP collapse; reveals substrate-dependent failure and defense limitations; only one of four detectors shows QC; defenses fail to detect/mitigate.
AI.motivation: Question the common coupling assumption between accuracy and detection count in adversarial defenses and test across SNN detectors and threat models.
AI.method: Empirical study across four SNN architectures and two threat models (l-infinity, l-2); apply standard PGD; measure mAP and detection counts; test five defenses.
AI.result: Observed a case where more than 70% of detections survive while mAP drops from 0.528 to 0.042; QC appears in EMS-YOLO only; defenses fail to detect or mitigate QC.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial failure modes can be substrate-dependent; defense ecosystems relying on accuracy-count coupling may be insufficient; need broader substrate-aware evaluation and defense design.

title: Learning from Many and Adapting to the Unknown in Open-set Test Streams
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) generalize across tasks via reusable representations and flexible reasoning, yet remain brittle in real deployment under evolving tasks and continual distribution shift. A common approach is Test-Time Adaptation (TTA), existing ones of which updates models with hand-designed unsupervised objectives over the full parameter space and mostly overlook preserving shared source knowledge and the reliability of adaptation signals. Drawing on molecular signaling cascades of memory updating in Drosophila, we propose Synapse Consolidation (SyCo), a parameter-efficient LLM adaptation method that updates low-rank adapters through Rac1 and MAPK pathways under the guidance of a structured TTA objective driven by problem understanding, process understanding, and source-domain guardrail. Rac1 confines plasticity to a tail-gradient subspace that is less critical for source knowledge, enabling rapid specialization while preserving source representations. MAPK uses a tiered controller to suppress noisy updates and consolidate useful adaptations under non-stationary streams. To model real deployments with multiple sources and continually emerging tasks, we introduce Multi-source Open-set Adaptation (MOA) setting, where a model is trained on multiple labeled source tasks and then adapts on open, non-stationary unlabeled test streams that mix seen and unseen tasks with partial overlap in label and intent space. Across 18 NLP datasets and the MOA setting, SyCo consistently outperforms strong baselines, achieving 78.31\% on unseen-task adaptation and 85.37\% on unseen-data shifts.

AI.tldr: SyCo is a parameter-efficient TTA method for LLMs that updates low-rank adapters using Rac1 and MAPK pathways to confine plasticity and suppress noisy updates, enabling robust adaptation in a Multi-source Open-set Adaptation (MOA) setting and achieving strong unseen-task and unseen-data performance across 18 NLP datasets.
AI.motivation: LLMs remain brittle under continual distribution shift; existing TTA methods typically update all parameters with unsupervised objectives and neglect preserving source knowledge and reliable adaptation signals. A parameter-efficient, signaling-guided adaptation framework is needed to handle non-stationary, multi-source deployment.
AI.method: Update low-rank adapters via Rac1 to restrict plasticity to a tail-gradient subspace, and use MAPK with a tiered controller to suppress noisy updates and consolidate useful changes. Guide the adaptation with a structured TTA objective emphasizing problem understanding, process understanding, and a source-domain guardrail. Evaluate in MOA: train on multiple labeled sources, adapt on open unlabeled streams with seen/unseen tasks and partial label/intent overlap.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over strong baselines across 18 NLP datasets in the MOA setting, with 78.31% unseen-task adaptation and 85.37% unseen-data shifts.
AI.conclusion: SyCo provides a compact, robust TTA approach that preserves source knowledge while enabling rapid, reliable adaptation to evolving tasks and data streams; the MOA results demonstrate its effectiveness in realistic, non-stationary multi-source environments.

title: TALENT: Target-aware Efficient Tuning for Referring Image Segmentation
summary: Referring image segmentation aims to segment specific targets based on a natural text expression. Recently, parameter-efficient tuning (PET) has emerged as a promising paradigm. However, existing PET-based methods often suffer from the fact that visual features can't emphasize the text-referred target instance but activate co-category yet unrelated objects. We analyze and quantify this problem, terming it the `non-target activation' (NTA) issue. To address this, we propose a novel framework, TALENT, which utilizes target-aware efficient tuning for PET-based RIS. Specifically, we first propose a Rectified Cost Aggregator (RCA) to efficiently aggregate text-referred features. Then, to calibrate `NTA' into accurate target activation, we adopt a Target-aware Learning Mechanism (TLM), including contextual pairwise consistency learning and target-centric contrastive learning. The former uses the sentence-level text feature to achieve a holistic understanding of the referent and constructs a text-referred affinity map to optimize the semantic association of visual features. The latter further enhances target localization to discover the distinct instance while suppressing associations with other unrelated ones. The two objectives work in concert and address `NTA' effectively. Extensive evaluations show that TALENT outperforms existing methods across various metrics (e.g., 2.5\% mIoU gains on G-Ref val set). Our codes will be released at: https://github.com/Kimsure/TALENT.

AI.tldr: Proposes TALENT, a target-aware PET framework for referring image segmentation that mitigates non-target activation (NTA) via RCA and TLM (contextual consistency + target-centric contrastive learning), achieving gains on G-Ref.
AI.motivation: In RIS with parameter-efficient tuning, visual features often activate non-target objects (NTA), degrading target localization. A parameter-efficient solution is needed to recalibrate features toward the referent without full fine-tuning.
AI.method: Rectified Cost Aggregator (RCA) to efficiently aggregate text-referred features. Target-aware Learning Mechanism (TLM) with two components: (1) contextual pairwise consistency learning using sentence-level text features to build a text-referred affinity map for semantic alignment of visual features; (2) target-centric contrastive learning to sharpen target localization by suppressing non-target associations.
AI.result: Empirically improves performance over existing PET-based RIS methods; e.g., about 2.5 percentage points mIoU gain on the G-Ref validation set. Code will be released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The two TLM objectives complement each other to convert NTA into accurate target activation, making PET-based RIS more effective. TALENT demonstrates strong gains and practical usefulness for RIS with parameter-efficient tuning.

title: HabitatAgent: An End-to-End Multi-Agent System for Housing Consultation
summary:
