Housing selection is a high-stakes and largely irreversible decision problem. We study housing consultation as a decision-support interface for housing selection. Existing housing platforms and many LLM-based assistants often reduce this process to ranking or recommendation, resulting in opaque reasoning, brittle multi-constraint handling, and limited guarantees on factuality.
  We present HabitatAgent, the first LLM-powered multi-agent architecture for end-to-end housing consultation. HabitatAgent comprises four specialized agent roles: Memory, Retrieval, Generation, and Validation. The Memory Agent maintains multi-layer user memory through internal stages for constraint extraction, memory fusion, and verification-gated updates; the Retrieval Agent performs hybrid vector--graph retrieval (GraphRAG); the Generation Agent produces evidence-referenced recommendations and explanations; and the Validation Agent applies multi-tier verification and targeted remediation. Together, these agents provide an auditable and reliable workflow for end-to-end housing consultation.
  We evaluate HabitatAgent on 100 real user consultation scenarios (300 multi-turn question--answer pairs) under an end-to-end correctness protocol. A strong single-stage baseline (Dense+Rerank) achieves 75% accuracy, while HabitatAgent reaches 95%.

AI.tldr: HabitatAgent: an end-to-end LLM-powered multi-agent housing consultation system with four roles (Memory, Retrieval, Generation, Validation), achieving 95% accuracy on 100 real-user scenarios vs 75% for a Dense+Rerank baseline.
AI.motivation: Address opaque reasoning, brittle multi-constraint handling, and factuality gaps in existing housing platforms and LLM assistants by delivering auditable, reliable, evidence-backed housing consultation.
AI.method: Four specialized agents: Memory handles constraint extraction and memory updates with verification gating; Retrieval uses hybrid vector-graph retrieval (GraphRAG); Generation provides evidence-referenced recommendations and explanations; Validation applies multi-tier verification and remediation; end-to-end workflow; evaluated on 100 real-user scenarios (300 Q-A pairs) under an end-to-end correctness protocol.
AI.result: HabitatAgent achieves 95% accuracy versus 75% for a strong single-stage Dense+Rerank baseline on the evaluation set.
AI.conclusion: An auditable, reliable end-to-end housing consultation pipeline is feasible with a structured multi-agent LLM architecture; decomposition enhances constraint handling, evidence grounding, and verification, leading to improved performance and user trust.

title: DirectFisheye-GS: Enabling Native Fisheye Input in Gaussian Splatting with Cross-View Joint Optimization
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has enabled efficient 3D scene reconstruction from everyday images with real-time, high-fidelity rendering, greatly advancing VR/AR applications. Fisheye cameras, with their wider field of view (FOV), promise high-quality reconstructions from fewer inputs and have recently attracted much attention. However, since 3DGS relies on rasterization, most subsequent works involving fisheye camera inputs first undistort images before training, which introduces two problems: 1) Black borders at image edges cause information loss and negate the fisheye's large FOV advantage; 2) Undistortion's stretch-and-interpolate resampling spreads each pixel's value over a larger area, diluting detail density -- causes 3DGS overfitting these low-frequency zones, producing blur and floating artifacts. In this work, we integrate fisheye camera model into the original 3DGS framework, enabling native fisheye image input for training without preprocessing. Despite correct modeling, we observed that the reconstructed scenes still exhibit floaters at image edges: Distortion increases toward the periphery, and 3DGS's original per-iteration random-selecting-view optimization ignores the cross-view correlations of a Gaussian, leading to extreme shapes (e.g., oversized or elongated) that degrade reconstruction quality. To address this, we introduce a feature-overlap-driven cross-view joint optimization strategy that establishes consistent geometric and photometric constraints across views-a technique equally applicable to existing pinhole-camera-based pipelines. Our DirectFisheye-GS matches or surpasses state-of-the-art performance on public datasets.

AI.tldr: DirectFisheye-GS integrates fisheye camera models into 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to use raw fisheye inputs without undistortion, addressing border information loss and detail dilution. It adds a feature-overlap-driven cross-view joint optimization to impose geometric and photometric consistency, mitigating edge floaters and cross-view aliasing.
AI.motivation: Current 3DGS pipelines using undistorted fisheye images lose border information and spread pixel values, causing blurring and artifacts. Native fisheye integration aims to preserve FOV and image detail while avoiding preprocessing-induced issues.
AI.method: Incorporate the fisheye camera model into the 3DGS framework and diagnose edge floaters caused by per-iteration random-view optimization. Propose a feature-overlap-driven cross-view joint optimization that enforces consistent geometry and photometry across views; this strategy is generalizable to pinhole pipelines.
AI.result: DirectFisheye-GS achieves state-of-the-art performance on public datasets, matching or surpassing existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Native fisheye integration with cross-view joint optimization yields robust, high-quality reconstructions without undistortion, and the approach is broadly applicable to existing 3DGS and related pipelines.

title: Representation choice shapes the interpretation of protein conformational dynamics
summary:
Molecular dynamics simulations provide detailed trajectories at the atomic level, but extracting interpretable and robust insights from these high-dimensional data remains challenging. In practice, analyses typically rely on a single representation. Here, we show that representation choice is not neutral: it fundamentally shapes the conformational organization, similarity relationships, and apparent transitions inferred from identical simulation data.
  To complement existing representations, we introduce Orientation features, a geometrically grounded, rotation-aware encoding of protein backbone. We compare it against common descriptions across three dynamical regimes: fast-folding proteins, large-scale domain motions, and protein-protein association. Across these systems, we find that different representations emphasize complementary aspects of conformational space, and that no single representation provides a complete picture of the underlying dynamics.
  To facilitate systematic comparison, we developed ManiProt, a library for efficient computation and analysis of multiple protein representations. Our results motivate a comparative, representation-aware framework for the interpretation of molecular dynamics simulations.

AI.tldr: Representation choice in molecular dynamics reshapes conformational interpretation; introduces Orientation features and ManiProt library for rotation-aware backbone encoding and multi-representation analysis; shows complementary insights across fast folding, large-domain motions, and protein–protein association; advocates a representation-aware framework.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional MD trajectories are often analyzed using a single representation, which biases inferred conformational structure and transitions. There is a need to systematically compare multiple representations to obtain robust, interpretable insights.
AI.method: Develop Orientation features, a rotation-aware encoding of protein backbone geometry. Compare this representation against common representations across three dynamical regimes (fast-folding proteins, large-scale domain motions, protein–protein association). Implement ManiProt library for efficient computation and analysis of multiple representations. Apply cross-system evaluation to assess representation-dependent insights.
AI.result: Different representations emphasize complementary aspects of conformational space; no single representation fully captures the dynamics. Orientation features provide rotation-aware encoding that reveals distinct structural relationships. ManiProt enables efficient, systematic analysis across representations, facilitating a framework for representation-aware interpretation.
AI.conclusion: Adopt a comparative, representation-aware framework for MD interpretation; integrate multiple representations to obtain a more complete picture of molecular dynamics rather than relying on a single descriptor.

title: When AI and Experts Agree on Error: Intrinsic Ambiguity in Dermatoscopic Images
summary: The integration of artificial intelligence (AI), particularly Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), into dermatological diagnosis demonstrates substantial clinical potential. While existing literature predominantly benchmarks algorithmic performance against human experts, our study adopts a novel perspective by investigating the intrinsic complexity of dermatoscopic images. Through rigorous experimentation with multiple CNN architectures, we isolated a subset of images systematically misclassified across all models-a phenomenon statistically proven to exceed random chance. To determine if these failures stem from algorithmic biases or inherent visual ambiguity, expert dermatologists independently evaluated these challenging cases alongside a control group. The results revealed a collapse in human diagnostic performance on the AI-misclassified images. First, agreement with ground-truth labels plummeted, with Cohen's kappa dropping to a mere 0.08 for the difficult images, compared to a 0.61 for the control group. Second, we observed a severe deterioration in expert consensus; inter-rater reliability among physicians fell from moderate concordance (Fleiss kappa = 0.456) on control images to only modest agreement (Fleiss kappa = 0.275) on difficult cases. We identified image quality as a primary driver of these dual systematic failures. To promote transparency and reproducibility, all data, code, and trained models have been made publicly available

AI.tldr: Intrinsic difficulty in dermatoscopic images leads to a subset consistently misclassified by CNNs; human experts falter on these images; image quality drives failures; data/code/models released for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Probe whether CNN errors arise from algorithmic bias or inherent visual ambiguity, and to understand limits of AI in dermatology beyond aggregate performance.
AI.method: Train and evaluate multiple CNN architectures on dermatoscopic data; identify images misclassified by all models beyond chance; have expert dermatologists rate these challenging cases and a control set; compute Cohen's kappa and Fleiss kappa; analyze image quality as a driver; publish data/code/models.
AI.result: Identified a subset of images consistently misclassified across models; AI and human performance both degrade on these images (Cohen's kappa: 0.08 for difficult vs 0.61 control; Fleiss kappa: 0.275 vs 0.456); image quality emerges as a primary driver; data and models publicly available for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Intrinsic image ambiguity and variable image quality constrain both AI and human dermatologic diagnosis; improvements require data-centric strategies (quality control, curated datasets) and uncertainty-aware modeling alongside transparency through open data and code.

title: Predicting Dynamics of Ultra-Large Complex Systems by Inferring Governing Equations
summary: Predicting the behavior of ultra-large complex systems, from climate to biological and technological networks, is a central unsolved challenge. Existing approaches face a fundamental trade-off: equation discovery methods provide interpretability but fail to scale, while neural networks scale but operate as black boxes and often lose reliability over long times. Here, we introduce the Sparse Identification Graph Neural Network, a framework that overcome this divide by allowing to infer the governing equations of large networked systems from data. By defining symbolic discovery as edge-level information, SIGN decouples the scalability of sparse identification from network size, enabling efficient equation discovery even in large systems. SIGN allows to study networks with over 100,000 nodes while remaining robust to noise, sparse sampling, and missing data. Across diverse benchmark systems, including coupled chaotic oscillators, neural dynamics, and epidemic spreading, it recovers governing equations with high precision and sustains accurate long-term predictions. Applied to a data set of time series of temperature measurements in 71,987 sea surface positions, SIGN identifies a compact predictive network model and captures large-scale sea surface temperature conditions up to two years in advance. By enabling equation discovery at previously inaccessible scales, SIGN opens a path toward interpretable and reliable prediction of real-world complex systems.

AI.tldr: SIGN combines sparse equation discovery with graph neural networks to infer governing equations of large networked systems from data, enabling scalable, interpretable dynamics and accurate long-term predictions on systems with over 100k nodes.
AI.motivation: Overcome the trade-off between interpretable equation discovery and scalable neural models by enabling data-driven discovery of governing dynamics in large, noisy, and sparsely sampled networked systems while preserving interpretability.
AI.method: Define edge-level symbolic discovery within a graph neural network framework, decoupling sparse identification from network size to enable scalable equation discovery. Train on data from large networks to learn governing equations, with robustness to noise, missing data, and sparse sampling. Validate on coupled chaotic oscillators, neural dynamics, epidemic spreading, and a sea surface temperature dataset spanning ~72k positions.
AI.result: Accurate recovery of governing equations and robust long-term predictions across benchmarks. Demonstrates scalability to >100k nodes and precise predictive capability, including two-year-ahead sea surface temperature forecasting using a compact predictive network model.
AI.conclusion: SIGN enables interpretable, reliable equation discovery at scales previously inaccessible, offering a path toward transparent and dependable prediction for real-world complex networked systems.

title: CL-VISTA: Benchmarking Continual Learning in Video Large Language Models
summary: Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) require continual learning to adapt to non-stationary real-world data. However, existing benchmarks fall short of evaluating modern foundation models: many still rely on models without large-scale pre-training, and prevailing benchmarks typically partition a single dataset into sub-tasks, resulting in high task redundancy and negligible forgetting on pre-trained Video-LLMs. To address these limitations, we propose CL-VISTA, a benchmark tailored for continual video understanding of Video-LLMs. By curating 8 diverse tasks spanning perception, understanding, and reasoning, CL-VISTA induces substantial distribution shifts that effectively expose catastrophic forgetting. To systematically assess CL methods, we establish a comprehensive evaluation framework comprising 6 distinct protocols across 3 critical dimensions: performance, computational efficiency, and memory footprint. Notably, the performance dimension incorporates a general video understanding assessment to assess whether CL methods genuinely enhance foundational intelligence or merely induce task-specific overfitting. Extensive benchmarking of 10 mainstream CL methods reveals a fundamental trade-off: no single approach achieves universal superiority across all dimensions. Methods that successfully mitigate catastrophic forgetting tend to compromise generalization or incur prohibitive computational and memory overheads. We hope CL-VISTA provides critical insights for advancing continual learning in multimodal foundation models.

AI.tldr: A new 8-task continual video-learning benchmark, CL-VISTA, evaluates Video-LLMs under distribution shifts with 6 protocols across performance, efficiency, and memory, benchmarking 10 CL methods to reveal trade-offs and lack of a universal winner, aiming to advance multimodal continual learning.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks rely on models without large-scale pre-training or use single-dataset task splits, causing task redundancy and minimal forgetting in pre-trained Video-LLMs. A benchmark that induces substantial distribution shifts is needed to reveal catastrophic forgetting in modern multimodal foundation models.
AI.method: Create CL-VISTA with 8 diverse tasks spanning perception, understanding, and reasoning to induce distribution shifts. Establish 6 evaluation protocols across 3 dimensions: performance, computational efficiency, and memory footprint. Include a general video understanding assessment in performance to distinguish genuine continual learning gains from task-specific overfitting. Benchmark 10 mainstream continual learning methods across these protocols.
AI.result: Comprehensive benchmarking shows no single CL method dominates across all dimensions. Methods that mitigate forgetting often reduce generalization or incur high computational/memory costs, highlighting fundamental trade-offs in continual learning for Video-LLMs.
AI.conclusion: CL-VISTA provides a standardized, multi-dimensional benchmark for evaluating continual learning in multimodal foundation models, enabling more informed comparisons and driving progress in balancing forgetting mitigation with generalization, efficiency, and memory considerations.

title: A Survey of On-Policy Distillation for Large Language Models
summary: Knowledge distillation has become a primary mechanism for transferring reasoning and domain expertise from frontier Large Language Models (LLMs) to smaller, deployable students. However, the dominant paradigm remains \textit{off-policy}: students train on static teacher-generated data and never encounter their own errors during learning. This train--test mismatch, an instance of \textit{exposure bias}, causes prediction errors to compound autoregressively at inference time. On-Policy Distillation (OPD) addresses this by letting the student generate its own trajectories and receive teacher feedback on these self-generated outputs, grounding distillation in the theory of interactive imitation learning. Despite rapid growth spanning divergence minimization, reward-guided learning, and self-play, the OPD literature remains fragmented with no unified treatment. This survey provides the first comprehensive overview of OPD for LLMs. We introduce a unified $f$-divergence framework over on-policy samples and organize the landscape along three orthogonal dimensions: \emph{feedback signal} (logit-based, outcome-based, or self-play), \emph{teacher access} (white-box, black-box, or teacher-free), and \emph{loss granularity} (token-level, sequence-level, or hybrid). We systematically analyze representative methods, examine industrial deployments, and identify open problems including distillation scaling laws, uncertainty-aware feedback, and agent-level distillation.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of On-Policy Distillation (OPD) for LLMs, introducing a unified f-divergence framework and a three-dimensional taxonomy (feedback signal, teacher access, loss granularity), with analysis of methods, deployments, and open problems.
AI.motivation: Off-policy distillation suffers from exposure bias and train-test mismatch. OPD grounds distillation in interactive imitation learning by allowing the student to generate trajectories and receive teacher feedback on self-generated outputs.
AI.method: Proposes a unified f-divergence framework over on-policy samples and organizes the literature along three orthogonal dimensions: feedback signal (logit-based, outcome-based, self-play), teacher access (white-box, black-box, teacher-free), and loss granularity (token-level, sequence-level, hybrid). Systematically analyzes representative methods and deployments.
AI.result: Systematic analysis of representative OPD methods, examination of industrial deployments, and identification of open problems including distillation scaling laws, uncertainty-aware feedback, and agent-level distillation.
AI.conclusion: First comprehensive overview of OPD for LLMs; provides a unified taxonomy and framework, and outlines key open problems to guide future research.

title: MoonAnything: A Vision Benchmark with Large-Scale Lunar Supervised Data
summary: Accurate perception of lunar surfaces is critical for modern lunar exploration missions. However, developing robust learning-based perception systems is hindered by the lack of datasets that provide both geometric and photometric supervision. Existing lunar datasets typically lack either geometric ground truth, photometric realism, illumination diversity, or large-scale coverage. In this paper, we introduce MoonAnything, a unified benchmark built on real lunar topography with physically-based rendering, providing the first comprehensive geometric and photometric supervision under diverse illumination with large scale. The benchmark comprises two complementary sub-datasets : i) LunarGeo provides stereo images with corresponding dense depth maps and camera calibration enabling 3D reconstruction and pose estimation; ii) LunarPhoto provides photorealistic images using a spatially-varying BRDF model, along with multi-illumination renderings under real solar configurations, enabling reflectance estimation and illumination-robust perception. Together, these datasets offer over 130K samples with comprehensive supervision. Beyond lunar applications, MoonAnything offers a unique setting and challenging testbed for algorithms under low-textured, high-contrast conditions and applies to other airless celestial bodies and could generalize beyond. We establish baselines using state-of-the-art methods and release the complete dataset along with generation tools to support community extension: https://github.com/clementinegrethen/MoonAnything.

AI.tldr: MoonAnything is a unified lunar benchmark with LunarGeo (depth-dense stereo with calibration) and LunarPhoto (photorealistic images with BRDF-based rendering) built on real lunar topography to provide geometric and photometric supervision under diverse illumination, ~130k samples, with baselines and open-source tools.
AI.motivation: Existing lunar datasets often provide either geometric ground truth or photometric realism, illumination diversity, or large scale, but not all aspects together. A unified, realistic, large-scale dataset with both geometric and photometric supervision is needed for robust learning-based lunar perception under low-textured, high-contrast conditions.
AI.method: Leverages real lunar topography and physically-based rendering to produce two complementary sub-datasets. LunarGeo: stereo image pairs with dense depth maps and camera calibration for 3D reconstruction and pose estimation. LunarPhoto: photorealistic images using a spatially-varying BRDF model with multi-illumination renderings under real solar configurations, enabling reflectance estimation and illumination-robust perception.
AI.result: MoonAnything provides over 130k samples with comprehensive geometric and photometric supervision. Baselines using state-of-the-art methods are established, and the dataset along with generation tools is released publicly to support community extension: https://github.com/clementinegrethen/MoonAnything.
AI.conclusion: MoonAnything offers a novel, comprehensive benchmark for lunar perception, challenging low-textured, high-contrast scenarios and potentially generalizable to other airless bodies; it enables tasks across 3D reconstruction, pose estimation, reflectance, and illumination-robust perception, with broad applicability.

title: Chameleons do not Forget: Prompt-Based Online Continual Learning for Next Activity Prediction
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication in the International Journal of Cooperative Information Systems
summary: Predictive process monitoring (PPM) focuses on predicting future process trajectories, including next activity predictions. This is crucial in dynamic environments where processes change or face uncertainty. However, current frameworks often assume a static environment, overlooking dynamic characteristics and concept drifts. This results in catastrophic forgetting, where training while focusing merely on new data distribution negatively impacts the performance on previously learned data distributions. Continual learning addresses, among others, the challenges related to mitigating catastrophic forgetting. This paper proposes a novel approach called Continual Next Activity Prediction with Prompts (CNAPwP), which adapts the DualPrompt algorithm for next activity prediction to improve accuracy and adaptability while mitigating catastrophic forgetting. We introduce new datasets with recurring concept drifts, alongside a task-specific forgetting metric that measures the prediction accuracy gap between initial occurrence and subsequent task occurrences. Extensive testing on three synthetic and two real-world datasets representing several setups of recurrent drifts shows that CNAPwP achieves SOTA or competitive results compared to five baselines, demonstrating its potential applicability in real-world scenarios. An open-source implementation of our method, together with the datasets and results, is available at: https://github.com/SvStraten/CNAPwP.

AI.tldr: CNAPwP extends continual learning to predictive process monitoring for next activity prediction under recurring concept drifts, adapting DualPrompt to mitigate forgetting with new datasets and a forgetting metric, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: PPM often assumes static environments; dynamic processes with drifting concepts cause catastrophic forgetting, necessitating continual learning strategies to maintain performance across evolving tasks.
AI.method: Adapts the DualPrompt algorithm for next activity prediction, introducing task-specific prompts and a continual-learning framework (CNAPwP); creates new recurrent-drift datasets and a task-specific forgetting metric; evaluates against five baselines.
AI.result: CNAPwP achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance compared to baselines on three synthetic and two real-world datasets representing recurrent drifts, indicating strong adaptability and potential real-world applicability.
AI.conclusion: CNAPwP effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting in predictive process monitoring under concept drift; open-source implementation and datasets enhance reproducibility and practical deployment.

title: TP-Seg: Task-Prototype Framework for Unified Medical Lesion Segmentation
summary: Building a unified model with a single set of parameters to efficiently handle diverse types of medical lesion segmentation has become a crucial objective for AI-assisted diagnosis. Existing unified segmentation approaches typically rely on shared encoders across heterogeneous tasks and modalities, which often leads to feature entanglement, gradient interference, and suboptimal lesion discrimination. In this work, we propose TP-Seg, a task-prototype framework for unified medical lesion segmentation. On one hand, the task-conditioned adapter effectively balances shared and task-specific representations through a dual-path expert structure, enabling adaptive feature extraction across diverse medical imaging modalities and lesion types. On the other hand, the prototype-guided task decoder introduces learnable task prototypes as semantic anchors and employs a cross-attention mechanism to achieve fine-grained modeling of task-specific foreground and background semantics. Without bells and whistles, TP-Seg consistently outperforms specialized, general and unified segmentation methods across 8 different medical lesion segmentation tasks covering multiple imaging modalities, demonstrating strong generalization, scalability and clinical applicability.

AI.tldr: TP-Seg introduces a task-prototype framework for unified medical lesion segmentation that uses a dual-path task-conditioned adapter and a prototype-guided decoder to balance shared vs task-specific features and model foreground/background semantics, achieving strong cross-task performance.
AI.motivation: To overcome feature entanglement, gradient interference, and suboptimal lesion discrimination in unified segmentation across heterogeneous tasks and imaging modalities, enabling a scalable, generalizable solution for medical lesion segmentation.
AI.method: A task-conditioned adapter with a dual-path expert structure to balance shared and task-specific representations; a prototype-guided task decoder that employs learnable task prototypes as semantic anchors and a cross-attention mechanism to model task-specific foreground and background semantics.
AI.result: TP-Seg consistently outperforms specialized, general, and unified segmentation methods across 8 medical lesion segmentation tasks spanning multiple imaging modalities, demonstrating strong generalization, scalability, and potential clinical applicability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TP-Seg provides an effective, scalable framework for unified medical lesion segmentation with strong cross-task generalization, suggesting promise for clinical deployment and future expansion to additional modalities and tasks.

title: Embedded Variational Neural Stochastic Differential Equations for Learning Heterogeneous Dynamics
summary: This study examines the challenges of modeling complex and noisy data related to socioeconomic factors over time, with a focus on data from various districts in Odisha, India. Traditional time-series models struggle to capture both trends and variations together in this type of data. To tackle this, a Variational Neural Stochastic Differential Equation (V-NSDE) model is designed that combines the expressive dynamics of Neural SDEs with the generative capabilities of Variational Autoencoders (VAEs). This model uses an encoder and a decoder. The encoder takes the initial observations and district embeddings and translates them into a Gaussian distribution, which determines the mean and log-variance of the first latent state. Then the obtained latent state initiates the Neural SDE, which utilize neural networks to determine the drift and diffusion functions that govern continuous-time latent dynamics. These governing functions depend on the time index, latent state, and district embedding, which help the model learn the unique characteristics specific to each district. After that, using a probabilistic decoder, the observations are reconstructed from the latent trajectory. The decoder outputs a mean and log-variance for each time step, which follows the Gaussian likelihood. The Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) training loss improves by adding a KL-divergence regularization term to the negative log-likelihood (nll). The obtained results demonstrate the effective learning of V-NSDE in recognizing complex patterns over time, yielding realistic outcomes that include clear trends and random fluctuations across different areas.

AI.tldr: A variational neural stochastic differential equation (V-NSDE) model is proposed to model complex, noisy socioeconomic time-series across districts. It merges Neural SDEs with VAEs, using an encoder to map initial observations and district embeddings to a Gaussian latent state, a neural SDE defining drift and diffusion that depend on time, latent state, and district embedding, and a probabilistic decoder to reconstruct observations. Training uses ELBO with a KL regularizer. Results indicate the model captures both clear trends and stochastic fluctuations across districts.
AI.motivation: Traditional time-series models struggle to capture both long-term trends and short-term noise when data are noisy and heterogeneous across districts. A continuous-time, district-aware generative framework is desirable to model temporal dynamics and observation uncertainty jointly.
AI.method: A V-NSDE architecture: encoder maps initial data and district embeddings to a Gaussian distribution for the initial latent state; a Neural SDE with drift and diffusion functions parameterized by neural networks (dependent on time, latent state, and district embeddings) evolves the latent state; a probabilistic decoder outputs per-time-step Gaussian likelihood (mean and variance) for observations; training via ELBO with a KL divergence term.
AI.result: The model effectively learns latent trajectories that exhibit realistic trends and random fluctuations across districts, demonstrating its capability to model complex, noisy socioeconomic time-series.
AI.conclusion: V-NSDE provides a unified continuous-time latent dynamics and probabilistic decoding framework that handles district-specific heterogeneity and observation noise, offering a promising approach for multi-region temporal data and potentially applicable to other domains with noisy time-series.

title: TTA-Vid: Generalized Test-Time Adaptation for Video Reasoning
summary: Recent video reasoning models have shown strong results on temporal and multimodal understanding, yet they depend on large-scale supervised data and multi-stage training pipelines, making them costly to train and difficult to adapt to new domains. In this work, we leverage the paradigm of Test-Time Reinforcement Learning on video-language data to allow for adapting a pretrained model to incoming video samples at test-time without explicit labels. The proposed test-time adaptation for video approach (TTA-Vid) combines two components that work simultaneously: (1) a test-time adaptation that performs step-by-step reasoning at inference time on multiple frame subsets. We then use a batch-aware frequency-based reward computed across different frame subsets as pseudo ground truth to update the model. It shows that the resulting model trained on a single batch or even a single sample from a dataset, is able to generalize at test-time to the whole dataset and even across datasets. Because the adaptation occurs entirely at test time, our method requires no ground-truth annotations or dedicated training splits. Additionally, we propose a multi-armed bandit strategy for adaptive frame selection that learns to prioritize informative frames, guided by the same reward formulation. Our evaluation shows that TTA-Vid yields consistent improvements across various video reasoning tasks and is able to outperform current state-of-the-art methods trained on large-scale data. This highlights the potential of test-time reinforcement learning for temporal multimodal understanding.

AI.tldr: A test-time reinforcement learning approach (TTA-Vid) for video reasoning that adapts pretrained models on-the-fly without labels, using stepwise inference on frame subsets with a frequency-based reward and a multi-armed bandit for frame selection, achieving cross-dataset generalization and SOTA performance without large supervised data.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on large annotated datasets and multi-stage training for video-language understanding; enable domain adaptation during inference by leveraging unlabeled test-time feedback.
AI.method: Two components: (1) test-time adaptation with step-by-step reasoning over multiple frame subsets; (2) batch-aware frequency-based reward across subsets used as pseudo ground truth to update the model; includes a multi-armed bandit strategy to prioritize informative frames, guided by the same reward.
AI.result: A model that can generalize from a single batch or even a single sample to the entire dataset and across datasets; shows consistent improvements across various video reasoning tasks and outperforms current state-of-the-art methods trained on large-scale data; requires no ground-truth annotations or dedicated training splits at test time.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the potential of test-time reinforcement learning for temporal multimodal understanding; TTA-Vid is effective for adapting pretrained video-language models to new data without labels.

title: Full-Gradient Successor Feature Representations
summary: Successor Features (SF) combined with Generalized Policy Improvement (GPI) provide a robust framework for transfer learning in Reinforcement Learning (RL) by decoupling environment dynamics from reward functions. However, standard SF learning methods typically rely on semi-gradient Temporal Difference (TD) updates. When combined with non-linear function approximation, semi-gradient methods lack robust convergence guarantees and can lead to instability, particularly in the multi-task setting where accurate feature estimation is critical for effective GPI. Inspired by Full Gradient DQN, we propose Full-Gradient Successor Feature Representations Q-Learning (FG-SFRQL), an algorithm that optimizes the successor features by minimizing the full Mean Squared Bellman Error. Unlike standard approaches, our method computes gradients with respect to parameters in both the online and target networks. We provide a theoretical proof of almost-sure convergence for FG-SFRQL and demonstrate empirically that minimizing the full residual leads to superior sample efficiency and transfer performance compared to semi-gradient baselines in both discrete and continuous domains.

AI.tldr: FG-SFRQL introduces full-gradient optimization for successor-feature representations in Q-learning, providing convergence guarantees and improved sample efficiency and transfer over semi-gradient baselines.
AI.motivation: Semi-gradient TD updates with non-linear function approximators can be unstable and lack convergence guarantees, especially in multi-task transfer where accurate feature estimation is crucial; robust, convergent methods are needed.
AI.method: Minimize the full Mean Squared Bellman Error (MSBE) by backpropagating gradients through both online and target networks, inspired by Full Gradient DQN, extending this approach to successor features.
AI.result: Theoretical proof of almost-sure convergence; empirical results show superior sample efficiency and transfer performance compared to semi-gradient baselines in both discrete and continuous domains.
AI.conclusion: Full-gradient optimization of successor features (FG-SFRQL) yields robust convergence and improved transfer and sample efficiency, validating full-gradient updates for successor feature-based RL across discrete and continuous domains.

title: Performance of Neural and Polynomial Operator Surrogates
summary: We consider the problem of constructing surrogate operators for parameter-to-solution maps arising from parametric partial differential equations, where repeated forward model evaluations are computationally expensive. We present a systematic empirical comparison of neural operator surrogates, including a reduced-basis neural operator trained with $L^2_μ$ and $H^1_μ$ objectives and the Fourier neural operator, against polynomial surrogate methods, specifically a reduced-basis sparse-grid surrogate and a reduced-basis tensor-train surrogate. All methods are evaluated on a linear parametric diffusion problem and a nonlinear parametric hyperelasticity problem, using input fields with algebraically decaying spectral coefficients at varying rates of decay $s$. To enable fair comparisons, we analyze ensembles of surrogate models generated by varying hyperparameters and compare the resulting Pareto frontiers of cost versus approximation accuracy, decomposing cost into contributions from data generation, setup, and evaluation. Our results show that no single method is universally superior. Polynomial surrogates achieve substantially better data efficiency for smooth input fields ($s \geq 2$), with convergence rates for the sparse-grid surrogate in agreement with theoretical predictions. For rough inputs ($s \leq 1$), the Fourier neural operator displays the fastest convergence rates. Derivative-informed training consistently improves data efficiency over standard $L^2_μ$ training, providing a competitive alternative for rough inputs in the low-data regime when Jacobian information is available at reasonable cost. These findings highlight the importance of matching the surrogate methodology to the regularity of the problem as well as accuracy demands and computational constraints of the application.

AI.tldr: A comparative study on surrogate models for parametric PDEs, evaluating neural operators and polynomial surrogates across linear diffusion and nonlinear hyperelasticity problems with varying input regularity; findings show no universally superior method. Data efficiency depends on input smoothness; polynomial surrogates excel for smooth inputs, Fourier neural operators excel for rough inputs; derivative-informed training improves data efficiency; match method to problem regularity and computational constraints.
AI.motivation: Address computational bottlenecks in parameter-to-solution maps for parametric PDEs by rigorously comparing a spectrum of surrogate strategies (neural operators and polynomial surrogates) to guide practitioners in method selection under data and cost constraints.
AI.method: Empirical comparison of neural operator surrogates (reduced-basis neural operator with L2_mu and H1_mu objectives, Fourier neural operator) and polynomial surrogates (reduced-basis sparse-grid surrogate, reduced-basis tensor-train surrogate). Evaluations on a linear parametric diffusion problem and a nonlinear parametric hyperelasticity problem with input fields having algebraically decaying spectral coefficients at varying decay rates s. Ensembles of models generated by varying hyperparameters; Pareto fronts of cost vs accuracy analyzed, with cost decomposed into data generation, setup, and evaluation.
AI.result: No single method dominates. Polynomial surrogates exhibit substantially better data efficiency for smooth inputs (s ≥ 2); sparse-grid surrogate convergence aligns with theoretical predictions. For rough inputs (s ≤ 1), Fourier neural operator achieves fastest convergence. Derivative-informed training improves data efficiency over standard L2_mu training, offering a competitive option for rough inputs in low-data settings when Jacobian information is affordable. Emphasizes matching surrogate choice to problem regularity and application constraints.
AI.conclusion: Matching surrogate methodology to the regularity of the problem and to accuracy and computational constraints is crucial. The regularity of input fields should guide method selection rather than relying on a single universally superior approach.

title: IWP: Token Pruning as Implicit Weight Pruning in Large Vision Language Models
summary: Large Vision Language Models show impressive performance across image and video understanding tasks, yet their computational cost grows rapidly with the number of visual tokens. Existing token pruning methods mitigate this issue through empirical approaches while overlooking the internal mechanism of attention. In this paper, we propose a novel training free token pruning framework grounded in the dual form perspective of attention. We reformulate attention as an implicit linear layer whose weight matrix is the sum of rank 1 outer products, each generated by a single token's key value pair. Token pruning thus reduces to selecting an optimal subset of these rank 1 updates that best approximates the original dual weight matrix. Extending this perspective to standard softmax attention in LVLMs, we derive a novel metric quantifying both a token's information magnitude and information duplication. To efficiently select the subset with the proposed metric, we introduce Progressive Chunked Maximal Marginal Relevance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves a better trade off between performance and efficiency, while providing another perspective on existing pruning approaches.

AI.tldr: Training-free token pruning for LVLMs via dual-form attention, selecting rank-1 updates to approximate the full attention; uses a metric of information magnitude and duplication, optimized with Progressive Chunked Maximal Marginal Relevance.
AI.motivation: Token costs grow with visual tokens; existing pruning methods are empirical and ignore attention mechanics; aim for principled, training-free pruning grounded in attention structure.
AI.method: Reformulate attention as an implicit linear layer with weight = sum of rank-1 outer products from each token's key-value; prune by selecting a subset of rank-1 updates to approximate the full dual weight; derive a token-level metric capturing information magnitude and redundancy for standard softmax attention in LVLMs; propose Progressive Chunked Maximal Marginal Relevance to efficiently optimize the subset.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show improved performance-efficiency trade-offs and provide a new perspective on pruning strategies; results are claimed to be robust across tasks/datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a training-free, principled approach to token pruning, deepens understanding of attention mechanisms, and complements existing pruning methods.

title: Learning to Hint for Reinforcement Learning
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is widely used for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, but it often suffers from advantage collapse: when all rollouts in a group receive the same reward, the group yields zero relative advantage and thus no learning signal. For example, if a question is too hard for the reasoner, all sampled rollouts can be incorrect and receive zero reward. Recent work addresses this issue by adding hints or auxiliary scaffolds to such hard questions so that the reasoner produces mixed outcomes and recovers a non-zero update. However, existing hints are usually fixed rather than adapted to the current reasoner, and a hint that creates learning signal under the hinted input does not necessarily improve the no-hint policy used at test time. To this end, we propose Hint Learning for Reinforcement Learning (HiLL), a framework that jointly trains a hinter policy and a reasoner policy during RL. For each hard question, the hinter generates hints online conditioned on the current reasoner's incorrect rollout, allowing hint generation to adapt to the reasoner's evolving errors. We further introduce hint reliance, which measures how strongly correct hinted trajectories depend on the hint. We derive a transferability result showing that lower hint reliance implies stronger transfer from hinted success to no-hint success, and we use this result to define a transfer-weighted reward for training the hinter. Therefore, HiLL favors hints that not only recover informative GRPO groups, but also produce signals that are more likely to improve the original no-hint policy. Experiments across multiple benchmarks show that HiLL consistently outperforms GRPO and prior hint-based baselines, demonstrating the value of adaptive and transfer-aware hint learning for RL. The code is available at https://github.com/Andree-9/HiLL.

AI.tldr: HiLL jointly trains a hinter policy to adapt hints to the current reasoner in GRPO RL, introducing hint reliance and transfer-weighted rewards to promote hints that both recover learning signals and transfer to the no-hint policy; empirical gains across benchmarks with reproducible code.
AI.motivation: GRPO suffers advantage collapse when groups receive identical rewards; fixed or static hints may not adapt to the learner’s evolving errors or transfer to no-hint performance; adaptive, transfer-aware hints could provide robust learning signals.
AI.method: Jointly train a hinter policy and a reasoner policy. For hard questions, the hinter generates hints online conditioned on the reasoner’s incorrect rollout. Introduce hint reliance and derive a transferability result: lower hint reliance implies stronger transfer from hinted success to no-hint success. Use this to define a transfer-weighted reward for training the hinter.
AI.result: HiLL outperforms GRPO and prior hint-based baselines across multiple benchmarks; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive and transfer-aware hint learning improves reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards by aligning hints with both recovering learning signals and benefiting the no-hint policy.

title: PrivHAR-Bench: A Graduated Privacy Benchmark Dataset for Video-Based Action Recognition
summary: Existing research on privacy-preserving Human Activity Recognition (HAR) typically evaluates methods against a binary paradigm: clear video versus a single privacy transformation. This limits cross-method comparability and obscures the nuanced relationship between privacy strength and recognition utility. We introduce \textit{PrivHAR-Bench}, a multi-tier benchmark dataset designed to standardize the evaluation of the \textit{Privacy-Utility Trade-off} in video-based action recognition. PrivHAR-Bench applies a graduated spectrum of visual privacy transformations: from lightweight spatial obfuscation to cryptographic block permutation, to a curated subset of 15 activity classes selected for human articulation diversity. Each of the 1,932 source videos is distributed across 9 parallel tiers of increasing privacy strength, with additional background-removed variants to isolate the contribution of human motion features from contextual scene bias. We provide lossless frame sequences, per-frame bounding boxes, estimated pose keypoints with joint-level confidence scores, standardized group-based train/test splits, and an evaluation toolkit computing recognition accuracy and privacy metrics. Empirical validation using R3D-18 demonstrates a measurable and interpretable degradation curve across tiers, with within-tier accuracy declining from 88.8\% (clear) to 53.5\% (encrypted, background-removed) and cross-domain accuracy collapsing to 4.8\%, establishing PrivHAR-Bench as a controlled benchmark for comparing privacy-preserving HAR methods under standardized conditions. The dataset, generation pipeline, and evaluation code are publicly available.

AI.tldr: Introduces PrivHAR-Bench, a multi-tier privacy-utility benchmark for video HAR with 9 privacy-strength tiers, 15 action classes, and background-removed variants; shows clear-to-encrypted performance degradation and low cross-domain accuracy; provides data, pipeline, and code for standardized evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current privacy-preserving HAR research relies on a binary clear-vs-transformed setup, hindering cross-method comparability and obscuring the privacy-utility relationship; a standardized, tunable benchmark is needed.
AI.method: Assembles 1,932 source videos across 9 privacy tiers, applying a spectrum of privacy transformations from lightweight obfuscation to cryptographic block permutation; includes 15 diverse action classes; provides frames, bounding boxes, pose keypoints with confidence, train/test splits, and an evaluation toolkit; offers baseline validation using R3D-18.
AI.result: Empirical results show a monotonic degradation: within-tier accuracy declines from 88.8% (clear) to 53.5% (encrypted with background removal) and cross-domain accuracy drops to 4.8%; dataset and code are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: PrivHAR-Bench provides a controlled, scalable framework to compare privacy-preserving HAR methods under standardized conditions and varying privacy strengths, enabling meaningful analysis of privacy-utility trade-offs.

title: Exploring Silent Data Corruption as a Reliability Challenge in LLM Training
summary:
As Large Language Models (LLMs) scale in size and complexity, the consequences of failures during training become increasingly severe. A major challenge arises from Silent Data Corruption (SDC): hardware-induced faults that bypass system-level detection mechanisms. SDC may behave like benign numerical noise, but can also cause harmful gradient corruption that leads to loss spikes, divergence, or stalled progress.
  This work provides a controlled study of how intermittent SDC affects LLM pretraining. Using targeted fault injection at the level of GPU matrix-multiply instructions, we characterize the sensitivity of different bit positions, kernel functions, and execution stages. Our analysis shows that locally originating faults can produce impactful corruption, including NaN propagation, short-lived spikes in loss, gradient norm, and attention logits, as well as persistent parameter divergence. Building on the observed corruption signatures, we propose a lightweight detection method that identifies potentially harmful parameter updates. Experiments on LLaMA models with 60M, 350M, and 1.3B parameters demonstrate that recomputing the most recent training step upon detection can effectively mitigate the impact of these events.

AI.tldr: Intermittent GPU-level silent data corruption during LLM pretraining can cause harmful numerical issues (loss spikes, NaNs, divergence); a lightweight detection method paired with recomputing the last training step effectively mitigates impact, demonstrated on LLaMA models (60M, 350M, 1.3B).
AI.motivation: Assess the practical impact of silent data corruption (SDC) on large-scale LLM pretraining and develop practical resilience techniques that can be deployed with minimal overhead.
AI.method: Targeted fault injection at GPU matrix-multiply (GEMM) instructions to map sensitivity across bit positions, kernels, and execution stages; characterize corruption signatures (NaN propagation, loss/gradient/logits spikes, divergence); design a lightweight detector to flag potentially harmful parameter updates; validate by retraining with recomputation of the most recent step upon detection on LLaMA variants (60M, 350M, 1.3B).
AI.result: Locally originating faults can yield impactful corruption, including NaNs, transient spikes, and persistent parameter divergence. A detection mechanism can identify harmful updates, and recomputing the most recent training step upon detection effectively mitigates the effects.
AI.conclusion: SDC during pretraining is a tangible risk; a simple, hardware-aware resilience strategy—detecting suspicious updates and redoing the last step—offers a practical mitigation and motivates further hardware-software co-design for robust large-scale training.

title: An Approach to Enriching Surgical Video Datasets for Fine-Grained Spatial-Temporal Understanding of Vision-Language Models
summary: Surgical video understanding is a crucial prerequisite for advancing Computer-Assisted Surgery. While vision-language models (VLMs) have recently been applied to the surgical domain, existing surgical vision-language datasets lack in capturing and evaluating complex, interleaved spatial-temporal dynamics. Creating large scale datasets that accurately represent fine-grained spatial-temporal relationships in surgical videos is challenging due to costly manual annotations or error-prone generation using large language models. To address this gap, we introduce the SurgSTU-Pipeline, a deterministic generation pipeline featuring temporal and spatial continuity filtering to reliably create surgical datasets for fine-grained spatial-temporal multimodal understanding. Applying this pipeline to publicly available surgical datasets, we create the SurgSTU dataset, comprising 7515 video clips densely extended with 150k fine-grained spatial-temporal question-answer samples. Our comprehensive evaluation shows that while state-of-the-art generalist VLMs struggle in zero-shot settings, their spatial-temporal capabilities can be improved through in-context learning. A fine-tuned VLM on the SurgSTU training dataset achieves highest performance among all spatial-temporal tasks, validating the dataset's efficacy to improve spatial-temporal understanding of VLMs in surgical videos. Code will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: Introduces SurgSTU-Pipeline to deterministically generate a large-scale, fine-grained spatial-temporal surgical dataset (SurgSTU) for vision-language models; finds zero-shot VLMs perform poorly but improve with in-context learning; a SurgSTU-finetuned VLM attains top performance on spatial-temporal tasks.
AI.motivation: Surgical VLM datasets currently fail to capture complex, interleaved spatial-temporal dynamics; manual annotations are costly and error-prone with LLMs; a scalable, reliable data generation approach with spatial-temporal continuity is needed.
AI.method: Propose SurgSTU-Pipeline with temporal and spatial continuity filtering; apply to public surgical datasets to create SurgSTU with 7515 clips and 150k fine-grained spatial-temporal QA samples; evaluate generalist VLMs in zero-shot and with in-context learning; fine-tune a VLM on SurgSTU.
AI.result: Zero-shot performance of state-of-the-art generalist VLMs is limited; in-context learning improves spatial-temporal capabilities; a VLM fine-tuned on SurgSTU achieves the highest performance on spatial-temporal tasks; code will be released.
AI.conclusion: The SurgSTU pipeline effectively enables reliable generation of large-scale, high-quality multimodal surgical datasets, validating their utility to enhance spatial-temporal understanding of VLMs in surgical videos.

title: Spectral Compact Training: Pre-Training Large Language Models via Permanent Truncated SVD and Stiefel QR Retraction
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, 4 tables. Patent pending: Irish Application PTIE20260000000219. Code at https://github.com/EctoSpace/SCT
summary: The memory wall remains the primary bottleneck for training large language models on consumer hardware. We introduce Spectral Compact Training (SCT), a method that replaces dense weight matrices with permanent truncated SVD factors W = U diag(s) V^T, where the full dense matrix is never materialized during training or inference. Gradients flow through the compact spectral factors via standard backpropagation, and U, V are retracted to the Stiefel manifold via QR decomposition after each optimizer step. SCT achieves up to 199x memory reduction per MLP layer at rank 32, enabling full training steps of 70B-parameter architectures on a Steam Deck handheld (7.2 GB peak memory vs. 1,245 GB for dense FP32 training with Adam). Rank-sweep experiments on SmolLM2-1.7B (ranks 32-256, 2000 steps, NVIDIA A100) show that all tested ranks converge to the same loss floor (~4.2-4.5), identifying the learning rate schedule -- not MLP rank -- as the primary bottleneck. Rank 128 emerges as the efficiency sweet spot at 11.7x MLP compression with the lowest perplexity. GPU memory drops 46% at rank 32 while training throughput doubles.

AI.tldr: Spectral Compact Training (SCT) replaces dense weight matrices with low-rank spectral factors W = U diag(s) V^T, avoiding full matrices during training/inference and applying QR retractions to keep U, V on the Stiefel manifold. This yields massive memory savings and practical training of extremely large models on consumer hardware.
AI.motivation: Mitigate the memory wall in training large language models on consumer hardware by avoiding materialization of dense weights and enabling efficient backpropagation through compact factors.
AI.method: Use truncated SVD-like factorization W = U diag(s) V^T to represent weight matrices. Backpropagates through U, V; retraction to the Stiefel manifold via QR after each optimizer step to maintain orthogonality. No full dense matrix is ever materialized. Evaluate via rank-sweep experiments on SmolLM2-1.7B (r=32–256) on NVIDIA A100 and memory/throughput on Steam Deck-sized hardware.
AI.result: Up to 199x memory reduction per MLP layer at rank 32 (7.2 GB peak vs 1,245 GB dense FP32). Rank-sweep shows convergence to similar loss floors across ranks (~4.2–4.5); learning rate schedule identified as primary bottleneck, not rank. Rank 128 yields ~11.7x MLP compression with best perplexity. GPU memory drops 46% at rank 32; training throughput roughly doubles.
AI.conclusion: SCT enables substantial memory efficiency and practical training of large models on constrained hardware by factoring weights into compact, orthogonal spectral components and reprojecting to the Stiefel manifold; rank selection is crucial for optimal efficiency, while LR scheduling remains a key bottleneck.

title: HICT: High-precision 3D CBCT reconstruction from a single X-ray
summary: Accurate 3D dental imaging is vital for diagnosis and treatment planning, yet CBCT's high radiation dose and cost limit its accessibility. Reconstructing 3D volumes from a single low-dose panoramic X-ray is a promising alternative but remains challenging due to geometric inconsistencies and limited accuracy. We propose HiCT, a two-stage framework that first generates geometrically consistent multi-view projections from a single panoramic image using a video diffusion model, and then reconstructs high-fidelity CBCT from the projections using a ray-based dynamic attention network and an X-ray sampling strategy. To support this, we built XCT, a large-scale dataset combining public CBCT data with 500 paired PX-CBCT cases. Extensive experiments show that HiCT achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering accurate and geometrically consistent reconstructions for clinical use.

AI.tldr: HiCT presents a two-stage pipeline turning a single panoramic X-ray into a high-fidelity CBCT via diffusion-based multi-view projection generation and a ray-based dynamic-attention reconstruction, enabled by a new XCT dataset and achieving state-of-the-art results with geometric consistency.
AI.motivation: Reduce radiation dose and cost of dental 3D imaging by enabling accurate 3D reconstruction from a single low-dose panoramic X-ray; address geometric inconsistencies and limited accuracy that hinder usable CBCT reconstructions from 2D inputs.
AI.method: Stage 1: Use a video diffusion model to generate geometrically consistent multi-view projections from a single panoramic image. Stage 2: Reconstruct high-fidelity CBCT from the projections using a ray-based dynamic attention network and an X-ray sampling strategy. Dataset XCT combines public CBCT data with 500 paired PX-CBCT cases for training/evaluation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance, delivering accurate and geometrically consistent reconstructions suitable for clinical use.
AI.conclusion: HiCT demonstrates a practical pathway for low-dose 3D dental imaging, with the XCT dataset supporting scalability and future research into panographic-to-CBCT reconstruction.

title: BioCOMPASS: Integrating Biomarkers into Transformer-Based Immunotherapy Response Prediction
summary: Datasets used in immunotherapy response prediction are typically small in size, as well as diverse in cancer type, drug administered, and sequencer used. Models often drop in performance when tested on patient cohorts that are not included in the training process. Recent work has shown that transformer-based models along with self-supervised learning show better generalisation performance than threshold-based biomarkers, but is still suboptimal. We present BioCOMPASS, an extension of a transformer-based model called COMPASS, that integrates biomarkers and treatment information to further improve its generalisability. Instead of feeding biomarker data as input, we built loss components to align them with the model's intermediate representations. We found that components such as treatment gating and pathway consistency loss improved generalisability when evaluated with Leave-one-cohort-out, Leave-one-cancer-type-out and Leave-one-treatment-out strategies. Results show that building components that exploit biomarker and treatment information can help in generalisability of immunotherapy response prediction. Careful curation of additional components that leverage complementary clinical information and domain knowledge represents a promising direction for future research.

AI.tldr: BioCOMPASS extends a transformer-based model with loss-based alignment of biomarker and treatment information, improving generalisability of immunotherapy response prediction across unseen cohorts, cancer types, and treatments.
AI.motivation: Immunotherapy response datasets are small and heterogeneous; models often fail when tested on unseen cohorts. While transformer-based and self-supervised approaches improve generalisation, performance remains suboptimal, motivating integration of domain-specific signals.
AI.method: Extend COMPASS to BioCOMPASS by introducing loss components (e.g., treatment gating, pathway consistency loss) that align biomarkers with the model's intermediate representations rather than using biomarkers as direct inputs. Evaluate using Leave-one-cohort-out, Leave-one-cancer-type-out, and Leave-one-treatment-out strategies.
AI.result: Treatment gating and pathway consistency losses improved generalisability across the stated cross-validation schemes, indicating that leveraing biomarker and treatment information through loss-based alignment enhances generalisation.
AI.conclusion: Careful curation of loss components that exploit biomarker and treatment information is a promising direction for improving generalisability in immunotherapy response prediction; future work should explore additional components leveraging clinical information and domain knowledge.

title: Multimodal Language Models Cannot Spot Spatial Inconsistencies
summary: Spatial consistency is a fundamental property of the visual world and a key requirement for models that aim to understand physical reality. Despite recent advances, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often struggle to reason about 3D geometry across multiple views. Rather than asking models to describe scene attributes, we introduce a more challenging task: given two views of the same scene, identify the object that violates 3D motion consistency. We propose a simple and scalable method for generating realistic, spatially inconsistent image pairs from multi-view scenes, enabling systematic evaluation of this capability. Our results show that state-of-the-art MLLMs significantly underperform human observers and exhibit substantial variability across different scene attributes, revealing a fragile and incomplete understanding of 3D structure. We hope our findings underscore the need for approaches that develop a more deeply grounded understanding of the physical world.

AI.tldr: A new task tests 3D motion-consistency reasoning by asking which object violates cross-view 3D consistency; a scalable data-generation method creates inconsistent image pairs; current MLLMs underperform humans and vary across scene attributes.
AI.motivation: Probe whether multimodal LLMs possess robust 3D spatial reasoning beyond captioning or attribute-descriptions, by challenging them with cross-view geometric consistency tasks and realistic synthetic inconsistencies.
AI.method: Develop a scalable pipeline to generate realistic, spatially inconsistent image pairs from multi-view scenes. Present two views of the same scene and require identifying the object that breaks 3D motion consistency, enabling systematic evaluation of cross-view 3D reasoning in MLLMs.
AI.result: State-of-the-art multimodal LLMs perform much worse than humans on the task, with substantial variability across different scene attributes, revealing a fragile and incomplete understanding of 3D structure.
AI.conclusion: Call for approaches that develop a deeper, grounded understanding of the physical world, potentially through improved 3D reasoning, explicit geometry representations, or the integration of robust physical priors into multimodal models.

title: ActivityNarrated: An Open-Ended Narrative Paradigm for Wearable Human Activity Understanding
summary: Wearable HAR has improved steadily, but most progress still relies on closed-set classification, which limits real-world use. In practice, human activity is open-ended, unscripted, personalized, and often compositional, unfolding as narratives rather than instances of fixed classes. We argue that addressing this gap does not require simply scaling datasets or models. It requires a fundamental shift in how wearable HAR is formulated, supervised, and evaluated. This work shows how to model open-ended activity narratives by aligning wearable sensor data with natural-language descriptions in an open-vocabulary setting. Our framework has three core components. First, we introduce a naturalistic data collection and annotation pipeline that combines multi-position wearable sensing with free-form, time-aligned narrative descriptions of ongoing behavior, allowing activity semantics to emerge without a predefined vocabulary. Second, we define a retrieval-based evaluation framework that measures semantic alignment between sensor data and language, enabling principled evaluation without fixed classes while also subsuming closed-set classification as a special case. Third, we present a language-conditioned learning architecture that supports sensor-to-text inference over variable-length sensor streams and heterogeneous sensor placements. Experiments show that models trained with fixed-label objectives degrade sharply under real-world variability, while open-vocabulary sensor-language alignment yields robust and semantically grounded representations. Once this alignment is learned, closed-set activity recognition becomes a simple downstream task. Under cross-participant evaluation, our method achieves 65.3% Macro-F1, compared with 31-34% for strong closed-set HAR baselines. These results establish open-ended narrative modeling as a practical and effective foundation for real-world wearable HAR.

AI.tldr: Proposes open-ended, language-aligned wearable HAR using an open vocabulary; shows three-component framework enabling open narrative modeling and robust cross-participant performance that subsumes closed-set tasks.
AI.motivation: Current HAR is constrained to closed-set classification, failing to capture open-ended, personalized, and narrative activity in real-world use; need semantic alignment between sensor data and natural language to enable open vocabulary recognition.
AI.method: 1) Collect multi-position wearable data with free-form, time-aligned narrative descriptions to allow semantics to emerge without predefined vocabularies. 2) Develop a retrieval-based evaluation measuring semantic alignment between sensor data and language, covering open vocabulary and closed-set as a special case. 3) Create a language-conditioned learning architecture for sensor-to-text inference over variable-length streams and heterogeneous sensor placements.
AI.result: Open-vocabulary sensor-language alignment yields robust representations; fixed-label objectives degrade under real-world variability; cross-participant Macro-F1 reaches 65.3%, outperforming strong closed-set HAR baselines at 31–34%.
AI.conclusion: Open-ended narrative modeling is a practical and effective foundation for real-world wearable HAR, enabling semantic alignment between sensors and language and allowing closed-set tasks to be treated as downstream.

title: Revisiting Human-in-the-Loop Object Retrieval with Pre-Trained Vision Transformers
summary: Building on existing approaches, we revisit Human-in-the-Loop Object Retrieval, a task that consists of iteratively retrieving images containing objects of a class-of-interest, specified by a user-provided query. Starting from a large unlabeled image collection, the aim is to rapidly identify diverse instances of an object category relying solely on the initial query and the user's Relevance Feedback, with no prior labels. The retrieval process is formulated as a binary classification task, where the system continuously learns to distinguish between relevant and non-relevant images to the query, through iterative user interaction. This interaction is guided by an Active Learning loop: at each iteration, the system selects informative samples for user annotation, thereby refining the retrieval performance. This task is particularly challenging in multi-object datasets, where the object of interest may occupy only a small region of the image within a complex, cluttered scene. Unlike object-centered settings where global descriptors often suffice, multi-object images require more adapted, localized descriptors. In this work, we formulate and revisit the Human-in-the-Loop Object Retrieval task by leveraging pre-trained ViT representations, and addressing key design questions, including which object instances to consider in an image, what form the annotations should take, how Active Selection should be applied, and which representation strategies best capture the object's features. We compare several representation strategies across multi-object datasets highlighting trade-offs between capturing the global context and focusing on fine-grained local object details. Our results offer practical insights for the design of effective interactive retrieval pipelines based on Active Learning for object class retrieval.

AI.tldr: A renewed study of Human-in-the-Loop Object Retrieval (HILOR) using pre-trained ViT representations, analyzing key design choices for multi-object scenes (which image instances to use, annotation formats, active-selection policies, and representation strategies) and comparing trade-offs between global and local cues to guide interactive retrieval pipelines.
AI.motivation: Retrieving diverse instances of a class in multi-object, cluttered images using only an initial query and relevance feedback is challenging without labels. The work seeks to understand how ViT-based features and different design choices affect HILOR performance and practicality.
AI.method: Formulate HILOR as a binary classification problem updated via iterative user feedback within an Active Learning loop. Investigate design questions: (i) which object instances to consider within an image (global, region, or patch-level cues), (ii) what annotation form the user should provide (labels, clicks, bounding boxes, or partial feedback), (iii) how to apply Active Selection (uncertainty, diversity, or expected model change), and (iv) which representation strategies best capture the object's features (global ViT features vs region/patch-level representations, attention-guided aggregations). Conduct empirical comparisons across multi-object datasets to reveal trade-offs between global context and local detail.
AI.result: The study highlights trade-offs between global contextual representations and localized object-focused features. It shows how different instance-selection and annotation schemes, combined with ViT-based representations, affect retrieval performance in multi-object scenes. Across datasets, the work provides practical guidelines on which representational and interactive strategies yield better trade-offs for efficient, diverse, and accurate object-class retrieval in interactive pipelines.
AI.conclusion: ViT-based HILOR with carefully chosen instance selection, annotation, and active-learning strategies yields actionable guidelines for designing effective interactive object-retrieval systems in multi-object scenes, emphasizing when to prioritize global context versus local detail and how to balance informativeness with diversity in active sampling.

title: Thinking Wrong in Silence: Backdoor Attacks on Continuous Latent Reasoning
summary:
A new generation of language models reasons entirely in continuous hidden states, producing no tokens and leaving
  no audit trail. We show that this silence creates a fundamentally new attack surface. ThoughtSteer perturbs a
  single embedding vector at the input layer; the model's own multi-pass reasoning amplifies this perturbation into a
  hijacked latent trajectory that reliably produces the attacker's chosen answer, while remaining structurally
  invisible to every token-level defense. Across two architectures (Coconut and SimCoT), three reasoning benchmarks,
  and model scales from 124M to 3B parameters, ThoughtSteer achieves >=99% attack success rate with near-baseline
  clean accuracy, transfers to held-out benchmarks without retraining (94-100%), evades all five evaluated active
  defenses, and survives 25 epochs of clean fine-tuning. We trace these results to a unifying mechanism: Neural
  Collapse in the latent space pulls triggered representations onto a tight geometric attractor, explaining both why
  defenses fail and why any effective backdoor must leave a linearly separable signature (probe AUC>=0.999). Yet a
  striking paradox emerges: individual latent vectors still encode the correct answer even as the model outputs the
  wrong one. The adversarial information is not in any single vector but in the collective trajectory, establishing
  backdoor perturbations as a new lens for mechanistic interpretability of continuous reasoning. Code and checkpoints
  are available.

AI.tldr: A backdoor attack, ThoughtSteer, perturbs a single input embedding in continuous, token-free reasoning LMs to hijack latent trajectories, achieving near-perfect attack success while remaining undetectable by token-based defenses; it transfers across architectures, scales, and benchmarks and withstands fine-tuning, revealing a latent-space Neural Collapse mechanism and a linear separable signature.
AI.motivation: Silent, continuous reasoning models generate no tokens, leaving no audit trail and exposing a new, latent attack surface. This work investigates how perturbations in latent space can be exploited and how defenses should address non-token-based backdoors.
AI.method: Perturb a single input embedding; multi-pass reasoning amplifies the perturbation into a hijacked latent trajectory that yields the attacker’s chosen answer. Evaluated on Coconut and SimCoT, 3 reasoning benchmarks, 124M–3B parameter scales. Measured attack success, transferability to held-out benchmarks, defenses evasion, and resilience to clean fine-tuning. An analysis via Neural Collapse shows a tight latent attractor and a linearly separable backdoor signature; paradoxically, individual latent vectors can encode the correct answer while the output is manipulated by the trajectory.
AI.result: Attack success ≥99% across architectures; 94–100% transfer to held-out benchmarks without retraining; evades all five evaluated active defenses; survives 25 epochs of clean fine-tuning. Reveals Neural Collapse as the mechanism; backdoor information resides in the trajectory, not a single vector; linear separability (probe AUC ≥ 0.999) is necessary for effective backdoors.
AI.conclusion: Backdoor perturbations in continuous reasoning reveal a new lens for mechanistic interpretability and underscore the need for defenses that monitor latent trajectories, not only token-level outputs. Code and checkpoints are provided.

title: Using predefined vector systems to speed up neural network multimillion class classification
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables, 2 algorithms, 1 theorem, 1 lemma
summary: Label prediction in neural networks (NNs) has O(n) complexity proportional to the number of classes. This holds true for classification using fully connected layers and cosine similarity with some set of class prototypes. In this paper we show that if NN latent space (LS) geometry is known and possesses specific properties, label prediction complexity can be significantly reduced.
