This is achieved by associating label prediction with the O(1) complexity closest cluster center search in a vector system used as target for latent space configuration (LSC). The proposed method only requires finding indexes of several largest and lowest values in the embedding vector making it extremely computationally efficient. We show that the proposed method does not change NN training accuracy computational results. We also measure the time required by different computational stages of NN inference and label prediction on multiple datasets. The experiments show that the proposed method allows to achieve up to 11.6 times overall acceleration over conventional methods. Furthermore, the proposed method has unique properties which allow to predict the existence of new classes.

AI.tldr: Proposes an O(1) label prediction approach by matching embeddings against a known cluster-center structure in latent space, claiming unchanged training accuracy and significant speedups (up to 11.6x) with the ability to predict new classes.
AI.motivation: Label prediction in NN classifiers scales with the number of classes (O(n)), imposing inference-time bottlenecks. If the latent space geometry and a target vector system (LSC) have exploitable structure, label prediction can be accelerated without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: Represent class labels as centers in a vector system acting as targets for the latent space configuration. During inference, determine label by locating indices of a few largest and/or smallest values in the embedding vector, effectively an O(1) operation given fixed-size centers. This leverages known LS geometry and center-based prototypes to avoid full class-wise comparison.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation indicates up to 11.6× overall acceleration over conventional methods across multiple datasets, with no degradation in training-time or inference-time accuracy. The method also facilitates sensing the existence of new classes.
AI.conclusion: If the latent space geometry and center structure hold, label prediction can be drastically more efficient without loss of accuracy, enabling scalable classification and potential open-set/new-class discovery. Additional work could explore robustness, scalability to very large class sets, and sensitivity to LS mis-specification.

title: Multicentric thrombus segmentation using an attention-based recurrent network with gradual modality dropout
summary: Detecting and delineating tiny targets in 3D brain scans is a central yet under-addressed challenge in medical imaging.In ischemic stroke, for instance, the culprit thrombus is small, low-contrast, and variably expressed across modalities(e.g., susceptibility-weighted T2 blooming, diffusion restriction on DWI/ADC), while real-world multi-center dataintroduce domain shifts, anisotropy, and frequent missing sequences. We introduce a methodology that couples an attention-based recurrent segmentation network (UpAttLLSTM), a training schedule that progressively increases the difficulty of hetero-modal learning, with gradual modality dropout, UpAttLLSTM aggregates context across slices via recurrent units (2.5D) and uses attention gates to fuse complementary cues across available sequences, making it robust to anisotropy and class imbalance. Gradual modality dropout systematically simulates site heterogeneity,noise, and missing modalities during training, acting as both augmentation and regularization to improve multi-center generalization. On a monocentric cohort, our approach detects thrombi in >90% of cases with a Dice score of 0.65. In a multi-center setting with missing modalities, it achieves-80% detection with a Dice score around 0.35. Beyond stroke, the proposed methodology directly transfers to other small-lesion tasks in 3D medical imaging where targets are scarce, subtle, and modality-dependent

AI.tldr: Attention-based recurrent 2.5D segmentation with gradual modality dropout for robust tiny target detection in 3D brain MRI, achieving Dice 0.65 monocentric and ~0.35 with missing modalities in multi-center data.
AI.motivation: Tiny targets in ischemic stroke are small, low-contrast, and modality-dependent; real-world multi-center data introduce domain shifts, anisotropy, and missing sequences; need robust cross-modal integration.
AI.method: UpAttLLSTM: attention-guided recurrent segmentation across slices (2.5D). Progressive hetero-modal learning schedule with gradual modality dropout. Attention gates fuse complementary cues across available sequences; robust to anisotropy and class imbalance.
AI.result: Monocentric Dice 0.65 with >90% detection; multi-center Dice ~0.35 with ~80% detection when modalities missing.
AI.conclusion: The approach generalizes to other small-lesion tasks in 3D medical imaging with scarce, subtle, modality-dependent targets.

title: Scalable Pretraining of Large Mixture of Experts Language Models on Aurora Super Computer
summary: Pretraining Large Language Models (LLMs) from scratch requires massive amount of compute. Aurora super computer is an ExaScale machine with 127,488 Intel PVC (Ponte Vechio) GPU tiles. In this work, we showcase LLM pretraining on Aurora at the scale of 1000s of GPU tiles. Towards this effort, we developed Optimus, an inhouse training library with support for standard large model training techniques. Using Optimus, we first pretrained Mula-1B, a 1 Billion dense model and Mula-7B-A1B, a 7 Billion Mixture of Experts (MoE) model from scratch on 3072 GPU tiles for the full 4 trillion tokens of the OLMoE-mix-0924 dataset. We then demonstrated model scaling by pretraining three large MoE models Mula-20B-A2B, Mula-100B-A7B, and Mula-220B-A10B till 100 Billion tokens on the same dataset. On our largest model Mula-220B-A10B, we pushed the compute scaling from 384 to 12288 GPU tiles and observed scaling efficiency of around 90% at 12288 GPU tiles. We significantly improved the runtime performance of MoE models using custom GPU kernels for expert computation, and a novel EP-Aware sharded optimizer resulting in training speedups up to 1.71x. As part of the Optimus library, we also developed a robust set of reliability and fault tolerant features to improve training stability and continuity at scale.

AI.tldr: Scales LLM pretraining to exascale hardware (Aurora) using Optimus; demonstrates dense and MoE training from 1B to 220B parameters; achieves ~90% efficiency at 12288 tiles and up to 1.71x speedups with EP-aware sharded optimizer and custom kernels.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable, reliable LLM pretraining on ExaScale infrastructure, pushing model sizes and dataset tokens while addressing performance bottlenecks.
AI.method: Develop Optimus library; pretrain 1B dense (Mula-1B) and 7B MoE (Mula-7B-A1B) on 3072 tiles for 4T tokens; extend to larger MoEs Mula-20B-A2B, Mula-100B-A7B, Mula-220B-A10B up to 100B tokens on Aurora; employ custom GPU kernels for expert computation and an EP-Aware sharded optimizer; incorporate reliability and fault-tolerance features.
AI.result: Achieved compute scaling from 384 to 12288 tiles with ~90% efficiency; trained MoE models up to 220B to 100B tokens; observed training speedups up to 1.71x due to optimized kernels and EP-aware optimizer; improved training reliability at scale.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of exascale MoE LLM pretraining using Optimus, delivering strong scaling, speedups, and robustness to support future large-scale AI workloads.

title: Continual Vision-Language Learning for Remote Sensing: Benchmarking and Analysis
summary: Current remote sensing vision-language models (RS VLMs) demonstrate impressive performance in image interpretation but rely on static training data, limiting their ability to accommodate continuously emerging sensing modalities and downstream tasks. This exposes a fundamental challenge: enabling RS VLMs to continually adapt without catastrophic forgetting. Despite its practical importance, the continual learning capability of RS VLMs remains underexplored, and no dedicated benchmark currently exists. In this work, we present CLeaRS, a comprehensive benchmark for continual vision-language learning in remote sensing. CLeaRS comprises 10 curated subsets with over 207k image-text pairs, spanning diverse interpretation tasks, sensing modalities, and application scenarios. We further define three evaluation protocols: long-horizon, modality-incremental, and task-incremental settings, to systematically assess continual adaptation. Extensive benchmarking of diverse vision-language models reveals catastrophic forgetting across all settings. Moreover, representative continual learning methods, when adapted to RS VLMs, exhibit limited effectiveness in handling task, instruction, and modality transitions. Our findings underscore the need for developing continual learning methods tailored to RS VLMs.

AI.tldr: CLeaRS introduces a benchmark for continual learning in remote-sensing vision-language models (RS VLMs). It provides 10 subsets with over 207k image-text pairs and three evaluation protocols (long-horizon, modality-incremental, task-incremental). Empirical results show catastrophic forgetting across settings and limited effectiveness of existing continual learning methods, highlighting the need for RS-tailored CL approaches.
AI.motivation: RS VLMs currently rely on static training data and struggle to adapt to new sensing modalities and tasks without forgetting. There is no dedicated benchmark for continual learning in this domain, hindering systematic evaluation.
AI.method: Construction of the CLeaRS benchmark comprising 10 curated subsets totaling >207k image-text pairs across diverse sensing modalities and tasks. Definition of three evaluation protocols (long-horizon, modality-incremental, task-incremental). Benchmarking of diverse RS VLMs and adaptation of representative continual learning methods to this domain.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation reveals catastrophic forgetting in RS VLMs under all three settings. Representative CL methods, when applied to RS VLMs, show limited effectiveness in managing task, instruction, and modality transitions.
AI.conclusion: There is a clear need for developing continual learning methods specifically tailored for RS VLMs to enable robust, continual adaptation to new modalities and tasks.

title: MIRANDA: MId-feature RANk-adversarial Domain Adaptation toward climate change-robust ecological forecasting with deep learning
summary: Plant phenology modelling aims to predict the timing of seasonal phases, such as leaf-out or flowering, from meteorological time series. Reliable predictions are crucial for anticipating ecosystem responses to climate change. While phenology modelling has traditionally relied on mechanistic approaches, deep learning methods have recently been proposed as flexible, data-driven alternatives with often superior performance. However, mechanistic models tend to outperform deep networks when data distribution shifts are induced by climate change. Domain Adaptation (DA) techniques could help address this limitation. Yet, unlike standard DA settings, climate change induces a temporal continuum of domains and involves both a covariate and label shift, with warmer records and earlier start of spring. To tackle this challenge, we introduce Mid-feature Rank-adversarial Domain Adaptation (MIRANDA). Whereas conventional adversarial methods enforce domain invariance on final latent representations, an approach that does not explicitly address label shift, we apply adversarial regularization to intermediate features. Moreover, instead of a binary domain-classification objective, we employ a rank-based objective that enforces year-invariance in the learned meteorological representations. On a country-scale dataset spanning 70 years and comprising 67,800 phenological observations of 5 tree species, we demonstrate that, unlike conventional DA approaches, MIRANDA improves robustness to climatic distribution shifts and narrows the performance gap with mechanistic models.

AI.tldr: MIRANDA is a domain adaptation method for plant phenology that uses mid-feature adversarial regularization with a rank-based objective to enforce year-invariance, addressing climate-driven covariate and label shifts and narrowing the gap with mechanistic models on a 70-year, multi-species dataset.
AI.motivation: Climate change creates a temporal continuum of domains with both covariate and label shifts (e.g., warmer temperatures, earlier springs), which degrades deep learning phenology models. Conventional DA focusing on final representations may fail under label shift; a method that aligns mid-feature representations across years could improve robustness.
AI.method: Introduce Mid-feature Rank-adversarial Domain Adaptation (MIRANDA). Apply adversarial regularization to intermediate (mid) features rather than final latent representations. Use a rank-based objective, rather than a binary domain classifier, to enforce year-invariance in meteorological representations. Evaluate on a country-scale dataset with 70 years of data (67,800 phenological observations) across 5 tree species.
AI.result: MIRANDA improves robustness to climate-driven distribution shifts and narrows the performance gap between deep learning models and mechanistic phenology models, outperforming conventional DA approaches on the tested dataset.
AI.conclusion: Mid-feature adversarial alignment with a rank-based, year-invariant objective is a promising direction for DA in temporally evolving phenology tasks, offering enhanced robustness to climate change and closer alignment with mechanistic models.

title: Video Patch Pruning: Efficient Video Instance Segmentation via Early Token Reduction
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated state-ofthe-art performance in several benchmarks, yet their high computational costs hinders their practical deployment. Patch Pruning offers significant savings, but existing approaches restrict token reduction to deeper layers, leaving early-stage compression unexplored. This limits their potential for holistic efficiency. In this work, we present a novel Video Patch Pruning framework (VPP) that integrates temporal prior knowledge to enable efficient sparsity within early ViT layers. Our approach is motivated by the observation that prior features extracted from deeper layers exhibit strong foreground selectivity. Therefore we propose a fully differentiable module for temporal mapping to accurately select the most relevant patches in early network stages. Notably, the proposed method enables a patch reduction of up to 60% in dense prediction tasks, exceeding the capabilities of conventional image-based patch pruning, which typically operate around a 30% patch sparsity. VPP excels the high-sparsity regime, sustaining remarkable performance even when patch usage is reduced below 55%. Specifically, it preserves stable results with a maximal performance drop of 0.6% on the Youtube-VIS 2021 dataset.

AI.tldr: Video Patch Pruning (VPP) uses a differentiable temporal mapping module to prune patches in early ViT layers using temporal priors, enabling up to 60% patch sparsity with minimal accuracy loss on video tasks.
AI.motivation: ViTs are accurate but computationally heavy; existing patch pruning mostly targets deeper layers, missing early-stage efficiency; leveraging temporal priors from deeper features can guide early patch selection for holistic efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce a fully differentiable temporal mapping module that transfers knowledge from deeper representations to select the most relevant patches in early ViT layers, enabling high sparsity in video tasks.
AI.result: Achieves up to 60% patch sparsity in dense prediction tasks, outperforming image-based pruning which is typically around 30%; preserves performance under high sparsity, with a maximum 0.6% accuracy drop on YouTube-VIS 2021.
AI.conclusion: Temporal priors enable effective early-stage patch pruning in ViTs for video, delivering substantial efficiency gains with minimal performance loss and showing promise for practical deployment.

title: Routing-Free Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Standard Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models rely on centralized routing mechanisms that introduce rigid inductive biases. We propose Routing-Free MoE which eliminates any hard-coded centralized designs including external routers, Softmax, Top-K and load balancing, instead encapsulating all activation functionalities within individual experts and directly optimized through continuous gradient flow, enabling each expert to determine its activation entirely on its own. We introduce a unified adaptive load-balancing framework to simultaneously optimize both expert-balancing and token-balancing objectives through a configurable interpolation, allowing flexible and customizable resource allocation. Extensive experiments show that Routing-Free MoE can consistently outperform baselines with better scalability and robustness. We analyze its behavior in detail and offer insights that may facilitate future MoE design ad optimization.

AI.tldr: Routing-Free MoE eliminates centralized routing (including external routers, Softmax, Top-K, and load-balancing) and lets each expert govern its activation; it introduces a unified adaptive load-balancing framework with interpolated objectives to balance expert and token usage, yielding better scalability and robustness across tasks.
AI.motivation: Centralized MoE routing imposes rigid inductive biases and bottlenecks; to unlock more flexible, scalable, and robust models, activation decisions should be made at the expert level without hard routing, enabling end-to-end differentiable optimization.
AI.method: Remove external routers and hard routing heuristics; encapsulate activation logic within individual experts; enable continuous gradient flow for expert activations; introduce a unified adaptive load-balancing framework that jointly optimizes expert-balancing and token-balancing via a configurable interpolation parameter, allowing flexible resource allocation; operate without Softmax/Top-K and explicit load balancing.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show Routing-Free MoE consistently outperforms baselines, with better scalability and robustness; ablation and analysis offer insights into behavior and design implications for future MoE systems.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability and benefits of fully decentralized MoE routing; the adaptive load-balancing framework enables flexible resource allocation and provides actionable guidance for future MoE design and optimization.

title: LinguDistill: Recovering Linguistic Ability in Vision- Language Models via Selective Cross-Modal Distillation
summary: Adapting pretrained language models (LMs) into vision-language models (VLMs) can degrade their native linguistic capability due to representation shift and cross-modal interference introduced during multimodal adaptation. Such loss is difficult to recover, even with targeted task-specific fine-tuning using standard objectives. Prior recovery approaches typically introduce additional modules that act as intermediate alignment layers to maintain or isolate modality-specific subspaces, which increases architectural complexity, adds parameters at inference time, and limits flexibility across models and settings. We propose LinguDistill, an adapter-free distillation method that restores linguistic capability by utilizing the original frozen LM as a teacher. We overcome the key challenge of enabling vision-conditioned teacher supervision by introducing layer-wise KV-cache sharing, which exposes the teacher to the student's multimodal representations without modifying the architecture of either model. We then selectively distill the teacher's strong linguistic signal on language-intensive data to recover language capability, while preserving the student's visual grounding on multimodal tasks. As a result, LinguDistill recovers $\sim$10% of the performance lost on language and knowledge benchmarks, while maintaining comparable performance on vision-heavy tasks. Our findings demonstrate that linguistic capability can be recovered without additional modules, providing an efficient and practical solution to modality-specific degradation in multimodal models.

AI.tldr: LinguDistill is an adapter-free distillation approach that recovers linguistic capabilities in vision-language models after LM adaptation by using the frozen LM as a teacher with layer-wise KV-cache sharing, selectively distilling language-heavy data to recover language performance without harming vision grounding.
AI.motivation: Adapting pretrained LMs into VLMs degrades native linguistic capability due to representation shift and cross-modal interference; existing recovery methods add complexity via extra modules. A simple, efficient method that preserves language without increasing model size is needed.
AI.method: Use the frozen LM as a teacher and enable vision-conditioned supervision by layer-wise KV-cache sharing, allowing the student and teacher to share multimodal representations without modifying architectures. Perform selective distillation focusing on language-intensive data to recover language capabilities while maintaining visual grounding.
AI.result: Recovered about 10% of the performance lost on language and knowledge benchmarks; maintained comparable performance on vision-heavy tasks.
AI.conclusion: LinguDistill demonstrates that linguistic capability can be recovered without additional modules, offering an efficient and practical solution to modality-specific degradation in multimodal models.

title: Cost-Penalized Fitness in FMA-Orchestrated Mixture of Experts: Experimental Evidence for Molecular Memory in Domain Adaptation
summary: We present experimental results from seven controlled runs of nanoFMT, a Free-Market Algorithm (FMA) orchestrated transformer with dynamic Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) management. The experiments address a fundamental question for advanced LLM development: how should an MoE system manage its expert pool when operating at full capacity under changing data distributions? We demonstrate that cost-penalized fitness metrics, combined with a linear grace period for newborn experts, produce a system that accumulates domain expertise through diversification rather than replacement. The central result is a round-trip domain shift experiment showing 9-11x faster recovery when returning to a previously learned domain, with zero expert births or replacements required. This "molecular memory" effect -- where dormant experts survive and reactivate when their domain returns -- has no analogue in current MoE management approaches. A preliminary cost analysis estimates annual savings of $39.1M and 27.1 GWh energy reduction for an OpenAI-scale provider under a moderate scenario.

AI.tldr: Cost-penalized MoE management with dormant experts yields molecular memory: 9-11x faster domain recovery with no births/replacements; potential large-scale cost/energy savings.
AI.motivation: To determine how to manage a large pool of MoE experts under full capacity and evolving data distributions, preserving domain knowledge while reducing maintenance overhead.
AI.method: Seven controlled nanoFMT runs using a Free-Market Algorithm for dynamic MoE management; employ cost-penalized fitness and a linear grace period for newborn experts; perform round-trip domain-shift experiments to test memory effects; report recovery speed and absence of births/replacements; provide preliminary cost-energy estimates.
AI.result: Demonstrated 9-11x faster recovery when returning to a previously learned domain; dormant experts reactivate without births/replacements; evidence of a 'molecular memory' effect; preliminary annual savings estimated at $39.1M and 27.1 GWh energy reduction for an OpenAI-scale provider under a moderate scenario.
AI.conclusion: The observed molecular memory indicates dormant experts can preserve and reactivate domain-specific capabilities, enabling rapid adaptation and substantial cost/energy benefits; this challenges conventional MoE management that relies on frequent births/replacements and suggests new strategies for memory retention and expert lifecycle design.

title: Disentangling to Re-couple: Resolving the Similarity-Controllability Paradox in Subject-Driven Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Subject-Driven Text-to-Image (T2I) Generation aims to preserve a subject's identity while editing its context based on a text prompt. A core challenge in this task is the "similarity-controllability paradox", where enhancing textual control often degrades the subject's fidelity, and vice-versa. We argue this paradox stems from the ambiguous role of text prompts, which are often tasked with describing both the subject and the desired modifications, leading to conflicting signals for the model. To resolve this, we propose DisCo, a novel framework that first Disntangles and then re-Couples visual and textual information. First, our textual-visual decoupling module isolates the sources of information: subject identity is extracted exclusively from the reference image with the entity word of the subject, while the text prompt is simplified to contain only the modification command, where the subject refers to general pronouns, eliminating descriptive ambiguity. However, this strict separation can lead to unnatural compositions between the subject and its contexts. We address this by designing a dedicated reward signal and using reinforcement learning to seamlessly recouple the visually-defined subject and the textually-generated context. Our approach effectively resolves the paradox, enabling simultaneous high-fidelity subject preservation and precise textual control. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, producing highly realistic and coherent images.

AI.tldr: DisCo addresses the similarity-controllability paradox in subject-driven T2I by decoupling subject identity from modification prompts and then recoupling via RL with a task-specific reward, achieving state-of-the-art fidelity and controllability.
AI.motivation: Prompts that describe both the subject and desired modifications create conflicting signals, limiting both fidelity to the subject and precision of textual control. Decoupling aims to separate sources of information to improve both aspects.
AI.method: Textual-visual decoupling: extract subject identity exclusively from the reference image using an entity word for the subject; simplify the text prompt to only contain the modification command (subject referred to with general pronouns). Then use a dedicated reward signal and reinforcement learning to recouple the visually-defined subject with the textually-generated context, ensuring natural composition.
AI.result: Claims state-of-the-art performance with high-fidelity subject preservation and precise textual control, supported by extensive experiments showing realistic and coherent images.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling visual and textual information followed by RL-based recoupling resolves the similarity-controllability paradox, enabling simultaneous subject fidelity and controllability in T2I generation.

title: Optimal Brain Decomposition for Accurate LLM Low-Rank Approximation
summary: Low-rank decomposition has emerged as an important problem in Large Language Model (LLM) fine-tuning and inference. Through Singular Value Decomposition (SVD), the weight matrix can be factorized into low-rank spaces optimally. Previously, a common practice was to decompose the weight in the activation-whitened space, and then achieve satisfying results. In this work, we propose Optimal Brain Decomposition LLM (OBD-LLM), which studies the decomposition problem in the model space by utilizing second-order Hessian information. Through a rigorous Kronecker-factorization of the Hessian, we show that the decomposition needs to consider both input and output information of the layer, and achieves much better decomposition results compared to input only method. Our loss-aware decomposition method involves a bi-directional whitening on the weight matrix. As a result, OBD-LLM is a closed-form solution for the optimal decomposition of weights in the language model. Remarkably, we achieve ~20-40\% better results than previous state-of-the-art decomposition methods, the SVD-LLM.

AI.tldr: OBD-LLM introduces a Hessian-informed, loss-aware weight decomposition in model space using Kronecker-factorization and bi-directional whitening, yielding a closed-form optimal low-rank decomposition that outperforms SVD-LLM by ~20-40%.
AI.motivation: To improve low-rank decomposition for LLMs beyond activation-space (input whitening) methods by incorporating second-order curvature information and full layer input/output statistics, aiming for better decomposition and downstream performance during fine-tuning and inference.
AI.method: Leverages second-order Hessian information with Kronecker-factorization to model-space decomposition. Demonstrates that both input and output statistics are essential. Proposes a loss-aware, bi-directional whitening of the weight matrix, yielding a closed-form solution for optimal weight decomposition (OBD-LLM).
AI.result: Empirically achieves 20-40% improvements over the prior SVD-LLM decomposition method, and surpasses input-only decomposition baselines.
AI.conclusion: Model-space, Hessian-informed, loss-aware decomposition with bi-directional whitening provides a superior, closed-form low-rank factorization for LLMs, highlighting the importance of incorporating second-order information and full-layer statistics over traditional input-only approaches.

title: MotionGrounder: Grounded Multi-Object Motion Transfer via Diffusion Transformer
comment: Please visit our project page at https://kaist-viclab.github.io/motiongrounder-site/
summary: Motion transfer enables controllable video generation by transferring temporal dynamics from a reference video to synthesize a new video conditioned on a target caption. However, existing Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based methods are limited to single-object videos, restricting fine-grained control in real-world scenes with multiple objects. In this work, we introduce MotionGrounder, a DiT-based framework that firstly handles motion transfer with multi-object controllability. Our Flow-based Motion Signal (FMS) in MotionGrounder provides a stable motion prior for target video generation, while our Object-Caption Alignment Loss (OCAL) grounds object captions to their corresponding spatial regions. We further propose a new Object Grounding Score (OGS), which jointly evaluates (i) spatial alignment between source video objects and their generated counterparts and (ii) semantic consistency between each generated object and its target caption. Our experiments show that MotionGrounder consistently outperforms recent baselines across quantitative, qualitative, and human evaluations.

AI.tldr: A DiT-based multi-object motion-transfer framework, MotionGrounder, leveraging Flow-based Motion Signals, OCAL, and a new Object Grounding Score to achieve stable, grounded multi-object video generation conditioned on captions and outperform baselines.
AI.motivation: Current DiT-based motion transfer methods are restricted to single-object videos, hindering fine-grained control in real-world scenes with multiple objects; there is a need for effective grounding of captions to spatial regions and stable motion priors for multi-object generation.
AI.method: Introduce Flow-based Motion Signal (FMS) as a stable motion prior; Object-Caption Alignment Loss (OCAL) to align object captions with their spatial regions; propose Object Grounding Score (OGS) to jointly assess spatial alignment between source objects and generated objects, and semantic consistency with the target captions; build MotionGrounder as a DiT-based framework enabling multi-object motion transfer with these components.
AI.result: MotionGrounder consistently outperforms recent baselines across quantitative metrics, qualitative assessments, and human evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides stable, grounded multi-object motion transfer for video generation, expanding DiT-based capabilities to multi-object scenes and improving alignment between objects and captions.

title: Learning to Learn-at-Test-Time: Language Agents with Learnable Adaptation Policies
summary: Test-Time Learning (TTL) enables language agents to iteratively refine their performance through repeated interactions with the environment at inference time. At the core of TTL is an adaptation policy that updates the actor policy based on experience from previous episodes, thereby improving future behavior. Existing methods rely on fixed, hand-crafted adaptation policies rather than optimizing them for downstream improvement. We argue that optimal adaptation policies should be learned from task environments, not hand-engineered based on human intuition. To achieve this, we introduce Meta-TTL, a framework that formulates the discovery of effective adaptation policies as a bi-level optimization problem. Within this framework, the inner loop executes the standard TTL process, measuring how effectively a candidate adaptation policy helps an agent correct errors across sequential episodes. Guided by the agent's performance, the outer loop employs evolutionary search over a diverse distribution of training tasks to iteratively refine the adaptation policy. We evaluate Meta-TTL on Jericho and WebArena-Lite across both in-distribution (ID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) settings, using multiple meta-agent backbones. Results on both benchmarks show that Meta-TTL consistently outperforms hand-crafted baselines, suggesting that the optimized adaptation policy encodes transferable strategies that generalize beyond the training task distribution.

AI.tldr: Meta-TTL learns a transferable test-time adaptation policy via bi-level optimization; the inner loop runs the TTL process to assess how a candidate policy improves error correction across episodes, while the outer loop uses evolutionary search over diverse training tasks to optimize the policy. Evaluations on Jericho and WebArena-Lite show consistent improvement over hand-crafted TTL baselines in both ID and OOD settings, across multiple meta-agent backbones.
AI.motivation: Current test-time learning (TTL) relies on fixed, hand-crafted adaptation policies that are not optimized for downstream performance or generalization. Learning adaptation policies from task environments could yield policies that generalize beyond the training distribution.
AI.method: Formulates the discovery of effective adaptation policies as a bi-level optimization problem. The inner loop executes standard TTL with a candidate adaptation policy and measures its effectiveness in correcting errors across sequential episodes. The outer loop uses evolutionary search over a diverse distribution of training tasks to iteratively refine the adaptation policy. Evaluations are conducted on Jericho and WebArena-Lite with multiple meta-agent backbones.
AI.result: Meta-TTL consistently outperforms hand-crafted baselines on both benchmarks in both in-distribution (ID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) settings, suggesting the learned adaptation policy encodes transferable strategies that generalize beyond the training task distribution.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing adaptation policies via a bi-level, evolutionary-search framework yields transferable, generalizable strategies for test-time learning, improving performance over hand-crafted adaptation policies across benchmarks and backbones.

title: Perturb-and-Restore: Simulation-driven Structural Augmentation Framework for Imbalance Chromosomal Anomaly Detection
comment: This preprint version of the manuscript has been submitted to the IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics (JBHI) for review
summary: Detecting structural chromosomal abnormalities is crucial for accurate diagnosis and management of genetic disorders. However, collecting sufficient structural abnormality data is extremely challenging and costly in clinical practice, and not all abnormal types can be readily collected. As a result, deep learning approaches face significant performance degradation due to the severe imbalance and scarcity of abnormal chromosome data. To address this challenge, we propose a Perturb-and-Restore (P&R), a simulation-driven structural augmentation framework that effectively alleviates data imbalance in chromosome anomaly detection. The P&R framework comprises two key components: (1) Structure Perturbation and Restoration Simulation, which generates synthetic abnormal chromosomes by perturbing chromosomal banding patterns of normal chromosomes followed by a restoration diffusion network that reconstructs continuous chromosome content and edges, thus eliminating reliance on rare abnormal samples; and (2) Energy-guided Adaptive Sampling, an energy score-based online selection strategy that dynamically prioritizes high-quality synthetic samples by referencing the energy distribution of real samples. To evaluate our method, we construct a comprehensive structural anomaly dataset consisting of over 260,000 chromosome images, including 4,242 abnormal samples spanning 24 categories. Experimental results demonstrate that the P&R framework achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, surpassing existing methods with an average improvement of 8.92% in sensitivity, 8.89% in precision, and 13.79% in F1-score across all categories.

AI.tldr: A simulation-driven augmentation framework (P&R) for chromosomal anomaly detection to address data imbalance by perturbing normal chromosome banding to synthesize abnormals, restoring them with a diffusion network, and using energy-guided adaptive sampling to select high-quality synthetic samples, achieving SOTA improvements on a large dataset.
AI.motivation: Deep learning performance suffers from severe imbalance and scarcity of abnormal chromosome data; synthetic data generation and efficient sample selection are needed to improve detection accuracy.
AI.method: Structure Perturbation and Restoration Simulation: perturb banding patterns of normal chromosomes to synthesize abnormalities, followed by a restoration diffusion network that reconstructs continuous content and edges. Energy-guided Adaptive Sampling: online selection based on an energy score aligned with real-sample energy distribution to prioritize high-quality synthetic samples.
AI.result: On a dataset of over 260k chromosome images, including 4,242 abnormal samples across 24 categories, the method achieves state-of-the-art performance with avg gains of 8.92% in sensitivity, 8.89% in precision, and 13.79% in F1-score across categories.
AI.conclusion: The Perturb-and-Restore framework effectively mitigates data imbalance for chromosome anomaly detection and yields substantial performance gains; the approach demonstrates promise for scalable, data-efficient anomaly detection in cytogenetics.

title: Policy Improvement Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a central post-training paradigm for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models. Yet existing methods share a common blind spot: they optimize policies based on instantaneous group-level or batch-level statistics without ever verifying whether the resulting update actually improved the model. This open-loop design -- updating in isolation at each step, guided only by within-group (batch) reward signals -- means optimization can drift or collapse with no mechanism to detect and correct these failures. We argue that the missing ingredient is policy improvement feedback: the ability to measure and optimize inter-iteration progress directly. To this end, we introduce Policy Improvement Reinforcement Learning (PIRL), a framework that replaces surrogate reward maximization with the explicit objective of maximizing cumulative policy improvement across iterations, and prove this temporal objective is perfectly aligned with maximizing final task performance. Building on PIRL, we propose Policy Improvement Policy Optimization (PIPO), which implements closed-loop optimization through retrospective verification. At each iteration, PIPO evaluates whether the previous update yielded genuine improvement against a sliding-window historical baseline, then actively reinforces beneficial updates and suppresses the harmful ones -- transforming an open-loop process into a self-correcting one. We provide theoretical analysis showing that PIPO performs ascent on the PIRL objective in expectation, and experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate improved stability and performance over GRPO and its variants.

AI.tldr: PIRL replaces surrogate reward maximization with a cumulative policy-improvement objective, enabling closed-loop, retrospective verification via PIPO; theory shows ascent in expectation and experiments show improved stability over GRPO on math reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Current RL post-training methods optimize batch-level statistics without verifying actual policy improvement across iterations, risking drift or collapse. A mechanism to assess inter-iteration progress is needed to ensure final task performance.
AI.method: Introduce Policy Improvement Reinforcement Learning (PIRL) as the objective to maximize cumulative policy improvement over iterations. Build Policy Improvement Policy Optimization (PIPO) that uses retrospective verification against a sliding-window baseline to accept or suppress updates, turning open-loop updates into a self-correcting loop.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis demonstrates ascent on the PIRL objective in expectation. Empirical results on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show improved stability and performance relative to GRPO and its variants.
AI.conclusion: PIRL-PIPO provides a principled framework to ensure policy updates actually yield improvement, aligning training with final performance and reducing instability of open-loop RL post-training approaches.

title: Sparkle: A Robust and Versatile Representation for Point Cloud based Human Motion Capture
summary: Point cloud-based motion capture leverages rich spatial geometry and privacy-preserving sensing, but learning robust representations from noisy, unstructured point clouds remains challenging. Existing approaches face a struggle trade-off between point-based methods (geometrically detailed but noisy) and skeleton-based ones (robust but oversimplified). We address the fundamental challenge: how to construct an effective representation for human motion capture that can balance expressiveness and robustness. In this paper, we propose Sparkle, a structured representation unifying skeletal joints and surface anchors with explicit kinematic-geometric factorization. Our framework, SparkleMotion, learns this representation through hierarchical modules embedding geometric continuity and kinematic constraints. By explicitly disentangling internal kinematic structure from external surface geometry, SparkleMotion achieves state-of-the-art performance not only in accuracy but crucially in robustness and generalization under severe domain shifts, noise, and occlusion. Extensive experiments demonstrate our superiority across diverse sensor types and challenging real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: Sparkle unifies skeletal joints and surface anchors into a kinematic-geometric factorized representation (Sparkle) for point-cloud motion capture, achieving strong accuracy and robustness across sensors, noise, and occlusion.
AI.motivation: Existing point-based and skeleton-based methods trade expressiveness for robustness. No representation cleanly balances geometry, kinematic structure, and privacy-preserving sensing under noise and domain shifts.
AI.method: Introduce Sparkle, a structured representation that couples joints and surface anchors with explicit kinematic-geometric factorization. SparkleMotion uses hierarchical modules enforcing geometric continuity and kinematic constraints, disentangling internal kinematic structure from external surface geometry to learn a robust motion representation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in accuracy, robustness, and generalization under domain shifts, noise, and occlusion. Demonstrated across diverse sensor types and challenging real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling kinematics from surface geometry yields robust, generalizable motion capture representations. SparkleMotion offers a principled framework with strong cross-domain performance and privacy-preserving potential; future work may explore broader motion types and sensor configurations.

title: Super-Resolving Coarse-Resolution Weather Forecasts With Flow Matching
summary: Machine learning-based weather forecasting models now surpass state-of-the-art numerical weather prediction systems, but training and operating these models at high spatial resolution remains computationally expensive. We present a modular framework that decouples forecasting from spatial resolution by applying learned generative super-resolution as a post-processing step to coarse-resolution forecast trajectories. We formulate super-resolution as a stochastic inverse problem, using a residual formulation to preserve large-scale structure while reconstructing unresolved variability. The model is trained with flow matching exclusively on reanalysis data and is applied to global medium-range forecasts. We evaluate (i) design consistency by re-coarsening super-resolved forecasts and comparing them to the original coarse trajectories, and (ii) high-resolution forecast quality using standard ensemble verification metrics and spectral diagnostics. Results show that super-resolution preserves large-scale structure and variance after re-coarsening, introduces physically consistent small-scale variability, and achieves competitive probabilistic forecast skill at 0.25° resolution relative to an operational ensemble baseline, while requiring only a modest additional training cost compared with end-to-end high-resolution forecasting.

AI.tldr: Generative super-resolution is used as a post-processing step on coarse forecast trajectories to produce high-resolution forecasts with competitive probabilistic skill, while preserving large-scale structure and adding plausible small-scale variability at modest training cost.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational burden of high-resolution weather forecasting by decoupling resolution from the forecast model, enabling efficient ensemble predictions at 0.25° resolution.
AI.method: A modular post-processing framework applying learned generative super-resolution as a stochastic inverse problem with a residual formulation. It uses flow-matching trained exclusively on reanalysis data and is applied to global medium-range forecasts. Evaluation includes design consistency via re-coarsening and standard ensemble verification plus spectral diagnostics.
AI.result: Large-scale structure and variance are preserved after re-coarsening; small-scale variability is physically consistent; probabilistic forecast skill at 0.25° is competitive with an operational ensemble baseline; training cost is modest compared with end-to-end high-resolution forecasting.
AI.conclusion: Generative super-resolution as a post-processing step effectively enables high-resolution ensemble forecasts at reduced cost, maintaining structure and offering competitive skill; promising for scalable operational deployment with further refinements.

title: Shape Representation using Gaussian Process mixture models
summary: Traditional explicit 3D representations, such as point clouds and meshes, demand significant storage to capture fine geometric details and require complex indexing systems for surface lookups, making functional representations an efficient, compact, and continuous alternative. In this work, we propose a novel, object-specific functional shape representation that models surface geometry with Gaussian Process (GP) mixture models. Rather than relying on computationally heavy neural architectures, our method is lightweight, leveraging GPs to learn continuous directional distance fields from sparsely sampled point clouds. We capture complex topologies by anchoring local GP priors at strategic reference points, which can be flexibly extracted using any structural decomposition method (e.g. skeletonization, distance-based clustering). Extensive evaluations on the ShapeNetCore and IndustryShapes datasets demonstrate that our method can efficiently and accurately represent complex geometries.

AI.tldr: Functional GP mixture model for object-specific surface representation; lightweight, continuous directional distance fields learned from sparse point clouds; local GP priors anchored at reference points via skeletonization or clustering; validated on ShapeNetCore and IndustryShapes.
AI.motivation: Traditional explicit 3D representations (points, meshes) are storage-intensive and require complex indexing. A compact, continuous, functional representation can capture geometry efficiently and robustly, especially for complex topologies.
AI.method: Proposes an object-specific functional shape representation using Gaussian Process mixture models to learn directional distance fields. Local GP priors are anchored at strategic reference points extracted through structure-agnostic methods (e.g., skeletonization, distance-based clustering). The approach avoids heavy neural architectures in favor of a lightweight GP-based framework.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on ShapeNetCore and IndustryShapes datasets show that the method can efficiently and accurately represent complex geometries from sparsely sampled data.
AI.conclusion: A GP mixture-based functional representation provides a compact, continuous alternative to traditional explicit representations, capable of modeling complex topology with sparse samples and flexible anchoring strategies.

title: Fatigue-Aware Learning to Defer via Constrained Optimisation
summary: Learning to defer (L2D) enables human-AI cooperation by deciding when an AI system should act autonomously or defer to a human expert. Existing L2D methods, however, assume static human performance, contradicting well-established findings on fatigue-induced degradation. We propose Fatigue-Aware Learning to Defer via Constrained Optimisation (FALCON), which explicitly models workload-varying human performance using psychologically grounded fatigue curves. FALCON formulates L2D as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP) whose state includes both task features and cumulative human workload, and optimises accuracy under human-AI cooperation budgets via PPO-Lagrangian training. We further introduce FA-L2D, a benchmark that systematically varies fatigue dynamics from near-static to rapidly degrading regimes. Experiments across multiple datasets show that FALCON consistently outperforms state-of-the-art L2D methods across coverage levels, generalises zero-shot to unseen experts with different fatigue patterns, and demonstrates the advantage of adaptive human-AI collaboration over AI-only or human-only decision-making when coverage lies strictly between 0 and 1.

AI.tldr: Fatigue-aware Learning-to-Defer (L2D) treats human-AI collaboration as a constrained decision process, modeling workload-driven human performance and optimizing accuracy under collaboration budgets; introduces a fatigue-aware benchmark and demonstrates robust, zero-shot generalization and superior adaptive collaboration.
AI.motivation: Existing L2D methods assume static human performance, which contradicts fatigue-induced degradation observed in real users. Accounting for workload-related fatigue is crucial to improve when AI should act or defer.
AI.method: Model fatigue via psychology-grounded fatigue curves; formulate L2D as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP) with state including task features and cumulative workload; optimize using PPO-Lagrangian training to satisfy collaboration budget constraints; introduce FA-L2D benchmark varying fatigue dynamics from static to rapidly degrading regimes.
AI.result: FALCON outperforms state-of-the-art L2D methods across datasets and coverage levels; demonstrates zero-shot generalization to unseen fatigue patterns; adaptive human-AI collaboration yields advantages when coverage is between 0 and 1, compared to AI-only or human-only baselines.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating fatigue dynamics into L2D yields more reliable, efficient human-AI collaboration and broad generalization; the CMDP framework with PPO-Lagrangian is a viable approach for workload-aware decision-making in real-world settings.

title: A 4D Representation for Training-Free Agentic Reasoning from Monocular Laparoscopic Video
summary: Spatiotemporal reasoning is a fundamental capability for artificial intelligence (AI) in soft tissue surgery, paving the way for intelligent assistive systems and autonomous robotics. While 2D vision-language models show increasing promise at understanding surgical video, the spatial complexity of surgical scenes suggests that reasoning systems may benefit from explicit 4D representations. Here, we propose a framework for equipping surgical agents with spatiotemporal tools based on an explicit 4D representation, enabling AI systems to ground their natural language reasoning in both time and 3D space. Leveraging models for point tracking, depth, and segmentation, we develop a coherent 4D model with spatiotemporally consistent tool and tissue semantics. A Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) then acts as an agent on tools derived from the explicit 4D representation (e.g., trajectories) without any fine-tuning. We evaluate our method on a new dataset of 134 clinically relevant questions and find that the combination of a general purpose reasoning backbone and our 4D representation significantly improves spatiotemporal understanding and allows for 4D grounding. We demonstrate that spatiotemporal intelligence can be "assembled" from 2D MLLMs and 3D computer vision models without additional training. Code, data, and examples are available at https://tum-ai.github.io/surg4d/

AI.tldr: Proposes a 4D explicit representation (time + 3D space) for surgical AI to ground language reasoning in spatiotemporal context. Uses 2D tracking/depth/segmentation to build a 4D model; an MLLM operates on derived tools without fine-tuning; evaluated on 134 clinically relevant questions; shows improved spatiotemporal grounding; demonstrates assembly from 2D MLLMs and 3D CV models without extra training; code available.
AI.motivation: Surgical scenes are spatially complex and require temporal grounding; explicit 4D representations can enable robust spatiotemporal reasoning and grounding for AI assistants in surgery.
AI.method: Create a 4D model by fusing point tracking, depth, and segmentation into spatiotemporally consistent tool/tissue semantics; derive trajectories/tools from this representation; deploy a general-purpose Multimodal LLM as an agent on these tools without fine-tuning; evaluate on a new dataset of 134 clinically relevant questions; provide code/data.
AI.result: The combination of a general reasoning backbone with the explicit 4D representation yields significant improvements in spatiotemporal understanding and enables 4D grounding; demonstrates that 4D intelligence can be assembled from 2D MLLMs and 3D CV models without additional training.
AI.conclusion: Explicit 4D representations can enhance AI spatiotemporal reasoning in surgery and can be composed from existing 2D/3D models with minimal or no additional training; code and data are publicly available to facilitate reproducibility.

title: Event Embedding of Protein Networks : Compositional Learning of Biological Function
comment: Machine Learning for Genomics Explorations (MLGenX) ICLR 2026 Workshop
summary: In this work, we study whether enforcing strict compositional structure in sequence embeddings yields meaningful geometric organization when applied to protein-protein interaction networks. Using Event2Vec, an additive sequence embedding model, we train 64-dimensional representations on random walks from the human STRING interactome, and compare against a DeepWalk baseline based on Word2Vec, trained on the same walks. We find that compositional structure substantially improves pathway coherence (30.2$\times$ vs 2.9$\times$ above random), functional analogy accuracy (mean similarity 0.966 vs 0.650), and hierarchical pathway organization, while geometric properties such as norm--degree anticorrelation are shared with or exceeded by the non-compositional baseline. These results indicate that enforced compositionality specifically benefits relational and compositional reasoning tasks in biological networks.

AI.tldr: Enforcing compositional structure in sequence embeddings (Event2Vec) yields stronger relational organization in protein-protein interaction networks than a non-compositional baseline.
AI.motivation: To determine whether strict additive compositional structure in sequence embeddings yields meaningful geometric and relational organization in protein-protein interaction networks.
AI.method: Train 64-dimensional Event2Vec embeddings on random walks from the human STRING interactome and compare to a DeepWalk baseline (Word2Vec) trained on the same walks.
AI.result: Compositional structure substantially improves pathway coherence (30.2× above random vs 2.9×), functional analogy accuracy (mean similarity 0.966 vs 0.650), and hierarchical pathway organization; geometric properties like norm–degree anticorrelation are similar to or exceeded by the non-compositional baseline.
AI.conclusion: Enforced compositionality benefits relational and compositional reasoning tasks in biological networks, though not all geometric properties are improved compared with non-compositional methods.

title: PixelPrune: Pixel-Level Adaptive Visual Token Reduction via Predictive Coding
summary: Document understanding and GUI interaction are among the highest-value applications of Vision-Language Models (VLMs), yet they impose exceptionally heavy computational burden: fine-grained text and small UI elements demand high-resolution inputs that produce tens of thousands of visual tokens. We observe that this cost is largely wasteful -- across document and GUI benchmarks, only 22--71\% of image patches are pixel-unique, the rest being exact duplicates of another patch in the same image. We propose \textbf{PixelPrune}, which exploits this pixel-level redundancy through predictive-coding-based compression, pruning redundant patches \emph{before} the Vision Transformer (ViT) encoder. Because it operates in pixel space prior to any neural computation, PixelPrune accelerates both the ViT encoder and the downstream LLM, covering the full inference pipeline. The method is training-free, requires no learnable parameters, and supports pixel-lossless compression ($τ{=}0$) as well as controlled lossy compression ($τ{>}0$). Experiments across three model scales and document and GUI benchmarks show that PixelPrune maintains competitive task accuracy while delivering up to 4.2$\times$ inference speedup and 1.9$\times$ training acceleration. Code is available at https://github.com/OPPO-Mente-Lab/PixelPrune.

AI.tldr: PixelPrune compresses pixel-level redundancy with predictive-coding-based pruning to remove duplicate patches before the ViT encoder, enabling fast, training-free acceleration of document/GUIs VLMs with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models for document understanding and GUI interaction incur heavy computation due to high-resolution inputs; a large fraction of patches are duplicates. Exploiting this redundancy can reduce computation substantially.
AI.method: PixelPrune uses predictive-coding-based compression directly in pixel space to prune redundant patches before the Vision Transformer encoder. It is training-free with no learnable parameters and supports both lossless (tau = 0) and controlled lossy (tau > 0) compression.
AI.result: Across three model scales and document/GUI benchmarks, PixelPrune maintains competitive task accuracy while delivering up to 4.2× inference speedup and 1.9× training acceleration. Code is available at the provided GitHub repo.
AI.conclusion: PixelPrune offers a practical, parameter-free approach to prune pixel-level redundancies prior to ViT processing, accelerating the full inference pipeline for document and GUI tasks with tunable accuracy-speed trade-offs via tau.

title: Orthogonal Learner for Estimating Heterogeneous Long-Term Treatment Effects
summary: Estimation of heterogeneous long-term treatment effects (HLTEs) is widely used for personalized decision-making in marketing, economics, and medicine, where short-term randomized experiments are often combined with long-term observational data. However, HLTE estimation is challenging due to limited overlap in treatment or in observing long-term outcomes for certain subpopulations, which can lead to unstable HLTE estimates with large finite-sample variance. To address this challenge, we introduce the LT-O-learners (Long-Term Orthogonal Learners), a set of novel orthogonal learners for HLTE estimation. The learners are designed for the canonical HLTE setting that combines a short-term randomized dataset $\mathcal{D}_1$ with a long-term historical dataset $\mathcal{D}_2$. The key idea of our LT-O-Learners is to retarget the learning objective by introducing custom overlap weights that downweight samples with low overlap in treatment or in long-term observation. We show that the retargeted loss is equivalent to the weighted oracle loss and satisfies Neyman-orthogonality, which means our learners are robust to errors in the nuisance estimation. We further provide a general error bound for the LT-O-Learners and give the conditions under which quasi-oracle rate can be achieved. Finally, our LT-O-learners are model-agnostic and can thus be instantiated with arbitrary machine learning models. We conduct empirical evaluations on synthetic and semi-synthetic benchmarks to confirm the theoretical properties of our LT-O-Learners, especially the robustness in low-overlap settings. To the best of our knowledge, ours are the first orthogonal learners for HLTE estimation that are robust to low overlap that is common in long-term outcomes.

AI.tldr: LT-O-learners are orthogonal meta-learners for heterogeneous long-term treatment effects (HLTE) that fuse short-term randomized data with long-term observational data using overlap-based weights to downweight low-overlap samples, achieving Neyman-orthogonality and robustness to nuisance estimation in low-overlap settings.
AI.motivation: HLTE estimation suffers from instability and high variance when there is limited overlap in treatment or long-term outcomes across subpopulations; there is a need for robust, model-agnostic methods that can leverage both short-term randomized data and long-term observational data.
AI.method: Introduce LT-O-learners that retarget the learning objective with custom overlap weights, proving the retargeted loss equals a weighted oracle loss and satisfies Neyman-orthogonality. Provide general error bounds and conditions for quasi-oracle rates. Model-agnostic, applicable with arbitrary ML models; empirical evaluation on synthetic and semi-synthetic benchmarks.
AI.result: The LT-O-learners demonstrate theoretical robustness to nuisance estimation errors, derive error bounds, and achieve quasi-oracle rates under specified conditions. Empirical results corroborate robustness in low-overlap scenarios.
AI.conclusion: LT-O-learners are the first orthogonal learners for HLTE estimation that are robust to low overlap, offering a practical, model-agnostic approach for combining short-term and long-term data in heterogeneous long-term treatment effect estimation.

title: Adversarial Attenuation Patch Attack for SAR Object Detection
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures. Source code is available at https://github.com/boremycin/SAAP
summary: Deep neural networks have demonstrated excellent performance in SAR target detection tasks but remain susceptible to adversarial attacks. Existing SAR-specific attack methods can effectively deceive detectors; however, they often introduce noticeable perturbations and are largely confined to digital domain, neglecting physical implementation constrains for attacking SAR systems. In this paper, a novel Adversarial Attenuation Patch (AAP) method is proposed that employs energy-constrained optimization strategy coupled with an attenuation-based deployment framework to achieve a seamless balance between attack effectiveness and stealthiness. More importantly, AAP exhibits strong potential for physical realization by aligning with signal-level electronic jamming mechanisms. Experimental results show that AAP effectively degrades detection performance while preserving high imperceptibility, and shows favorable transferability across different models. This study provides a physical grounded perspective for adversarial attacks on SAR target detection systems and facilitates the design of more covert and practically deployable attack strategies. The source code is made available at https://github.com/boremycin/SAAP.

AI.tldr: A novel energy-constrained Adversarial Attenuation Patch (AAP) attacks SAR detectors with stealth and potential physical realizability, balancing attack effectiveness and imperceptibility with good transferability; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between digital SAR adversarial examples—often conspicuous or purely digital—and real-world, physically realizable attacks that exploit communication/jamming-like mechanisms to stealthily degrade SAR target detection.
AI.method: Introduce Adversarial Attenuation Patch (AAP) using energy-constrained optimization and attenuation-based deployment, designed to align with signal-level electronic jamming. The approach emphasizes physical feasibility and transferability across detectors.
AI.result: AAP significantly degrades SAR detector performance while maintaining high imperceptibility, with favorable cross-model transferability.
AI.conclusion: Provides a physically grounded perspective for adversarial attacks on SAR systems and promotes the design of covert, practically deployable attack strategies; code is released for reproducibility.

title: Generalization Bounds for Spectral GNNs via Fourier Domain Analysis
summary: Spectral graph neural networks learn graph filters, but their behavior with increasing depth and polynomial order is not well understood. We analyze these models in the graph Fourier domain, where each layer becomes an element-wise frequency update, separating the fixed spectrum from trainable parameters and making depth and order explicit. In this setting, we show that Gaussian complexity is invariant under the Graph Fourier Transform, which allows us to derive data-dependent, depth, and order-aware generalization bounds together with stability estimates. In the linear case, our bounds are tighter, and on real graphs, the data-dependent term correlates with the generalization gap across polynomial bases, highlighting practical choices that avoid frequency amplification across layers.

AI.tldr: Analyzes spectral GNNs in the graph Fourier domain, showing Gaussian complexity invariance under Graph Fourier Transform, and deriving depth- and polynomial-order-aware generalization and stability bounds; tighter bounds in the linear case; empirical correlation between data-dependent term and generalization gap across polynomial bases, offering practical guidance to avoid frequency amplification.
AI.motivation: To understand how depth and polynomial order affect generalization in spectral GNNs, and to provide principled bounds and practical guidance via a frequency-domain perspective that separates fixed spectrum from trainable filters.
AI.method: Express each layer as a frequency-wise update in the graph Fourier domain, treat the spectrum as fixed while learning coefficients; leverage invariance of Gaussian complexity under Graph Fourier Transform to derive data-dependent, depth-, and order-aware generalization bounds and stability estimates; obtain tighter bounds in the linear case; validate on real graphs by correlating the data-dependent term with generalization gaps across polynomial bases.
AI.result: Proven invariance of Gaussian complexity under Graph Fourier Transform; derived data-dependent, depth- and order-aware generalization bounds; provided stability estimates; observed tighter bounds in linear case; data-dependent term correlates with generalization gap across bases on real graphs, suggesting practical design choices to mitigate frequency amplification.
AI.conclusion: The spectral-domain view clarifies how depth and polynomial order influence generalization and stability in spectral GNNs, enabling principled design choices (e.g., basis selection to avoid frequency amplification) and setting the stage for further analysis of frequency-wise dynamics in nonlinear regimes.

title: Representation Selection via Cross-Model Agreement using Canonical Correlation Analysis
summary: Modern vision pipelines increasingly rely on pretrained image encoders whose representations are reused across tasks and models, yet these representations are often overcomplete and model-specific. We propose a simple, training-free method to improve the efficiency of image representations via a post-hoc canonical correlation analysis (CCA) operator. By leveraging the shared structure between representations produced by two pre-trained image encoders, our method finds linear projections that serve as a principled form of representation selection and dimensionality reduction, retaining shared semantic content while discarding redundant dimensions. Unlike standard dimensionality reduction techniques such as PCA, which operate on a single embedding space, our approach leverages cross-model agreement to guide representation distillation and refinement. The technique allows representations to be reduced by more than 75% in dimensionality with improved downstream performance, or enhanced at fixed dimensionality via post-hoc representation transfer from larger or fine-tuned models. Empirical results on ImageNet-1k, CIFAR-100, MNIST, and additional benchmarks show consistent improvements over both baseline and PCA-projected representations, with accuracy gains of up to 12.6%.

AI.tldr: A training-free cross-model-Cross-CCA post-hoc operator reduces dimensionality of image representations from two pretrained encoders by over 75% while improving downstream accuracy, outperforming PCA and enabling cross-model transfer across tasks.
AI.motivation: Pretrained image encoders produce overcomplete, model-specific features; there is a need for efficient, shared representations that preserve semantic content without additional training.
AI.method: Apply a post-hoc canonical correlation analysis (CCA) to two pretrained encoders. Compute linear projections that maximize cross-model agreement, yielding a principled dimensionality reduction and representation selection. The method is training-free and can transfer representations from larger or fine-tuned models across tasks and datasets.
AI.result: Dimensionality can be reduced by >75% with improved downstream performance. At fixed dimensionality, transfer from larger or fine-tuned models yields gains. Across ImageNet-1k, CIFAR-100, MNIST and other benchmarks, improvements over baselines and PCA-projected representations, with accuracy gains up to 12.6%.
AI.conclusion: Cross-model CCA provides a simple, effective training-free approach for distilling and refining image representations, enabling substantial dimensionality reduction and cross-model transfer with consistent performance gains.

title: IDDM: Identity-Decoupled Personalized Diffusion Models with a Tunable Privacy-Utility Trade-off
summary:
Personalized text-to-image diffusion models (e.g., DreamBooth, LoRA) enable users to synthesize high-fidelity avatars from a few reference photos for social expression. However, once these generations are shared on social media platforms (e.g., Instagram, Facebook), they can be linked to the real user via face recognition systems, enabling identity tracking and profiling. Existing defenses mainly follow an anti-personalization strategy that protects publicly released reference photos by disrupting model fine-tuning. While effective against unauthorized personalization, they do not address another practical setting in which personalization is authorized, but the resulting public outputs still leak identity information.
  To address this problem, we introduce a new defense setting, termed model-side output immunization, whose goal is to produce a personalized model that supports authorized personalization while reducing the identity linkability of public generations, with tunable control over the privacy-utility trade-off to accommodate diverse privacy needs. To this end, we propose Identity-Decoupled personalized Diffusion Models (IDDM), a model-side defense that integrates identity decoupling into the personalization pipeline. Concretely, IDDM follows an alternating procedure that interleaves short personalization updates with identity-decoupled data optimization, using a two-stage schedule to balance identity linkability suppression and generation utility. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, diverse prompts, and state-of-the-art face recognition systems show that IDDM consistently reduces identity linkability while preserving high-quality personalized generation.

AI.tldr: A model-side defense (IDDM) for personalized diffusion models that reduces identity linkability of public outputs while preserving personalization quality, via alternating short personalization updates with identity-decoupled data optimization in a two-stage schedule.
AI.motivation: Publicly generated personalized avatars can be linked to real users through face recognition, even when personalization is authorized. Existing defenses focus on protecting reference photos (anti-personalization) but do not mitigate leakage in outputs. A defense that decouples identity at the model level is needed to balance privacy and utility.
AI.method: Identity-Decoupled personalized Diffusion Models (IDDM) interleave short personalization updates with identity-decoupled data optimization in an alternating, two-stage schedule to suppress identity linkability while maintaining generation quality.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple datasets, prompts, and state-of-the-art face recognition systems show IDDM reduces identity linkability while preserving high-quality personalized generation.
AI.conclusion: IDDM provides a tunable privacy-utility trade-off for authorized personalization, achieving model-side protection against identity leakage in public outputs without sacrificing personalization performance.

title: WARP: Guaranteed Inner-Layer Repair of NLP Transformers
summary: Transformer-based NLP models remain vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, yet existing repair methods face a fundamental trade-off: gradient-based approaches offer flexibility but lack verifiability and often overfit; methods that do provide repair guarantees are restricted to the final layer or small networks, significantly limiting the parameter search space available for repair. We present WARP (Weight-Adjusted Repair with Provability), a constraint-based repair framework that extends repair beyond the last layer of Transformer models. WARP formulates repair as a convex quadratic program derived from a first-order linearization of the logit gap, enabling tractable optimization over a high-dimensional parameter space. Under the condition that the first-order approximation holds, this formulation induces three per-sample guarantees: (i) a positive margin constraint ensuring correct classification on repaired inputs, (ii) preservation constraints over a designated remain set, and (iii) a certified robustness radius derived from Lipschitz continuity. To ensure feasibility across varying model architectures, we introduce a sensitivity-based preprocessing step that conditions the optimization landscape accordingly. We further show that the iterative optimization procedure converges to solutions satisfying all repair constraints under mild assumptions. Empirical evaluation on encoder-only Transformers with varying layer architectures validates that these guarantees hold in practice while improving robustness to adversarial inputs. Our results demonstrate that guaranteed, generalizable Transformer repair is achievable through principled constraint-based optimization.

AI.tldr: Constraint-based, convex repair (WARP) for Transformers that extends repair beyond the final layer, delivering per-sample guarantees under a first-order logit-gap linearization, with preprocessing for feasibility and convergence guarantees, validated on encoder-only models.
AI.motivation: Vulnerability of Transformer NLP models to adversarial perturbations; existing gradient-based repair is flexible but unverifiable and prone to overfitting, while provable methods are restricted to the last layer or small networks, limiting repair search space.
AI.method: Formulates repair as a convex quadratic program derived from a first-order linearization of the logit gap; introduces per-sample guarantees: (i) positive margin on repaired inputs, (ii) preservation on a remain set, and (iii) certified robustness radius via Lipschitz continuity; adds a sensitivity-based preprocessing step; uses iterative optimization with convergence guarantees.
AI.result: Empirical validation on encoder-only Transformers with varying architectures shows the guarantees hold in practice and improve robustness to adversarial inputs; demonstrates feasibility of guaranteed, generalizable Transformer repair across larger parameter spaces.
AI.conclusion: Guarantied, generalizable Transformer repair is achievable through principled constraint-based optimization that extends beyond the last layer.

title: JAMMEval: A Refined Collection of Japanese Benchmarks for Reliable VLM Evaluation
summary: Reliable evaluation is essential for the development of vision-language models (VLMs). However, Japanese VQA benchmarks have undergone far less iterative refinement than their English counterparts. As a result, many existing benchmarks contain issues such as ambiguous questions, incorrect answers, and instances that can be solved without visual grounding, undermining evaluation reliability and leading to misleading conclusions in model comparisons. To address these limitations, we introduce JAMMEval, a refined collection of Japanese benchmarks for reliable VLM evaluation. It is constructed by systematically refining seven existing Japanese benchmark datasets through two rounds of human annotation, improving both data quality and evaluation reliability. In our experiments, we evaluate open-weight and proprietary VLMs on JAMMEval and analyze the capabilities of recent models on Japanese VQA. We further demonstrate the effectiveness of our refinement by showing that the resulting benchmarks yield evaluation scores that better reflect model capability, exhibit lower run-to-run variance, and improve the ability to distinguish between models of different capability levels. We release our dataset and code to advance reliable evaluation of VLMs.
